name: Sprint Planning
objective: Determine what can be delivered in the Sprint and how that work will be achieved.
description: This event initiates the sprint. The entire Scrum Team collaborates to determine what can be delivered and, crucially, how that work will be achieved. It involves crafting a Sprint Goal that provides coherence to the selected work items and breaking down High-Level items into actionable tasks.

activities.name: Establish Capacity
activities.description: Review team availability and historical velocity to determine capacity.

activities.name: Draft Sprint Goal
activities.description: Product Owner proposes a goal; the team refines it to ensure it provides focus.

activities.name: Select Items
activities.description: Developers pull items from the Product Backlog that align with the Goal.

activities.name: Decompose Work
activities.description: Break down selected items into technical tasks.

work_products.name: Sprint Goal
work_products.description: A short, coherent objective for the Sprint.

work_products.name: Sprint Backlog
work_products.description: Selected items and the plan to deliver them.

completion_criteria: Sprint Goal is defined and Sprint Backlog is populated within team capacity.

metrics.name: Commitment Reliability
metrics.formula: Completed Points / Committed Points * 100

pitfalls: Overallocating work beyond team capacity.
Focusing on filling the bucket rather than the Goal.
benefits: Reduces uncertainty by detailed task planning.

name: Daily Scrum
objective: Inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt the Sprint Backlog.
description: A 15-minute time-boxed event for the Developers. The purpose is to synchronize activities and plan for the next 24 hours. It is not a status report to management, but a coordination meeting to identify impediments and ensure the Sprint Goal is on track.

activities.name: Review Board
activities.description: Walk the board from right (done) to left (to do).

activities.name: Identify Blockers
activities.description: Raise any impediments preventing progress.

activities.name: Adapt Plan
activities.description: Adjust the daily plan based on progress.

work_products.name: Updated Sprint Backlog
work_products.description: Reflects current progress.

completion_criteria: Team has a plan for the next 24 hours; impediments are logged.

metrics.name: Meeting Duration
metrics.formula: End Time - Start Time

pitfalls: Turning it into a status report for the Scrum Master.
Problem-solving during the 15 minutes (should be taken offline).

name: User Stories
objective: Define requirements in small, client-valued functional increments.
description: Work is divided into functional increments called 'user stories', following the '3 Cs' model: Card (physical token), Conversation (verbal discussion), and Confirmation (acceptance criteria). They shift focus from writing documentation to talking about the requirement.

activities.name: Write Card
activities.description: Stakeholder writes a brief reminder of value (As a... I want... So that...).

activities.name: Refine
activities.description: Team clarifies details via conversation.

activities.name: Confirm
activities.description: Define acceptance criteria (Gherkin or bullet points).

work_products.name: Story Card
work_products.description: The physical or digital representation of the requirement.

completion_criteria: Story satisfies INVEST criteria (Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable).

pitfalls: Creating 'technical stories' meant only for developers.
Treating the card as a detailed specification document.
benefits: Mitigates risk of misunderstanding through conversation.

name: Planning Poker
objective: Collaboratively estimate work complexity using a relative scale to reach consensus.
description: A consensus-based technique where teams assign relative effort values (often using the Fibonacci sequence: 1, 2, 3, 5, 8...) to work items. Members reveal estimates simultaneously to avoid anchoring bias, followed by a discussion of outliers to build shared understanding.

activities.name: Present Item
activities.description: Product Owner reads the story and answers questions.

activities.name: Vote
activities.description: Team members select a card privately and reveal simultaneously.

activities.name: Discuss
activities.description: High and low estimators explain their reasoning.

activities.name: Re-Vote
activities.description: Repeat until convergence/consensus.

work_products.name: Estimated Backlog
work_products.description: Backlog items with assigned Story Points.

completion_criteria: Consensus reached on the relative size of the item.

pitfalls: Equating points directly to hours.
Debating small differences (e.g., 3 vs 5) for too long.
benefits: Builds team ownership of the plan.

name: Test-Driven Development (TDD)
objective: Ensure code is testable, covered by regression tests, and designed simply.
description: A software engineering practice where tests are written before the production code. It follows the 'Red-Green-Refactor' cycle: Write a failing test (Red), write just enough code to pass it (Green), and optimize the code (Refactor).

activities.name: Red
activities.description: Write a small unit test that fails.

activities.name: Green
activities.description: Write the minimum code necessary to make the test pass.

activities.name: Refactor
activities.description: Clean up the code structure while keeping tests passing.

work_products.name: Unit Tests
work_products.description: Automated suite of tests.

work_products.name: Clean Code
work_products.description: Implementated code.

completion_criteria: Code is committed only when all tests pass (Green bar).

metrics.name: Code Coverage
metrics.formula: Lines of Code Executed by Tests / Total Lines of Code * 100

pitfalls: Writing the test after the code.
Skipping the Refactor step (accumulating technical debt).

name: Acceptance Test-Driven Development (ATDD)
objective: Collaborate to define completion criteria and write acceptance tests before implementation.
description: This practice involves the 'Three Amigos' (Business, Development, QA) meeting before development begins to discuss requirements and derive acceptance tests. These tests often serve as the requirements themselves. It ensures that the team solves the right problem.

activities.name: Workshop
activities.description: The Three Amigos discuss the user story and explore edge cases.

activities.name: Formulate
activities.description: Write acceptance tests (e.g., in Gherkin syntax).

activities.name: Automate
activities.description: Connect the tests to the system (often failing initially).

activities.name: Implement
activities.description: Develop code until acceptance tests pass.

work_products.name: Acceptance Tests
work_products.description: Executable specifications.

work_products.name: Examples
work_products.description: Concrete scenarios clarifying the rule.

completion_criteria: Shared understanding reached and acceptance tests defined.

pitfalls: Testing only the 'happy path'.
QA writing tests in isolation after development.

name: Visualize the Workflow (Kanban Board)
objective: Make hidden work and bottlenecks immediately apparent.
description: Teams create a visual model of their work process, typically on a Kanban Board. Columns represent different process states (e.g., Analysis, Development, Testing). This visualization supports transparency and makes bottlenecks visible.

activities.name: Map Process
activities.description: Define the steps a work item takes from start to finish.

activities.name: Construct Board
activities.description: Create columns for each step.

activities.name: Populate
activities.description: Place current work items on the board.

work_products.name: Kanban Board
work_products.description: Physical or digital representation of work.

completion_criteria: Board accurately reflects the real-time state of work.

metrics.name: Throughput
metrics.formula: Number of Completed Items / Time Period

metrics.name: Work In Progress (WIP)
metrics.formula: Count of Items in 'In Progress' Columns

metrics.name: Lead Time
metrics.formula: Date Completed - Date Created

pitfalls: Board becoming a 'zombie' (not updated).
Visualizing the wishful process instead of the actual process.

name: Continuous Integration
objective: Detect integration errors immediately by merging code frequently.
description: Developers merge their code into a shared repository multiple times a day. Each merge triggers an automated build and test sequence. This practice minimizes 'integration hell' and ensures the software is always in a releasable state.

activities.name: Commit
activities.description: Developer pushes code to the main branch.

activities.name: Build
activities.description: CI Server compiles the code.

activities.name: Test
activities.description: CI Server runs automated unit and integration tests.

work_products.name: Build Artifact
work_products.description: Deployable package.

work_products.name: Build Report
work_products.description: Status of the integration (Pass/Fail).

completion_criteria: The build is green (passing) and deployed to an integration environment.

metrics.name: Build Repair Time
metrics.formula: Time Build Fixed - Time Build Broke

pitfalls: Committing to a feature branch that lives for days.
Broken builds being ignored.

name: Pair Programming
objective: Enhance code quality and knowledge sharing by having two developers work on one machine.
description: Two developers work at one workstation. One, the 'driver,' writes code while the other, the 'navigator,' reviews each line as it is typed, considering the strategic direction. They switch roles frequently to maintain focus and energy.

activities.name: Setup
activities.description: Two developers sit at one computer.

activities.name: Drive/Navigate
activities.description: Perform the coding task collaboratively.

activities.name: Rotate
activities.description: Switch roles every few minutes or when a test passes.

work_products.name: Production Code
work_products.description: High-quality, reviewed code.

completion_criteria: Code is committed by the pair.

pitfalls: Navigator checking their phone (disengagement).
Domination by the more senior developer.

name: Refactoring
objective: Improve internal structure without changing external behavior.
description: The continuous improvement of the code's design to keep it clean, understandable, and maintainable. It prevents 'bit rot' and technical debt accumulation. It is distinct from rewriting; it involves small, safe transformations.

activities.name: Identify Smell
activities.description: Locate complex, duplicate, or unclear code.

activities.name: Transform
activities.description: Apply a refactoring pattern (e.g., Extract Method).

activities.name: Verify
activities.description: Run tests to ensure behavior is unchanged.

work_products.name: Clean Code
work_products.description: Codebase free of duplication and complexity.

completion_criteria: Code passes all tests and is structurally better.

pitfalls: Refactoring without test coverage.
Refactoring for days without delivering value (Gold Plating).

name: Definition of Done (DoD)
objective: Create a shared understanding of quality standards and eliminate ambiguity.
description: A comprehensive checklist of requirements (e.g., tested, reviewed, documented) that must be fulfilled before a user story or product increment can be considered complete. It acts as a quality gate ensuring the increment is potentially shippable.

activities.name: Draft
activities.description: Team brainstorms all necessary quality steps.

activities.name: Agree
activities.description: Commit to the list as a team.

activities.name: Apply
activities.description: Verify every item against the DoD before marking 'Complete'.

work_products.name: DoD Checklist
work_products.description: Visible list of completion criteria.

completion_criteria: DoD is visible and applied to every item.

pitfalls: Making the DoD so stringent that nothing ever gets done.
Ignoring the DoD under pressure.

name: Weighted Shortest Job First (WSJF)
objective: Sequence work to maximize economic benefit.
description: A prioritization mechanism used to sequence jobs (features) to produce maximum economic benefit. It calculates a score by dividing the Cost of Delay (CoD) by the Job Duration (or size).

activities.name: Calculate CoD
activities.description: Sum of User Value + Time Criticality + Risk Reduction.

activities.name: Estimate Size
activities.description: Determine relative duration/size of the job.

activities.name: Prioritize
activities.description: Rank items by WSJF score (highest first).

work_products.name: Prioritized Backlog
work_products.description: List of features sorted by economic value.

completion_criteria: Backlog is ordered by WSJF score.

metrics.name: WSJF Score
metrics.formula: Cost of Delay / Job Duration

pitfalls: Spending too much time calculating precise numbers for CoD.
Gaming the size estimates to get a higher score.

name: Story Mapping
objective: Organize user stories to understand the whole product and user journey.
description: A visualization technique that organizes user activities (the backbone) horizontally and breaks them down into details/stories vertically. It helps teams understand the product narrative, identify gaps, and plan releases based on user flow rather than random features.

activities.name: Build Backbone
activities.description: Identify high-level user activities (left to right).

activities.name: Break Down
activities.description: Add user tasks/stories under each activity.

activities.name: Slice Releases
activities.description: Draw horizontal lines to define MVPs and subsequent releases.

work_products.name: Story Map
work_products.description: Visual representation of the user journey and backlog.

completion_criteria: Map reveals a cohesive user journey and release slices.

pitfalls: Creating a map and never updating it.
Too much detail in the initial mapping.
benefits: Builds shared understanding of the 'Big Picture'.

name: Lean Coffee
objective: Collectively build and prioritize discussion agendas for structured meetings.
description: A structured, agenda-less meeting format where participants build the agenda democratically. Topics are generated on sticky notes, voted on, and discussed in strict time-boxed intervals (e.g., 5 mins + 2 mins extension).

activities.name: Generate
activities.description: Everyone writes topics on sticky notes.

activities.name: Vote
activities.description: Dot voting to prioritize topics.

activities.name: Discuss
activities.description: Discuss top items in timeboxes; vote to extend or end.

work_products.name: Prioritized Agenda
work_products.description: List of discussed topics.

completion_criteria: Timebox expires or topics run out.

pitfalls: Using it for meetings that require a pre-set decision (e.g., architectural review).
Lack of facilitation leading to aimless chatting.
benefits: Ensures participants discuss what matters to them.

name: Mob Programming
objective: The entire (or part of ) team works on the same task at the same time.
description: An extension of Pair Programming where the whole team (3+ people) works on the same thing, at the same time, in the same space, and at the same computer. It optimizes for flow efficiency over resource efficiency.

activities.name: Assemble
activities.description: Team gathers at one screen.

activities.name: Rotate Driver
activities.description: Driver changes every 10-15 minutes.

activities.name: Navigate
activities.description: Rest of the team guides the driver.

work_products.name: Production Code
work_products.description: Code produced by the mob.

completion_criteria: Task is completed and committed.

pitfalls: Passive team members tuning out.
Exhaustion (requires frequent breaks).
benefits: High quality code with few defects.
No need for separate code reviews.

name: Value Stream Mapping
objective: Visualize the end-to-end flow of work to identify and remove waste.
description: A Lean management practice that maps the steps a product takes from request to delivery. It specifically measures 'value-added time' versus 'wait time' to calculate flow efficiency and identifies value-creating and non-value-creating actions to eliminate waste.

activities.name: Select Product Family
activities.description: Identify the product family to map.

activities.name: Create Current State Map
activities.description: Walk the flow and capture the actual condition of material and information flow (cycle times, inventory, etc.).

activities.name: Create Future State Map
activities.description: Design the target condition using lean guidelines (Takt time, continuous flow, supermarkets).

activities.name: Develop Implementation Plan
activities.description: Create a plan to move from current to future state.

work_products.name: Current State Map
work_products.description: Visual diagram of current flow and metrics.

work_products.name: Future State Map
work_products.description: Visual diagram of desired flow.

completion_criteria: Future state map and implementation plan are agreed upon.

metrics.name: Flow Efficiency
metrics.formula: Value Added Time / Total Lead Time * 100

metrics.name: Lead Time
metrics.formula: Total time from order to delivery

metrics.name: Value Added Time
metrics.formula: Sum of cycle times

pitfalls: Mapping the 'official' process instead of reality.
Analysis paralysis (spending too long mapping).
Mapping without walking the gemba
Focusing only on material flow and ignoring information flow
benefits: Focuses on the whole system, not just local optimization.

name: STATIK
objective: Design a Kanban system tailored to the service's specific context.
description: Systems Thinking Approach To Introducing Kanban. It is a repeatable, humane way to get started with Kanban by analyzing sources of dissatisfaction, demand, and capabilities before designing the board.

activities.name: Analyze Dissatisfaction
activities.description: Identify what makes the current process painful.

activities.name: Analyze Demand
activities.description: Understand what work comes in and how often.

activities.name: Analyze Capability
activities.description: Assess the team's ability to handle different work types.

activities.name: Model Workflow
activities.description: Define the activity states.

activities.name: Identify class of service
activities.description: Set WIP limits and class of service.

activities.name: Design System
activities.description: Create the board, limits, and classes of service.

work_products.name: Kanban System Design
work_products.description: The configuration of the board and policies.

completion_criteria: A Kanban system is ready to launch.

pitfalls: Designing the system in isolation without the team.
Copying another team's board without analysis.
benefits: Customizes Kanban to the specific team context.

name: Overall Retrospective
objective: Improve the organizational system in a multi-team context.
description: In large-scale environments (like LeSS), individual team retrospectives are insufficient. This practice brings representatives from multiple teams together to discuss systemic impediments that affect everyone.

activities.name: Gather
activities.description: Product Owner, Scrum Masters, and Team Reps meet.

activities.name: Discuss System
activities.description: Identify issues crossing team boundaries.

activities.name: Plan Action
activities.description: Decide on organizational experiments.

work_products.name: Improvement Plan
work_products.description: Actions for the next sprint.

completion_criteria: Systemic impediment identified and action planned.

pitfalls: Turning it into a complaint session without action.
Excluding management who have the power to change the system.
benefits: Solves problems that individual teams cannot fix.

name: Sprint Retrospective
objective: Inspect the past Sprint and plan for improvements
description: The Sprint Retrospective is held at the end of the Sprint to plan ways to increase quality and effectiveness. The Scrum Team inspects how the last Sprint went with regards to individuals, interactions, processes, tools, and their Definition of Done. Elements that went well and problems encountered are discussed, and the most helpful changes to improve the team's effectiveness are identified and planned for the next Sprint.

activities.name: Set the Stage
activities.description: Gather the Scrum Team and establish a safe environment for discussion.

activities.name: Review Data
activities.description: Inspect the Sprint events, velocity, and other relevant data.

activities.name: Generate Insights
activities.description: Discuss what went well, what could have been better, and identify root causes.

activities.name: Decide on Actions
activities.description: Select specific improvement actions to implement in the next Sprint.

activities.name: Close the Retrospective
activities.description: Formalize the plan and conclude the meeting.

work_products.name: Improvement Plan
work_products.description: A list of actionable improvements, often added to the Sprint Backlog for the next Sprint.

completion_criteria: Concrete improvement actions are identified and assigned.

metrics.name: Improvement Action Completion Rate
metrics.formula: (Completed Actions / Planned Actions) * 100

guidelines.name: Scrum and XP from the Trenches

pitfalls: Focusing on who is at fault rather than process improvement.
Discussing problems without committing to concrete solutions.
Skipping the Event
Treating the retrospective as optional when under pressure.
benefits: Regularly enhances team effectiveness and product quality.
Provides a space to resolve interpersonal and process issues.

name: Sprint Review
objective: Inspect the outcome of the Sprint and determine future adaptations
description: The Sprint Review is held at the end of the Sprint to inspect the Increment and adapt the Product Backlog. The Scrum Team presents the results of their work to key stakeholders and progress toward the Product Goal is discussed. It is a working session where the Scrum Team and stakeholders collaborate on what to do next based on the inspection of the increment and changes in the environment.

activities.name: Present Done Items
activities.description: The Product Owner explains what Product Backlog items have been 'Done' and what has not.

activities.name: Discuss Development
activities.description: Developers discuss what went well during the Sprint, what problems they ran into, and how those problems were solved.

activities.name: Demonstrate Increment
activities.description: Developers demonstrate the work that was 'Done' and answer questions about the Increment.

activities.name: Review Product Backlog
activities.description: The Product Owner discusses the Product Backlog as it stands and projects likely target and delivery dates.

activities.name: Collaborate on Next Steps
activities.description: The entire group collaborates on what to do next, providing valuable input for the next Sprint Planning.

work_products.name: Updated Product Backlog
work_products.description: Adjustments to the backlog based on feedback and new insights.

work_products.name: Product Increment
work_products.description: The inspected and accepted working software.

completion_criteria: Feedback from stakeholders is gathered and the Product Backlog is updated.

metrics.name: Stakeholder Feedback Count
metrics.formula: Sum(Actionable Feedback Items)

metrics.name: Accepted Story Points
metrics.formula: Sum(Points of Done Items)

guidelines.name: Scrum and XP from the Trenches

pitfalls: Presenting slides instead of working software.
Holding the review without the people who provide feedback.
Treating the event as a sign-off meeting rather than a collaborative working session.
benefits: Direct input from users and stakeholders on the product direction.
Ensures the Product Backlog remains relevant to current business needs.
Fosters collaboration between the Scrum Team and stakeholders.

name: Backlog Refinement
objective: Break down and further define Product Backlog items into smaller more precise items
description: Product Backlog refinement is the act of breaking down and further defining Product Backlog items into smaller more precise items. This is an ongoing activity to add details, such as a description, order, and size. Attributes often vary with the domain of work. The Developers who will be doing the work are responsible for the sizing. The Product Owner may influence the Developers by helping them understand and select trade-offs.

activities.name: Review and Clarify
activities.description: The Product Owner and Developers review high-priority backlog items to ensure a shared understanding of the scope and intent.

activities.name: Decomposition
activities.description: Large items are broken down into smaller, more manageable items that can be completed within a single Sprint.

activities.name: Estimation
activities.description: Developers provide an estimate of the effort or size required for the items, often using techniques like Planning Poker.

activities.name: Ordering
activities.description: The Product Owner re-orders the Product Backlog based on new insights, value, and estimates.

work_products.name: Product Backlog
work_products.description: An emergent, ordered list of what is needed to improve the product.

completion_criteria: Backlog items are deemed 'Ready' for selection in a Sprint Planning event (e.g., they are clear, feasible, and testable).

metrics.name: Ready Backlog Depth
metrics.formula: Sum(Size of Ready Items) / Average Velocity

guidelines.name: Scrum and XP from the Trenches

pitfalls: Wasting effort on items that may change or be discarded before they are needed.
The Product Owner refining in isolation, leading to lack of technical feasibility or buy-in.
benefits: Reduces the time required for Sprint Planning by ensuring items are already understood.
Identifies dependencies and unknowns early, before the Sprint begins.

name: Swarming
objective: Maximize flow and reduce cycle time by focusing collective team effort on finishing one item before starting another
description: Swarming is a collaborative technique where multiple team members with different skills focus their efforts on a single work item (such as a user story or support ticket) simultaneously to complete it as quickly as possible. Rather than working in silos or handing off tasks sequentially (e.g., from developer to tester), the team works in parallel or together to solve the problem. This approach minimizes 'Work in Progress' (WIP), improves flow, ensures shared understanding, and reduces the time it takes to deliver value.

activities.name: Identify Priority
activities.description: Select the critical user story or issue that requires immediate attention, often one that is blocked or closest to completion.

activities.name: Form Swarm
activities.description: Identify all team members capable of contributing to the item (e.g., Developer, Tester, Designer).

activities.name: Agree on Approach
activities.description: The group decides how to attack the work simultaneously (e.g., breaking down tasks to work in parallel or pairing/mobbing on a single screen).

activities.name: Execute and Integrate
activities.description: Team members work collaboratively in real-time to complete tasks, review code, and test immediately.

work_products.name: User Story
work_products.description: The functional increment being worked on by the swarm.

work_products.name: Support Ticket
work_products.description: The issue or incident being resolved (in IT support contexts).

work_products.name: Test Cases
work_products.description: Tests created and executed during the swarming process.

work_products.name: Production Code
work_products.description: Code developed, reviewed, and integrated during the swarming session.

completion_criteria: The User Story is 100% Done or the Issue is fully resolved with no remaining tasks.

metrics.name: Cycle Time
metrics.formula: Time Finished - Time Started

metrics.name: Customer Satisfaction Score
metrics.formula: Average of Customer Ratings

guidelines.name: Swarming: A Team-Based Approach to Getting Work Done

pitfalls: High Work In Progress (WIP) if swarming is neglected
Team members struggling with autonomy initially
Focusing on individual efficiency metrics instead of flow
Lack of clear communication channels leading to confusion
benefits: Broader team understanding of the system
Real-time code review and quality assurance
Increased employee empowerment and skill development

name: Example Mapping
objective: Clarify and confirm acceptance criteria through a structured conversation using concrete examples.
description: Example Mapping is a simple, low-tech method for keeping story discovery conversations short and productive. It involves a group (typically the 'Three Amigos') using a pack of 4-colored index cards to capture different types of information (Story, Rules, Examples, Questions) as the conversation unfolds. This visual representation helps tease apart rules, identify core behaviors, and defer complexity, ensuring a shared understanding of what it takes for a story to be 'done' before development begins.

activities.name: Setup
activities.description: Write the User Story under discussion on a yellow card and place it at the top of the table.

activities.name: Map Rules
activities.description: Write known acceptance criteria or rules on blue cards and place them beneath the yellow story card.

activities.name: Create Examples
activities.description: For each rule, write one or more concrete examples (e.g., 'The one where...') on green cards to illustrate it and place them under the relevant rule.

activities.name: Capture Questions
activities.description: If questions arise that cannot be answered immediately, capture them on red cards to move the conversation forward.

activities.name: Review and Slice
activities.description: Assess the visual map. If there are too many blue cards (rules) or red cards (questions), consider slicing the story or deferring it for more research.

activities.name: Vote
activities.description: Conduct a quick thumb-vote after the timebox (e.g., 25 minutes) to decide if the story is ready for development.

work_products.name: Example Map
work_products.description: A visual arrangement of colored cards (Story, Rules, Examples, Questions) representing the shared understanding.

work_products.name: Refined User Story
work_products.description: A story that has been clarified, potentially sliced, or confirmed as ready for development.

work_products.name: List of Questions
work_products.description: Captured unknowns (red cards) requiring further research (known unknowns).

completion_criteria: The group is satisfied that the scope of the story is clear, or the 25-minute timebox has expired.

metrics.name: Session Duration
metrics.formula: End Time - Start Time (Target: ~25 min)

metrics.name: Story Readiness
metrics.formula: Thumb-vote result (Ready/Not Ready)

pitfalls: Writing formal Gherkin scenarios during the session (can be distracting)
Letting discussions ramble without capturing questions
Attempting to map stories that are too big or uncertain without slicing
Discussing opinions instead of capturing questions (red cards)
benefits: Acts as a filter to prevent large or unclear stories from entering sprints
Saves time and maintains interest of busy product people

name: Impact Mapping
objective: To create a visual hierarchy connecting business goals to specific features, ensuring value delivery.
description: Impact Mapping is a planning technique that creates a visual hierarchy from Goal to Actors (who can help/hinder), to Impacts (behavior changes), to Deliverables (features). It ensures every feature serves a business goal.

activities.name: Identify Goal
activities.description: Determine the central business goal to be achieved.

activities.name: Identify Actors
activities.description: Identify the people or groups who can help or hinder the achievement of the goal.

activities.name: Determine Impacts
activities.description: Define the behavior changes (impacts) required from the actors.

activities.name: Define Deliverables
activities.description: List the features or deliverables that will drive the desired impacts.

work_products.name: Impact Map
work_products.description: A visual hierarchy from Goal to Actors to Impacts to Deliverables.

completion_criteria: Map connects goals to deliverables.

guidelines.name: How Story Mapping and Impact Mapping Drive Alignment - Beyond the Backlog
Impact Mapping: Ultimate Guide for Product Teams - NEXT

pitfalls: Focusing on deliverables instead of impacts.
Creating a static map that is not updated.
benefits: Ensures every feature serves a business goa.

name: Opportunity Solution Trees
objective: Visualize discovery to stay aligned and drive outcomes
description: Opportunity solution trees are a visual representation of the paths a team might take to reach a desired outcome. The tree starts with a desired outcome at the root, branches into the opportunity space (customer needs, pain points, desires), then into the solution space, and finally into assumption tests to evaluate those solutions. This structure helps teams resolve the tension between business and customer needs and build a shared understanding.

activities.name: Verify Prerequisites
activities.description: Ensure the team has a clear outcome, a theory of the target customer/value proposition, and has conducted at least 3 story-based customer interviews.

activities.name: Define Outcome
activities.description: Place the clearly defined desired outcome (typically a product outcome) at the top of the tree.

activities.name: Map Opportunity Space
activities.description: Synthesize findings from customer interviews (often using an experience map) to group needs and pain points under the outcome.

activities.name: Select Target Opportunity
activities.description: Choose one specific opportunity to focus on to limit work in progress and deliver value iteratively.

activities.name: Brainstorm Solutions
activities.description: Generate potential solutions specifically for the selected target opportunity, not the whole tree.

activities.name: Select Solutions to Explore
activities.description: Choose three solutions to move forward with for comparison and contrasting.

activities.name: Identify Assumptions
activities.description: Break the selected solutions down into their underlying assumptions (desirability, viability, feasibility, usability).

activities.name: Test Assumptions
activities.description: Run rapid tests on the riskiest assumptions across the three ideas to gather data.

activities.name: Evaluate and Decide
activities.description: Use test data to decide whether to throw ideas away, refine them, or pivot to a new opportunity.

work_products.name: Opportunity Solution Tree
work_products.description: A visual diagram connecting the desired outcome to opportunities, solutions, and assumption tests

work_products.name: Experience Map
work_products.description: A visual map of the customer's experience used to structure the opportunity space

work_products.name: Assumption Tests
work_products.description: Experiments designed to validate or invalidate the risks associated with potential solutions

completion_criteria: The team has evaluated solutions based on assumption test data and decided on the next step (iterate, pivot, or deliver).

metrics.name: Business Outcome
metrics.formula: Revenue, Market Share, or Churn Rate

metrics.name: Product Outcome
metrics.formula: Customer behavior change or sentiment score

metrics.name: Traction Metric
metrics.formula: Adoption of a single feature

guidelines.name: Opportunity Solution Trees: Visualize Your Discovery to Stay Aligned and Drive Outcomes

pitfalls: Skipping prerequisites and making up opportunities
Confusing opportunities with solutions
Focusing on too many outcomes at once
Creating a single tree for the entire company
Ruling out opportunities early based on estimated effort
benefits: Resolves tension between business and customer needs
Builds shared understanding of how to reach outcomes

name: Definition of Ready
objective: Ensure backlog items are actionable, clear, and feasible before work begins
description: The Definition of Ready (DoR) is a working agreement and a set of criteria that a Product Backlog Item must meet before it can be selected for a Sprint. It acts as a gateway or checklist to ensure that work is sufficiently defined, free of external dependencies, and properly estimated, preventing the team from committing to ambiguous or unfeasible work. This practice facilitates the transition of work from the Product Backlog to the development stage.

activities.name: Define Criteria
activities.description: Collaborate with the team to establish specific criteria (e.g., INVEST attributes, clear acceptance criteria) that make an item ready.

activities.name: Refine Backlog
activities.description: Evaluate and prepare backlog items against the DoR checklist during refinement sessions, ensuring fields like estimates and 'how to demo' are filled.

activities.name: Verify Readiness
activities.description: Confirm that high-priority items meet the agreed criteria before or during Sprint Planning to ensure smooth execution.

work_products.name: Definition of Ready Checklist
work_products.description: A shared list of criteria (e.g., dependencies resolved, acceptance criteria defined) used to gauge backlog items.

work_products.name: Ready Product Backlog Item
work_products.description: A backlog item that has passed the DoR criteria and is eligible for sprint planning.

completion_criteria: The team collectively agrees that the backlog item meets all defined DoR criteria.

metrics.name: Readiness Ratio
metrics.formula: (Items meeting DoR / Total Items selected for Sprint) * 100

guidelines.name: What is Definition of Ready | Atlassian
Scrum and XP from the Trenches

pitfalls: Gatekeeping behavior that impedes flow
Rigid adherence preventing necessary negotiability
Using it as a substitute for conversation
benefits: Improves communication and aligns team expectations
Reduces back-and-forth and errors during the sprint
Ensures backlog items are actionable and feasible

name: Timeline Roadmap
objective: Visualize strategic direction and major work phases
description: A strategic planning tool that visualizes project initiatives, deliverables, and milestones along a chronological timeline. Unlike detailed Gantt charts, it provides a high-level view to communicate direction and priorities to stakeholders. It uses flexible time periods (months, quarters) and swim lanes for workstreams.

activities.name: Prepare the timeline frame
activities.description: Set start date, time range, and configure dimensions

activities.name: Define key initiatives
activities.description: Identify major projects and dependencies

activities.name: Build the roadmap
activities.description: Add cards, position on timeline, and color-code

activities.name: Review and refine
activities.description: Check for resource conflicts and sequencing logic

activities.name: Establish update cadence
activities.description: Agree on review frequency and ownership

work_products.name: Roadmap
work_products.description: Visual timeline of initiatives and milestones

completion_criteria: Roadmap visualized and agreed upon.

pitfalls: Adding too much detail too early
Planning at 100% capacity without room for flexibility
benefits: Aligns cross-functional teams on delivery sequencing

name: T-Shirt Estimation
objective: Quickly categorize work based on relative effort
description: A relative sizing method where teams classify tasks using clothing sizes (XS, S, M, L, XL) instead of specific time units. It focuses on relative scope and complexity rather than precise time estimates, making it useful for early planning and backlog sizing.

activities.name: Prepare Your Backlog
activities.description: Import items and prioritize them

activities.name: Set Context
activities.description: Explain sizing categories and establish a baseline

activities.name: Estimate Each Item
activities.description: Vote on sizes and discuss differences

activities.name: Review & Adjust
activities.description: Check distribution and break down 'Too Big' items

work_products.name: Sized Backlog
work_products.description: List of work items with relative size estimates

completion_criteria: Backlog items have size estimates.

pitfalls: Overthinking exact boundaries between sizes
Allowing scope creep during discussions
benefits: Reduced debate over precise hours or days

name: Tigers, Paper Tigers, and Elephants
objective: Identify and mitigate project risks before they materialize
description: A pre-mortem exercise that distinguishes between real threats (Tigers), perceived threats that aren't genuine (Paper Tigers), and unspoken issues (Elephants). It helps teams anticipate challenges proactively rather than looking backward.

activities.name: Introduction
activities.description: Explain the three categories of risks

activities.name: Individual Reflection
activities.description: Team members add sticky notes to each area

activities.name: Sharing and Discussion
activities.description: Read inputs and vote on importance

activities.name: Grouping and Analysis
activities.description: Group themes and discuss patterns

activities.name: Action Planning
activities.description: Create mitigation plans for priority risks

work_products.name: Risk Mitigation Plan
work_products.description: Actionable steps to address identified risks

completion_criteria: Risks identified and mitigation plans created.

guidelines.name: Tigers, Paper Tigers, and Elephants Template

pitfalls: Vague concerns that are difficult to address
Jumping to solutions before understanding risks
benefits: Create psychological safety to discuss uncomfortable topics

name: Graveyard of Gloom
objective: Uncover potential fatal flaws by imagining project failure
description: A pre-mortem technique where the team imagines the project has already failed and works backward to determine causes. It uses a cemetery metaphor with quadrants for Original Goals, Original Plan, What Went Wrong, and The Result .

activities.name: Set the Scene
activities.description: Adopt a mindset that the project is dead

activities.name: Define the Foundation
activities.description: List original goals and plans

activities.name: Imagine Failure
activities.description: Add sticky notes for causes of failure and results

activities.name: Share and Group
activities.description: Identify recurring themes in failure scenarios

activities.name: Develop Mitigations
activities.description: Brainstorm preventative measures for high risks

work_products.name: Mitigation Strategy
work_products.description: Preventative actions for identified failure points

completion_criteria: Risks identified and mitigation plans created.

pitfalls: Listing generic risks instead of specific failure scenarios
benefits: Reduced cognitive bias in project planning
More thorough risk identification than traditional planning

name: RAID Analysis
objective: Proactively identify and track potential roadblocks
description: A strategic planning framework that categorizes project variables into Risks (future problems), Assumptions (unverified beliefs), Issues (current problems), and Dependencies (external needs) . It provides a comprehensive view of factors impacting success.

activities.name: Introduction
activities.description: Explain RAID categories

activities.name: Individual Brainstorming
activities.description: Add sticky notes to each quadrant

activities.name: Group Discussion
activities.description: Review, de-duplicate, and discuss impact

activities.name: Prioritization & Action Planning
activities.description: Identify high-priority items and assign owners

activities.name: Establish Review Cadence
activities.description: Set schedule for updating the RAID log

work_products.name: RAID Log
work_products.description: Documented list of risks, assumptions, issues, and dependencies

completion_criteria: RAID Log created and owners assigned.

pitfalls: Vague descriptions leading to vague actions
Confusing risks (future) with issues (current)
benefits: Improved risk management through early identification
Enhanced team alignment on project challenges

name: MoSCoW Prioritization
objective: Categorize features based on importance and urgency
description: A requirements prioritization technique that categorizes items into Must Have, Should Have, Could Have, and Won't Have . It helps teams build consensus and make informed decisions about resource allocation.

activities.name: Preparation
activities.description: Import backlog items

activities.name: Explain the Framework
activities.description: Review meanings of Must, Should, Could, Won't

activities.name: Prioritization Process
activities.description: Move items to categories and reach consensus

activities.name: Review and Finalize
activities.description: Check for balance and adjust

activities.name: Export and Action
activities.description: Save prioritized backlog and assign owners

work_products.name: Prioritized Backlog
work_products.description: Backlog items sorted by MoSCoW categories

completion_criteria: Backlog items are categorized and prioritized.

pitfalls: Categorizing too many items as 'Must Have'

name: Lean Prioritization Matrix
objective: Make data-driven decisions by weighing value against effort
description: A 2x2 matrix framework that compares backlog items based on Business Value and Implementation Effort. Items are divided into Quick Wins, Major Strategic Items, Filler Tasks, and Time Sinks.

activities.name: Prepare backlog items
activities.description: Add items as sticky notes

activities.name: Define evaluation criteria
activities.description: Establish definitions for Value and Effort

activities.name: Plot items on the matrix
activities.description: Collaboratively place items on the 2x2 grid

activities.name: Review and refine
activities.description: Adjust positioning and look for patterns

activities.name: Create an action plan
activities.description: Schedule Quick Wins and break down Strategic Items

work_products.name: Prioritized Matrix
work_products.description: Visual classification of backlog items

completion_criteria: Backlog items plotted on matrix.

pitfalls: Making arbitrary value decisions without criteria

name: Empathy Map
objective: Deeply understand users' needs, motivations, and frustrations
description: A collaborative canvas that organizes customer research into what users See, Hear, Say, Do, Think, and Feel (Pains and Gains). It helps teams move beyond assumptions to evidence-based insights.

activities.name: Preparation
activities.description: Select user segment and brief participants

activities.name: Define the User
activities.description: Capture demographics and context

activities.name: Complete Sections
activities.description: Fill in Goals, Sees, Hears, Says, Does, Pains, and Gains

activities.name: Analysis & Insight Generation
activities.description: Identify patterns and contradictions

work_products.name: Empathy Map
work_products.description: Visual representation of user experience and mindset

completion_criteria: Empathy Map completed.

pitfalls: Using assumptions instead of real data ]
Using generic statements like 'ease of use' ]
benefits: Prioritizes features based on actual user needs ]

name: Value Proposition Canvas
objective: Align product offerings with customer needs
description: A framework that maps the Customer Profile (Jobs, Pains, Gains) to the Value Map (Products, Pain Relievers, Gain Creators). It ensures product-market fit by validating that features address verified customer needs.

activities.name: Set the context
activities.description: Clarify customer segment

activities.name: Complete Customer Profile
activities.description: Document jobs, pains, and gains

activities.name: Build Value Map
activities.description: List products, pain relievers, and gain creators

activities.name: Analyze the Fit
activities.description: Identify gaps and prioritize pains/gains

activities.name: Define Next Steps
activities.description: Identify assumptions to validate

work_products.name: Value Proposition Map
work_products.description: Visual alignment of customer needs and product value

completion_criteria: Customer Profile and Value Map completed.

pitfalls: Starting with solution instead of customer profile ]
Using vague descriptions for pains and gains ]
benefits: Prevents feature development that doesn't address needs ]

name: User Journey Mapping
objective: Track and improve user interactions across multiple touchpoints
description: A visualization tool that maps the complete user experience sequence, capturing thoughts, feelings, pain points, and sentiment at each step. It helps identify friction points and opportunities for improvement.

activities.name: Prepare
activities.description: Define persona and gather research

activities.name: Map Journey Steps
activities.description: Identify 5-7 key touchpoints

activities.name: Document User Thinking
activities.description: Capture cognitive processes

activities.name: Map Emotional Responses
activities.description: Track sentiment and emotions

activities.name: Identify Pain Points
activities.description: Document challenges

activities.name: Brainstorm Improvements
activities.description: Develop solutions for pain points

work_products.name: User Journey Map
work_products.description: Visual timeline of user experience including sentiment

completion_criteria: User Journey Map completed.

pitfalls: Downplaying negative experiences
benefits: Aligns cross-functional teams around user perspective
Prioritizes improvements based on user impact

name: Product Vision Board
objective: Describe, visualize, and validate product vision and strategy
description: A strategic planning framework that aligns vision with market needs and business objectives. It covers Vision, Target Group, Needs, Product, and Business Goals, and extends to Competitors, Revenue, Costs, and Channels.

activities.name: Prepare team
activities.description: Gather stakeholders and research

activities.name: Articulate product vision
activities.description: Document motivation and positive change

activities.name: Define core product strategy
activities.description: Complete target group, needs, product, and goals

activities.name: Expand to business model
activities.description: Analyze competitors, revenue, costs, and channels

activities.name: Review and prioritize
activities.description: Check for consistency and identify assumptions

work_products.name: Product Vision Board
work_products.description: One-page strategic overview of product and business model

completion_criteria: Vision Board completed.

pitfalls: Overcomplicating instead of forcing clarity

name: Experiment Planner
objective: Test ideas and hypotheses methodically before full implementation
description: A collaborative framework for designing controlled tests for product ideas. It guides teams through defining the problem, hypothesis, methodology, audience, KPIs, and results.

activities.name: Define the problem
activities.description: Articulate specific problem to solve

activities.name: Formulate a hypothesis
activities.description: Create a testable statement

activities.name: Design the method
activities.description: Determine approach, timeframe, and resources

activities.name: Identify the audience
activities.description: Specify participants and recruitment

activities.name: Establish KPIs
activities.description: Define metrics for success

work_products.name: Experiment Design
work_products.description: Structured plan for validating a hypothesis

completion_criteria: Experiment Plan designed.

metrics.name: KPI
metrics.formula: Experiment Specific

pitfalls: Vague problem statements ]
Creating hypotheses that cannot be validated/invalidated ]
benefits: Reduced risk by testing ideas early ]

name: Team Canvas
objective: Align team on identity, purpose, and ways of working
description: A visual alignment tool serving as a 'social contract' for teams. It covers nine sections: People/Roles, Common Goals, Purpose, Personal Goals, Values, Needs, Strengths, Weaknesses, and Rules/Activities.

activities.name: People and Roles
activities.description: Introductions and responsibilities

activities.name: Strengths and Weaknesses
activities.description: Identify capabilities and challenges

activities.name: Purpose and Goals
activities.description: Define team existence and objectives

activities.name: Values and Needs
activities.description: Define core principles and individual needs

activities.name: Rules and Activities
activities.description: Establish working agreements

work_products.name: Team Canvas
work_products.description: Documented team agreements and identity

completion_criteria: Team Canvas completed and agreed upon.

pitfalls: Lack of follow through on agreements ]

name: RACI Matrix
objective: Clearly define roles and responsibilities for project tasks
description: A responsibility assignment framework that maps tasks to four involvement levels: Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, and Informed . It clarifies decision-making authority and prevents work duplication.

activities.name: Task Identification
activities.description: List all project tasks

activities.name: Assigning RACI Roles
activities.description: Determine R, A, C, I for each task

activities.name: Review and Refinement
activities.description: Check for overloaded members and accountability gaps

activities.name: Agreement
activities.description: Get explicit agreement from participants

work_products.name: RACI Matrix
work_products.description: Grid mapping tasks to roles

completion_criteria: RACI Matrix completed.

pitfalls: Overloading team members with too many 'Responsible' assignments ]
Tasks with too many 'Consulted' roles slowing decisions ]

name: Stakeholder Mapping
objective: Identify, categorize, and strategize engagement with stakeholders
description: A visual technique using a 2x2 influence/interest matrix to categorize stakeholders into Manage Closely, Keep Satisfied, Keep Informed, and Monitor. It helps develop tailored communication strategies.

activities.name: Brainstorm All Stakeholders
activities.description: Identify everyone affecting or affected by the project

activities.name: Assess Influence and Interest
activities.description: Map stakeholders to the matrix

activities.name: Develop Engagement Strategies
activities.description: Define approaches for each quadrant

activities.name: Action Planning
activities.description: Create next steps for high-priority stakeholders

work_products.name: Stakeholder Map
work_products.description: Matrix categorizing stakeholders by influence and interest

work_products.name: Communication Plan
work_products.description: Strategy for engaging each stakeholder group

completion_criteria: Stakeholder Map completed.

pitfalls: Dishonest assessment of influence ]
Vague action plans like 'keep in loop' ]
benefits: Prevent project derailment by identifying blockers ]

name: 5 Whys
objective: Discover the root cause of a problem
description: The practice of asking 'why' repeatedly (typically five times) whenever a problem is encountered in order to get beyond obvious symptoms to discover the underlying root cause.

activities.name: Define Problem
activities.description: Clearly state the specific problem encountered.

activities.name: Ask Why
activities.description: Ask why the problem happened to identify the direct cause.

activities.name: Repeat Why
activities.description: Ask why for the previous answer repeatedly until the root cause is found (often 5 times).

activities.name: Establish Countermeasures
activities.description: Implement actions to eliminate the root cause and prevent recurrence.

work_products.name: Root Cause Analysis
work_products.description: Record of the question chain and identified root cause.

completion_criteria: Root cause is identified and countermeasure prevents recurrence.

pitfalls: Stopping at symptoms
Blaming people instead of processes

name: Gemba Walk
objective: Grasp the current situation through direct observation
description: A management practice of going to the 'actual place' (gemba) where value is created to observe the process, interact with workers, and confirm data/facts firsthand, rather than relying on reports.

activities.name: Go to Gemba
activities.description: Physically go to the place where work is done.

activities.name: Observe
activities.description: Watch the actual process and conditions.

activities.name: Inquire
activities.description: Ask questions to workers to understand the situation (Purpose, Process, People).

activities.name: Confirm
activities.description: Verify facts and data through observation.

completion_criteria: Leader has a factual understanding of the current condition.

guidelines.name: Gemba Walk : La méthode en 4 étapes

pitfalls: Using it to police or blame workers
Trying to solve problems without full understanding

name: A3 Report
objective: Summarize problem-solving, status, or planning on a single sheet
description:
The A3 Report is not merely a document; it is a structured problem-solving and continuous improvement process named after the ISO A3 paper size (approximately 11x17 inches) used to contain it. The physical constraint of the page forces the author to be concise, filter out noise, and focus only on the most critical facts and data. 

The process follows the PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) logic. It requires the author to engage in 'Nemawashi' (consensus building) by discussing the A3 with stakeholders one-on-one before presenting it formally. The A3 serves as a storyboard that guides the dialogue between a mentor and a learner, shifting the management style from 'command and control' to 'leading through questioning.' There are three primary types of A3s: Problem Solving, Proposal, and Status Reporting.

activities.name: Identify Situation
activities.description: Define current situation and the nature of the issue (Background, Current Condition).

activities.name: Set Goals
activities.description: Define the target condition.

activities.name: Analyze
activities.description: Determine root causes (using 5 Whys, Fishbone).

activities.name: Propose Countermeasures
activities.description: Identify best countermeasures to address root causes.

activities.name: Plan Action
activities.description: Define who will do what, when, and how (Implementation Plan).

activities.name: Check Results
activities.description: Follow up and verify evidence that issue is addressed.

work_products.name: A3 Report
work_products.description: Single sheet document summarizing the problem solving process.

completion_criteria: A3 is completed and approved, and countermeasures are implemented.

pitfalls: Treating it as a form/template to fill out
Skipping analysis to jump to solutions

name: 1-2-4-All
objective: Tap into the wisdom of crowds to generate ideas and build consensus by engaging every member of a group simultaneously.
description: 1-2-4-All is a Liberating Structure designed to include and unleash everyone in a group, regardless of size. It systematically moves from individual reflection to pairs, then quartets, and finally the whole group, ensuring all voices are heard, incorporating “silent” conversations, and expanding input diversity.

activities.name: Pose the Question
activities.description: The facilitator asks a question about an issue, problem to resolve, or a proposal to put forward.

activities.name: Individual Self-reflection
activities.description: Individuals self-reflect on the shared challenge for 1 minute.

activities.name: Pair Generation
activities.description: Individuals pair up to generate ideas in pairs, building on ideas from self-reflection for 2 minutes.

activities.name: Foursome Development
activities.description: Pairs group into foursomes to share and develop ideas from each pair for 4 minutes.

activities.name: Collective Synthesis
activities.description: The facilitator pulls the entire group together to share and develop a common idea for 5 minutes.

completion_criteria: The facilitator pulls the entire group together to share and develop a common idea within the defined timebox.

metrics.name: Participant Engagement
metrics.formula: Number of participating individuals / total group size

metrics.name: Idea Generation Time
metrics.formula: Time elapsed from first question to final group synthesis

pitfalls: HiPPOs (Highest Paid Person’s Opinion) dominating the conversation
Failing to respect the strict timeboxes for each step
Unclear or overly complex initial prompt question

name: 10-for-10
objective: To help teams think quickly and objectively and be focused with their prioritization of ideas.
description: A lightweight and structured brainstorming workshop designed to avoid common pitfalls like messy discussions where the loudest voices or hierarchies dominate. It rephrases topics into actionable 'How Might We' challenges and uses time-boxed ideation and voting to reach consensus.

activities.name: Set the challenge
activities.description: The facilitator rephrases the topic into an actionable 'How Might We' challenge for the participants.

activities.name: Rapid Ideation
activities.description: Give participants 5 minutes to create as many ideas as possible on sticky notes.

activities.name: Select Top 10
activities.description: Participants spend 1 minute selecting their top 10 ideas and discarding the rest.

activities.name: Share Ideas
activities.description: Participants stick their top 10 ideas on a white board or wall.

activities.name: Review and Deduplicate
activities.description: Read out all sticky notes to the room and remove any duplicate ideas.

activities.name: Prepare for Voting
activities.description: Distribute 10 sticky dots (votes) to each participant.

activities.name: Dot Voting
activities.description: Participants have 3 minutes to place their 10 dots on the ideas they find most valuable.

activities.name: Order by Votes
activities.description: Re-order the sticky notes based on the number of dots received to identify the winning ideas.

completion_criteria: The brainstorming session is complete when all ideas are voted upon and re-ordered by the number of votes within the 10-minute timeframe.

metrics.name: Idea Count
metrics.formula: Number of unique ideas generated

guidelines.name: The Workshopper Playbook by Jonathan Courtney

pitfalls: Loudest person wins the discussion
Boss or hierarchy influence on selection
Big, messy discussions with no clear outcome
Lack of actionable phrasing

name: $100 Prioritisation
objective: To help groups prioritise anything through democratic relative budget allocation.
description: A democratic relative prioritisation exercise where people get $100 each to distribute across whatever needs prioritising, be it objectives, requirements, user stories, or a team to-do list. This method ensures everyone is heard and navigates the group towards consensus in an equitable way.

activities.name: Budget Distribution
activities.description: Provide each participant with $100 in physical or virtual currency (e.g., Monopoly money, post-its, or digital currency).

activities.name: Individual Allocation
activities.description: Participants allocate their dollars across the items to be prioritised based on how much they value each thing. They can put all dollars on one item or spread them out. Keeping allocations secret until the end is recommended to avoid tactical voting.

activities.name: Results Calculation
activities.description: Tally the results to create a prioritized list. Converting totals to percentages can help the group understand the final weightings more easily.

work_products: Budget/Currency (Monopoly money, custom notes, or post-its)
completion_criteria: The $100 budget for all participants has been allocated and the final prioritized results are shared with the group.

metrics.name: Priority Percentage
metrics.formula: (Total dollars for item / Total group budget) * 100

pitfalls: Tactical voting if allocations are not kept secret
Difficulty counting in very large groups
Over-spreading funds leading to lack of clear focus
benefits: Everyone in the group is heard

name: 15/5 Reports
objective: To keep a regular pulse across teams and organizations by using an efficient reporting structure that informs leadership and 1-1 meetings.
description: Originally created by Yvon Chouinard of Patagonia and inspired by Scrum, 15/5 reports are weekly status updates designed to be written in 15 minutes and read in 5 minutes. They provide a structured way to share successes, concerns, morale, and feedback without the overhead of traditional reporting, helping leaders stay informed while providing team members with a safe space for reflection.

activities.name: Establish Cadence and Timing
activities.description: Determine a regular weekly schedule for the reports, such as requesting them by Friday morning to allow for review before the weekend.

activities.name: Define Questions
activities.description: Set roughly three mandatory questions covering achievements, concerns, and morale, along with optional questions for deeper context or feedback.

activities.name: Ongoing Note-Taking
activities.description: Encourage team members to make small notes throughout the week to ensure the final report is easy to write and accurate.

activities.name: Draft the Report
activities.description: The reporter writes their responses, sticking to a 15-minute timebox to ensure brevity and focus.

activities.name: Review and Digest
activities.description: The leader or manager reads the reports, sticking to a 5-minute limit per report to understand progress and challenges.

activities.name: Acknowledge and Respond
activities.description: The recipient must respond to every report, even with a brief acknowledgment, to maintain engagement and value.

activities.name: Integrate with 1-1s
activities.description: Use the insights gained from the reports to focus the agenda of subsequent one-on-one meetings.

completion_criteria: The report is submitted weekly, reviewed by the lead within the timebox, and the reporter receives a response or acknowledgment.

metrics.name: Writing Duration
metrics.formula: Time Finished - Time Started

metrics.name: Reading Duration
metrics.formula: Time Finished - Time Started

metrics.name: Response Consistency
metrics.formula: Total Reports Responded / Total Reports Received

guidelines.name: 15/5 Reports - How Patagonia's Roving CEO Stays in the Loop
Tech Republic - How 5/15 reports improve communication

pitfalls: Failing to respond to reports, which demotivates the writer
Waiting until the deadline to write, leading to lost details
Treating it as a bureaucratic task rather than a communication tool
Exceeding the timebox limits for writing or reading
benefits: Efficient alternative to long status meetings
Provides a pulse on team culture and morale
Creates a historical record of challenges and achievements
Supports asynchronous communication for remote or busy teams

name: 5 Elements Canvas
objective: Building empathy and collaboration between silos to solve complex problems.
description: It is a canvas that facilitates deeper understanding of the complexity of an issue. It helps identify where pains are, where they come from, who owns the problem, and ultimately derives use cases to design experiments. It aims to identify areas of mutual interest and benefit between teams or silos that are trying to solve similar problems independently.

activities.name: Establish Shared Understanding
activities.description: Ensure the group has a shared understanding of the challenge, potentially using practices like 'target outcomes' to help define the problem statement.

activities.name: Brainstorm Stakeholder Pains
activities.description: Set a timer for 5 minutes and focus on one specific stakeholder group (e.g., Operations). Place stickies representing pain points observed, deduced, or assumed for that group regarding the challenge.

activities.name: Affinity Mapping
activities.description: Extend time as required and use affinity mapping to consolidate responses and group similar pain points.

activities.name: Map Causality
activities.description: Draw lines to show the causality or source of the pain; for example, showing how technical debt in Engineering might be caused by prioritization decisions in Product.

activities.name: Perspective Shift
activities.description: Move to the next stakeholder group, ideally following the source of the arrows. Identify their pains and see the problem from their perspective.

activities.name: Iterate
activities.description: Repeat the process until all stakeholder groups on the canvas are covered.

activities.name: Identify Ownership
activities.description: Identify who cares the most about the issue. While everyone plays a part, determine which group should own the issue, make a business case, or lead the resolution.

completion_criteria: The canvas is validated with all relevant stakeholder groups and used as a springboard to identify collaborative experiments or use cases.

pitfalls: Solving complex problems from a siloed perspective
The 'Field of Dreams' approach: building solutions without understanding customer needs
Over-engineered solutions that don't deliver required features
Attempting to solve speed-to-market issues by only changing one part of a waterfall process
benefits: Lowering silo walls and barriers to collaboration
Earning the right to have difficult conversations
Supporting stakeholders to raise issues to leadership or budget holders

name: 6 Dimensions of Discovery
objective: Gather, document, sort and prioritise challenges and bright spots across 6 dimensions to build shared understanding.
description: This is a facilitated information gathering activity designed to explore, document and prioritise current challenges and bright spots in an organisation, team or project across six interrelated dimensions: People & culture, Tools & technologies, Process, Communication, Work environment, and Governance. The aim is not perfect categorization but thinking through different facets of the context to reveal pain points and strengths.

activities.name: Set-up
activities.description: Prepare easel pad sheets or a digital whiteboard (like Mural) with each of the 6 dimensions: People & Culture, Tools & Tech, Process, Work Environment, Communication, and Governance.

activities.name: Individual Brainstorming
activities.description: Participants individually write up their challenges and bright spots on sticky notes.

activities.name: Categorization
activities.description: Participants post their notes on the walls or digital board in the relevant dimensions.

activities.name: Affinity Mapping
activities.description: The facilitator or a volunteer reviews, clusters, and summarises the responses into logical groups.

activities.name: Prioritization Voting
activities.description: Participants vote for the most pressing challenges and most relevant bright spots to focus on, potentially using a prioritisation matrix.

completion_criteria: The group has identified, categorized, and voted on the most critical challenges and strengths across all six dimensions.

metrics.name: Insight Density
metrics.formula: Total number of sticky notes generated

metrics.name: Consensus Rate
metrics.formula: (Number of items with more than 3 votes / Total number of unique items) * 100

guidelines.name: Switch: How to change, when change is hard

pitfalls: Debating the overlaps between dimensions for too long
Losing focus on whether the scope is the team, project, or organization
Failing to define terms for those who need clarification
benefits: Quickly builds shared understanding of context

name: Abstraction Laddering
objective: Reconsider a problem statement by broadening or narrowing its focus to explore context and possibilities.
description: Abstraction Laddering is a problem-framing activity that has participants reconsider problem statements by broadening or narrowing its focus. Participants brainstorm and explore 'why' and 'how' statements generated from the initial problem statement in order to explore context, possibilities, and potentially reframe the original problem statement to one that is more compelling or broader in nature.

activities.name: Initial Setup
activities.description: Identify an initial problem statement and draw a ladder with multiple rungs on a whiteboard or digital tool.

activities.name: Placement
activities.description: Write the initial problem statement in the middle rung of the ladder.

activities.name: Why Framing - Brainstorming
activities.description: Ask the group to write down 3 reasons as to WHY this is a problem, each on its own sticky note.

activities.name: Why Framing - Clustering
activities.description: Cluster similar stickies together and reword clusters with a new sticky that captures the essence of the group.

activities.name: Why Framing - Voting
activities.description: Have the group vote on which problem statements they like the most using 3 dots per person.

activities.name: Why Framing - Progression
activities.description: Place the top 3 stickies on the next rung up and repeat the brainstorming and voting steps twice more.

activities.name: Causality Framing - Brainstorming
activities.description: Going back to the original statement, ask the group to write down 3 answers to 'what problems does this cause?'.

activities.name: Causality Framing - Clustering
activities.description: Cluster similar causality stickies and reword them into problem statements that capture the cluster essence.

activities.name: Causality Framing - Voting
activities.description: Have the group vote on which causality-based problem statements are most compelling.

activities.name: Causality Framing - Progression
activities.description: Place the top 3 stickies on the next rung down and repeat the brainstorming and voting steps twice more.

activities.name: Problem Statement Selection
activities.description: Perform a final vote to select which problem statement on the entire ladder is most worth solving for the biggest impact.

completion_criteria: A final problem statement is selected and agreed upon by the team for the ideation phase.

guidelines.name: Using Abstraction Laddering: How to Build the Right Question and Stick to it
Up and Down the Ladder of Abstraction

pitfalls: The team decides the original problem statement was the best after all, feeling the time was wasted
Failing to capture the essence of clusters accurately during rewording
Focusing too much on technicalities instead of the problem context
benefits: Broadens the aperture of creative solutions
Increases the creative potential of the final solution
Helps reframe statements in a way that better lends itself to innovative thinking
Validates the current wording of a problem through exploration

name: Action steps
objective: Commit the first step towards action to bridge the gap between discussion and results.
description: Action steps is a collaborative practice that invites everyone to commit to taking a first step towards action. Inspired by De Bono's Action shoes, it focuses on identifying specific next steps (who, what, when, and with whom) to ensure work continues beyond a meeting or workshop.

activities.name: Talk
activities.description: Ask everyone to reflect on the work that's been done and how they might continue that work beyond the meeting.

activities.name: Type/Write
activities.description: Get everyone to write down Who (Name), What (Action to take), When (Time and Date it will be done), and With (Help from someone to get it done, review it, or celebrate it).

activities.name: Read
activities.description: Get each person to read their card out loud and request help from someone in the room.

activities.name: Review
activities.description: Use Affinity Mapping to cluster and summarize action steps, or vote to prioritize the most important actions to start with.

completion_criteria: Each participant has identified and publicly shared a specific, timed action and identified a collaborator or helper.

guidelines.name: Six Action Shoes - Dr. Edward de Bono

pitfalls: Meetings that feel like they are all talk and no action
benefits: Quick and easy practice to define next steps
Effective way to close a workshop
Encourages personal commitment to team outcomes

name: AEIOU Observation Framework
objective: Categorize and interpret observations gathered during user research field studies and usability testing.
description: AEIOU (Activities, Environments, Interactions, Objects, Users) is a heuristic framework used to document contextual inquiries during ethnographic studies. It provides a mnemonic for observing qualitative research and categorizing raw notes to inform research analysis and develop key insights.

activities.name: Prepare Worksheet
activities.description: Create a worksheet or discussion guide based on the AEIOU categories: Activities, Environments, Interactions, Objects, and Users.

activities.name: Conduct Observation
activities.description: Perform contextual inquiries or field studies while using the worksheet to capture body language, artifacts, quotes, and environmental details.

activities.name: Categorize and Analyze
activities.description: Organize raw notes into the five heuristic categories to facilitate research analysis and identify key insights.

completion_criteria: The observational data is categorized across the five AEIOU dimensions and incorporated into the research analysis deliverables.

pitfalls: Failing to iterate on category definitions to align with specific project goals
Relying on broad data collection without a strategy for categorization
benefits: Provides an easy-to-recall mnemonic for qualitative research
Quickly turns raw field notes into organized categories
Helps researchers focus on non-verbal cues like body language and environment

name: Affinity Mapping
objective: Identify insights, patterns and trends in research and ideas
description: Affinity Mapping is a practice used to organize ideas or insights. It allows large numbers of ideas stemming from brainstorming to be sorted into groups, based on their natural relationships, for review and analysis. It is also frequently used in contextual inquiry as a way to organize notes and insights from field interviews for synthesis and analysis. Additionally, it can be used for organizing qualitative data such as open-ended survey responses or support call logs.

activities.name: Define Objective or Collect Data
activities.description: Clearly state the objective of the brainstorm (e.g., How Might We statement) or collect all research data and write each observation on a separate sticky note.

activities.name: Post Notes
activities.description: Have the group post all notes on a flat working surface that is visible to everyone.

activities.name: Initial Clustering
activities.description: Move related sticky notes next to each other to start forming clusters based on relationships, involving the group in the process as much as possible.

activities.name: Manage Outliers and Redundancy
