activities.description: Create a parking lot for notes that do not fall into natural categories and retain redundant ideas to show commonality of thought.

activities.name: Categorize and Label
activities.description: Ask the group to suggest and agree on names for the columns or clusters and write them at the top of each group.

completion_criteria: All data points are organized into labeled clusters or columns with an outliers parking lot established.

metrics.name: Synthesis Completion Time
metrics.formula: End Time - Start Time

guidelines.name: Detailed post from the Interaction Design Foundation
Detailed steps and tips from General Assembly
Detailed steps and tips from Game Storming

pitfalls: Spending too much time agreeing on category names
Discarding redundant ideas which indicate frequency
Searching for higher categories too early in the process
benefits: Discover embedded patterns and break old patterns of thinking
Gathers meaning from large quantities of data
Provides a sense of group focus and priority

name: Agile Agenda
objective: A flexible way to manage the agenda for an in-person workshop using sticky notes and a whiteboard.
description: The Agile Agenda is a dynamic scheduling method for workshops that uses physical tools to maintain visibility and flexibility. By drawing a calendar on a whiteboard and using sticky notes for sessions, teams can manage schedules that don't fit neatly into fixed time boxes. It acts as an Information Radiator, providing a central location for team huddles and real-time updates as the workshop progresses.

activities.name: Prepare the Board
activities.description: Draw a day's calendar on a whiteboard and split it into two or more tracks if necessary.

activities.name: Create Agenda Items
activities.description: Write agenda items on sticky notes, ensuring you include the session owner on the note.

activities.name: Populate the Calendar
activities.description: Place the sticky notes into the correct time slots on the whiteboard calendar.

activities.name: Maintain and Update
activities.description: Review the agenda in the morning and return to it throughout the day to update it dynamically as sessions conclude or change.

completion_criteria: The workshop agenda is visibly displayed, updated to reflect real-time progress, and accessible to all participants.

metrics.name: Agenda Update Frequency
metrics.formula: Total number of sticky note moves or updates per day

pitfalls: Digital agenda format issues or access restrictions
Rigid time-boxing for complex sessions like Event Storming
benefits: Easily modified as session durations fluctuate

name: Agile Health Check
objective: A quick team assessment based on agile manifesto principles to align on individual and collective work preferences.
description: A team evaluation of how to get better based on agile principles. By making improvements tangible and actionable, it empowers the team to drive their own development and can provide themes for retrospectives.

activities.name: Define Boundary Scenarios
activities.description: Ask the team to describe how they would see things working if a principle was as great as possible versus as terrible as it could be.

activities.name: Contextualize Principles
activities.description: Amend or summarize principles based on the specific team context.

activities.name: Individual Voting
activities.description: Using a 1 to 10 scale, have individuals dot vote on where they see the team operating currently for each principle.

activities.name: Discussion and Analysis
activities.description: Calculate the median score for each item and discuss outliers to understand different perspectives.

completion_criteria: Median scores for all principles calculated and outliers discussed by the team.

metrics.name: Health Check Median Score
metrics.formula: Median of individual team member votes per principle

guidelines.name: Health checks for Teams and Leadership

pitfalls: Ignoring outliers during the discussion phase
Not making the results actionable

name: Alternative Worlds
objective: Use different perspectives to generate fresh ideas and break down mental barriers.
description: Alternative Worlds is a brainstorming exercise where a team intentionally solves a problem from a different point of view, such as that of a famous person, a creative organization, or another individual. By looking at common problems in an uncommon way, teams can overcome perceived constraints and discover new possibilities.

activities.name: Group Formation
activities.description: Separate participants into groups of 4-5 people.

activities.name: Brainstorm Entities
activities.description: Each group brainstorms entities (companies, brands, or people) that represent innovative problem solvers, using one sticky note per entity.

activities.name: Identify Shared Entities
activities.description: Each group discusses their list and identifies the entities that are known to everyone in that group.

activities.name: Select Perspective
activities.description: Each group selects one innovative problem solver from their shared list to act as their 'Alternative World'.

activities.name: Ideate from Perspective
activities.description: Groups brainstorm answers to the question: 'How would [selected entity] solve our problem statement?' using one sticky note per idea.

activities.name: Analysis
activities.description: Analyze the generated ideas using a visually-based facilitated group process like affinity mapping or impact/effort prioritization.

completion_criteria: Generated ideas have been analyzed and prioritized through a follow-up facilitated process.

guidelines.name: Alternative Worlds interview with Ben Tinker

pitfalls: Letting real or imagined constraints cloud the ability to think of new ideas
Selecting an entity for perspective that is not known to all group members
benefits: Helps teams break down mental barriers
Opens up new possibilities for great ideas
Gives participants freedom from their own perspectives

name: Architectural Decision Records (ADR)
objective: Maintain an open and transparent history of software design choices and architectural decisions.
description: An Architectural Decision Record (ADR) is a document that captures a software design choice addressing a functional or non-functional requirement that is architecturally significant. This includes technology choices, IDE choices, or feature decisions. ADRs provide a way to make implicit assumptions explicit and maintain a versioned history of project architecture to facilitate understanding for current and future team members.

activities.name: Research Templates
activities.description: Examine available ADR templates such as MADR, Michael Nygard's template, or Sustainable Architectural Decisions (Y-Statements).

activities.name: Select or Adapt Template
activities.description: Choose a template or create a custom adaptation in a format (e.g., Markdown) that works for the team and organization.

activities.name: Document Decision
activities.description: Write the record including the context, problem statement, options considered, and the final decision outcome with its rationale.

activities.name: Version and Store
activities.description: Store the decision record in a version-controlled repository to ensure it is easy to track and reference over time.

completion_criteria: The architectural decision is documented in the agreed template, stored in the project repository, and accessible to all relevant stakeholders.

pitfalls: Interpreting 'architecture' too strictly
Making the record-keeping process too heavy or difficult
Failing to version the decisions
benefits: Quick reference to understand past decisions
Shares thinking and methods with stakeholders

name: Autoscaling
objective: Changing amount of computing resources based on the applications measured load.
description: Autoscaling is a method of changing amount of computing resources based on the applications measured load. This load could be CPU, memory, network or some other measurable metric that can be used as the trigger of scaling. It can also be used as an automatic recovery for failed application instances. Because when coupled with health checks, auto scale can kill or restart the non-operational instance of the app and automatically replace it with a new working one.

activities.name: Identify Scaling Metrics
activities.description: Determine which metrics (CPU, memory, network, or queue size) will trigger scaling actions.

activities.name: Configure Health Checks
activities.description: Set up health checks to allow the system to identify and replace non-operational instances.

activities.name: Implement Load Balancing
activities.description: Ensure the application can dynamically add or remove instances from load balancing as they scale.

activities.name: Enable Scaling Policy
activities.description: Apply dynamic scaling features via cloud service providers, either manually or automatically.

completion_criteria: The application infrastructure automatically adjusts resource capacity in response to traffic or load fluctuations without manual intervention while maintaining availability.

metrics.name: Resource Utilization
metrics.formula: Average CPU/Memory usage per instance

metrics.name: Queue Depth
metrics.formula: Number of pending tasks in message queue

pitfalls: Application is not stateless
Lack of instance synchronization
Slow startup times for new instances
Costs exceeding budget if limits are not set
benefits: Support for rolling upgrades and rollbacks

name: BHAGs (Big, Hairy, Audacious Goals)
objective: Create compelling, long-term goal that can inspire and motivate groups of people to take action.
description: BHAGs stands for Big, Hairy, Audacious Goals. A BHAG is a compelling, long-term (10-30 years) goal that defines the essence of a team’s existence and motivates groups of people to take action with urgency. They complement shorter-term goals frameworks (3-12m) and are clear and compelling, needing little explanation.

activities.name: Select a Strategic Category
activities.description: Choose one of four categories to guide the goal creation: Role Model (emulating success), Common Enemy (David vs Goliath motivation), Targeting (setting a huge definable target), or Internal Transformation (revitalizing internally).

activities.name: Define the Long-term Goal
activities.description: Establish a clear, compelling target that defines the essence of the team's existence over a 10-30 year horizon.

activities.name: Communicate the Vision
activities.description: Formulate the goal into a concise statement that requires little explanation, similar to the 1961 Moon landing goal.

completion_criteria: A clear and compelling 10-30 year goal is defined that requires little explanation and inspires immediate action.

pitfalls: Creating goals that are too verbose or require too much explanation
Focusing only on short-term targets (3-12 months)
Setting a goal that is not sufficiently audacious or inspiring
benefits: Provides a huge target for an organization to hit

name: Blameless Postmortem
objective: To turn an incident into an investment in future reliability by identifying contributing factors without assigning blame.
description: Blameless Postmortem is a post-incident practice assessing an incident, its timeline, and contributing factors without judgment. It focuses on organizational learning rather than individual blame, evolving from Root Cause Analysis (RCA) by emphasizing psychological safety and the story of the incident.

activities.name: Information Gathering
activities.description: Secure incident tickets, reports, and a list of actions taken during remediation with timestamps and outcomes before the meeting.

activities.name: Identify Participants
activities.description: Identify all parties involved in the incident response to ensure they are represented at the meeting.

activities.name: Set Expectations
activities.description: Establish that the meeting is for learning, not blame, and assumes everyone acted with best intentions based on the information available at the time.

activities.name: Review Incident Timeline
activities.description: Walk through the incident timeline starting from identification, discussing major actions and the context in which they were taken.

activities.name: Analyze Actions and Outcomes
activities.description: Discuss actions in terms of what led to them, whether the outcome was positive or negative, and what could be done differently.

activities.name: Identify Remediation Points
activities.description: Raise and record specific Action Points to track remediation and decrease future reliability risk.

activities.name: Summarize and Close
activities.description: Create a brief, factual summary of the postmortem to be shared with stakeholders after review by senior engineers.

completion_criteria: The incident is fully understood by participants, and a postmortem summary has been prepared and accepted by all parties present.

metrics.name: Action Point Completion Rate
metrics.formula: Resolved Action Points / Total Action Points Created

metrics.name: Post-Incident Risk Level
metrics.formula: Initial Risk Score - Remediation Impact Score

pitfalls: Pointing fingers or assigning individual blame
Involving business stakeholders in the active discussion too early
Neglecting to follow up on identified Action Points
Prioritizing documentation over the actual story of the incident
benefits: Identifies factors leading to incidents and affected timelines
Promotes a safe culture where it is okay to fail

name: Blue Green Deployments
objective: Always have a fall back solution and reduce downtime to practically zero.
description: Blue Green Deployment is a technique that relies on two productive environments. One environment (green) is operational and takes load from the reverse proxy, while the other (blue) is a copy upgraded to a new version. The team performs upgrades on the idle environment without downtime pressure, redirects the load balancer once ready, and can instantly switch back if issues arise.

activities.name: Prepare Standby Environment
activities.description: Upgrade the idle environment (e.g., 'blue') to the new version while the other environment ('green') handles live traffic.

activities.name: Validation
activities.description: Perform smoke tests and various checks on the standby environment to ensure the new version is ready for production.

activities.name: Traffic Redirection
activities.description: Redirect the reverse proxy, load balancer, or router to point to the newly prepared environment.

activities.name: Monitor and Cycle
activities.description: If the new environment is stable, the old environment becomes the standby for the next release. If things go bad, switch back to the stable environment instantly.

completion_criteria: Traffic is successfully routed to the new version with zero downtime and the ability to roll back is maintained until stability is confirmed.

metrics.name: Deployment Downtime
metrics.formula: Switch Completion Time - Switch Start Time

metrics.name: Rollback Time
metrics.formula: Time to restore traffic to previous stable version

guidelines.name: Blue-green Deployments, A/B Testing, and Canary Releases

pitfalls: New version performance degradation relative to the previous version
Database schema synchronization issues between environments
Glitches during the routing switch
benefits: Reduced time pressure on technical teams

name: Burndown
objective: Visualisation of work left to do within a specified time period
description: A burndown chart is a visualisation of work left to do within a specified time period. The remaining work (or backlog) is often on the vertical axis, with time along the horizontal. Teams often use burndown charts to track progress within fixed time periods (for example, Sprint Burndown charts). They can also be used to visualise progress for longer time periods and milestones (for example, Release Burndown charts).

activities.name: Draw Axes
activities.description: Draw a horizontal and vertical line on the flip-chart.

activities.name: Set Time Axis
activities.description: Mark the horizontal axis with the time measurement (for a Sprint Burndown, this is likely to be each day in the Sprint).

activities.name: Set Work Axis
activities.description: Mark the vertical axis with the work being measured (this may be number of tasks, number of stories/features, number of story points or estimated time).

activities.name: Draw Ideal Trend
activities.description: Draw a diagonal line connecting the maximum work units to the last time measurement - this line represents the target/aspirational trend line.

activities.name: Burn Down Work
activities.description: As work is complete, draw a line (in a different color) representing the time point the remaining work has 'burned down' and how much work is remaining.

activities.name: Review and Radiate
activities.description: Re-visit and discuss what information the chart is radiating with the team and interested stakeholders.

completion_criteria: The burndown chart is updated, and the trajectory is reviewed and discussed by the team and stakeholders.

metrics.name: Remaining Work
metrics.formula: Total Scope - Completed Work

guidelines.name: Jeff Sutherland's background to the Burndown Chart

pitfalls: Assuming the burndown chart is a status report rather than a planning tool
Focusing on the line rather than the conversation it triggers
benefits: Provides real data about a team's velocity
Provides a mechanism to determine if current trajectory meets committed goals

name: Burnup Chart
objective: Track the progress of a project over time.
description: The Burnup Chart is a graphical representation used in project management, particularly within agile methodologies. It tracks the progress of a project by showing the amount of work completed over time against the total scope of the project. This chart typically consists of two main lines: one indicating the total amount of work (often referred to as the "scope line") and the other showing the work completed to date.

activities.name: Identify Total Scope
activities.description: Identify all the tasks, user stories, or features that need to be completed and represent this as the upper scope line on the chart.

activities.name: Track Completed Work
activities.description: Regularly update the chart with the amount of work completed at consistent time intervals, represented as a rising line.

activities.name: Update for Scope Changes
activities.description: Adjust the scope line whenever there are changes to the project's total scope to ensure accurate reflection.

activities.name: Review and Analyze
activities.description: Regularly review the chart with the team and stakeholders to discuss progress, forecast completion, and make adjustments.

activities.name: Utilize Tools
activities.description: Use project management software to automatically generate and update charts based on project data.

completion_criteria: The chart is updated with current scope and completion data, and a progress review has been conducted.

metrics.name: Forecasted Completion Date
metrics.formula: (Remaining Work / Velocity) + Current Date

guidelines.name: What is a burn up chart and how to create one

pitfalls: Ignoring scope changes in the scope line
Inconsistent update intervals
Misinterpretation of the gap between lines

name: Business Priorities Exercise
objective: Get alignment on what will drive value and realise goals.
description: The Business Priorities Exercise is a collaborative approach to getting stakeholders to agree on 10,000ft what the top priorities are to drive business value for whatever they are trying to achieve. It helps teams to first align and identify from 10,000ft what they should be focusing on to drive business value, rather than jumping directly into features which might not bind back to the actual value stream.

activities.name: Select Initial Priorities
activities.description: Ask the group to pick 3-4 top business priorities that they want to achieve from the project.

activities.name: Clarify and Frame
activities.description: Run through each priority with the team and provide an example without leading the participants. Facilitate an open discussion to frame the terms clearly.

activities.name: Identify Missing Priorities
activities.description: Provide empty spaces to add any new priorities in case the team identifies something missing during the framing discussion.

activities.name: Dot Voting and Mind Mapping
activities.description: Use Dot Marking to vote for the top 3 priorities, then create a mind map for those selections to identify their specific meanings.

activities.name: Deep Dive Analysis
activities.description: Take each of the top 3 priorities and use different coloured markers to denote current positives, current challenges, and future hopes.

completion_criteria: The team has reached a consensus on the top 3 business priorities and documented the associated challenges and future hopes for each.

metrics.name: Session Duration
metrics.formula: Actual duration of the exercise vs 60 minute timebox

guidelines.name: Business Priorities Exercise - Open Practice Library

pitfalls: Leading the witness during the framing discussion
Jumping directly into features instead of focusing on high-level value
Missing key business perspectives
benefits: Create a shared understanding of pains and gains
Envision the dream for the future

name: Bytesize Architecture Sessions
objective: Iteratively collaborate to learn about systems and create a homogeneous understanding within the team.
description: Bytesize Architecture Sessions are a workshop format that helps teams understand the systems they work on. Each session focuses on a small slice of a system, lasting between 45 and 90 minutes. It follows four parts: Session Goal, Alone Together, Consensus, and Summary.

activities.name: Preparation
activities.description: Invite the team (builders and requirement owners). Ensure participants are trained on the modeling tool, such as C4.

activities.name: Goal Setting
activities.description: Spend five minutes defining a specific subsection of the system to model as it exists currently.

activities.name: Alone Together
activities.description: Participants work quietly and individually on the model for 3-5 minutes, then each person explains their diagram to the group.

activities.name: Consensus
activities.description: Collaborate for 20-30 minutes to create a single, unified diagram from scratch based on the team's combined knowledge.

activities.name: Summary
activities.description: Conduct a mini-retro for the last few minutes to review achievements and collect feedback on sticky notes.

completion_criteria: Timebox respected and feedback/learnings captured on sticky notes.

metrics.name: Team Alignment Score
metrics.formula: Average of post-session understanding ratings

metrics.name: Session Velocity
metrics.formula: Total sessions / Months

guidelines.name: Original post about Bytesize Architecture Sessions

pitfalls: Group size exceeds 10 people
Focusing on the tool rather than the modeling
Skipping individual thinking time
Knowledge silos preventing consensus
benefits: Reduced technical debt through better planning
Improved team dynamics and active listening

name: C4 Architecture
objective: Allows for simple layers of abstraction (Context, Containers, Components, Code) for different components of an architecture design.
description: The C4 model is a lean graphical notation technique for modelling the architecture of software systems. It is based on a structural decomposition of a system into containers and components and relies on existing modelling techniques such as the Unified Modelling Language (UML) or Entity Relation Diagrams (ERD) for the more detailed decomposition of the architectural building blocks.

activities.name: Define Context
activities.description: Create a high-level diagram showing the system as a box in the center, surrounded by its users and the other systems it interacts with.

activities.name: Identify Containers
activities.description: Zoom into the software system to show the containers (applications, data stores, microservices, etc.) that make up the system.

activities.name: Outline Components
activities.description: Zoom into an individual container to show the components inside it and how they interact.

activities.name: Map Code
activities.description: Optionally zoom into an individual component to show how it is implemented as code (e.g., using UML class diagrams).

activities.name: Iterate and Refine
activities.description: Refine and iterate on diagrams during each options pivot and as part of the learning portion of the delivery loop.

completion_criteria: C4 diagrams are documented across the four levels of abstraction and are regularly updated to reflect the current state of the system.

metrics.name: Developer Onboarding Speed
metrics.formula: Time from start to full productivity

pitfalls: Diagrams becoming stale and not regularly updated
Spending too much time on level 4 (Code) diagrams
Overcomplicating the diagrams with too many details too early
benefits: Shared understanding across different delivery teams
Visualizes complex systems at different levels of abstraction

name: Canary Release
objective: Exposing a small number of real users to a new version of a product to monitor for regressions and performance issues.
description: In software development, this is a form of Continuous Delivery in which only a small part of the real users of a product will be exposed to the new version of the product. The team would monitor for regressions, performance issues and other adverse effects and can easily move users back to the working old version if issues are spotted. The term comes from the use of caged birds in coal mines to discover the build up of dangerous gases early on. The gases would kill the bird long before they become life threatening for the miners. As with the canary in the mine, this release practice provides an early warning mechanism for avoiding bigger issues.

activities.name: Initial Deployment
activities.description: Deploy the new version of the software to a small, controlled subset of users or infrastructure.

activities.name: Monitoring and Observation
activities.description: Closely monitor technical performance metrics, error rates, and regressions on the canary version.

activities.name: Analysis
activities.description: Compare the performance of the canary version against the baseline version to assess risks.

activities.name: Phased Rollout
activities.description: Gradually increase the number of users on the new version if no adverse effects are observed.

activities.name: Completion or Rollback
activities.description: Complete the full deployment to 100% of users, or roll back to the previous version immediately if issues are spotted.

completion_criteria: The new version is fully deployed to all users without technical regressions, or successfully rolled back if issues were found.

metrics.name: Error Rate
metrics.formula: Number of Errors / Total Requests

metrics.name: System Latency
metrics.formula: Average Response Time

guidelines.name: Blue-green Deployments, A/B Testing, and Canary Releases

pitfalls: Confusing Canary Release with A/B testing (which focuses on business outcomes rather than technical performance)
Inadequate monitoring tools making it hard to spot regressions early
Manual rollback processes that are too slow to mitigate user impact
benefits: Early warning mechanism for avoiding bigger issues
Enables phased roll-out with increased safety
Reduces the number of customers impacted by bugs or downtime
Provides prompt feedback from real-life use

name: Candy or Swag
objective: Demonstrate the value of open organization principles like collaboration and transparency through a game environment.
description: The Candy or Swag? practice uses a game environment to reinforce critical open organization values like collaboration and transparency. The goal is to demonstrate how outcomes improve for everyone when members of self-organizing teams collaborate and hold each other accountable for results, especially when transitioning from siloed legacies. It provides a memorable way to distribute team swag while teaching organizational values.

activities.name: Prerequisites and Setup
activities.description: Gather materials: enough individual pieces of candy for each participant for expected rounds, one piece of high-value swag per participant, and two index cards per student labeled Candy and Swag.

activities.name: Phase 1: Control
activities.description: Run rounds with no talking. Participants choose Candy or Swag cards hidden from others. If more than one person chooses Swag, no one gets anything. This illustrates silos and lack of communication.

activities.name: Phase 2: Collaboration
activities.description: Allow one minute of collaboration before participants choose cards. The choices remain hidden. This phase observes how the team attempts to establish sharing protocols and how bad actors might affect outcomes.

activities.name: Phase 3: Transparency
activities.description: Allow one minute of collaboration as before, but participants must place their chosen cards face-up on the table for everyone to see. This illustrates the benefit of combined collaboration and transparency.

activities.name: Distribute the Swag
activities.description: After reflection on the principles, distribute the high-value swag to everyone who has not already received it.

completion_criteria: All participants have received swag and the team has reflected on how collaboration and transparency improved the game outcomes.

metrics.name: Successful Swag Rounds
metrics.formula: Number of rounds where exactly one person (or the allowed limit) chose Swag

guidelines.name: Candy or Swag Game Blog Post
Teaching the Prisoner's Dilemma More Effectively

pitfalls: Building too much frustration in Phase 1 without explaining the point
Allowing a bad actor to ruin the experience without a follow-up transparency phase
Running out of time for reflection
benefits: Clearly demonstrates the cost of silos
Reinforces the value of transparency in group decision making
Creates a fun environment for distributing team swag

name: Celebrating Failure
objective: Raise the team's collective awareness of product failure root causes
description: Celebrating failure involves bringing the team together in an informal and safe environment to share first-hand stories of product failures and their root causes. These causes are visualized on large stickies on a 'Fail Wall' to empower team members to identify and prevent similar risks in current and future projects.

activities.name: Gather the team
activities.description: Bring the team together in an informal and safe environment, typically during the inception of a new product.

activities.name: Summarize root causes
activities.description: Team members write down the root causes of failure from their own first-hand experiences on large stickies.

activities.name: Share stories
activities.description: Participants take turns sharing their stories, providing brief context, the root cause, and the impact on the product.

activities.name: Visualize results
activities.description: Attach the stickies to a highly visible board (Fail Wall) for the team to reference throughout development.

activities.name: Ongoing updates
activities.description: Add new failures to the board as they are identified during development or during retrospectives to maintain awareness.

completion_criteria: The team has shared first-hand failure stories and root causes are documented and displayed on a visible board.

metrics.name: Fail Wall Items
metrics.formula: Total number of failure stories documented on the wall

pitfalls: Sharing stories without first-hand experience
Lack of a safe environment leading to a blame culture
Focusing on the failure without identifying the root cause
benefits: Empowers team members to prevent repeated failures

name: Change Agenda
objective: Capture and define the strategy changes or shifts that an organization or engagement needs to achieve in order to realize their desired outcomes.
description: Change Agenda is a framework used to identify strategic shifts or changes that an organization, team, or engagement needs to achieve. It allows a team to assess the current state and define the future state of an environment via change dimensions. It serves as a powerful communication tool, providing context for why changes are implemented and helping leadership articulate cultural, structural, and operating changes necessary for transition.

activities.name: Define Strategic Foundation
activities.description: Start with a vision statement, engagement goal, or strategic destination to anchor the change agenda.

activities.name: Generate Change Dimensions
activities.description: Identify the categories or dimensions where execution shifts must occur. Use affinity mapping to group and reduce the number of dimensions if necessary.

activities.name: Define Current State
activities.description: For each dimension, create a definition of the current situation. These are often identified as pain points or snapshots in time.

activities.name: Define Future State
activities.description: For each dimension, define the desired future state using descriptive, colorful language representing success factors and opportunities.

activities.name: Validate and Review
activities.description: Ensure alignment by reviewing the completed change agenda with stakeholders against the original vision and expectations.

completion_criteria: The Change Agenda is validated against the original vision statement or strategic destination, and stakeholders reach alignment on the identified execution gaps.

metrics.name: Execution Gap Clarity
metrics.formula: Number of Dimensions with Defined Future States / Total Change Dimensions

pitfalls: Practice becoming an 8th grade work-smithing experience
Client push back due to progress occurring between interview and review
Change dimensions becoming too granular rather than strategic supersets
benefits: Provides rich context for why changes are implemented
Articulates cultural, structural, and operating shifts
Triggers conversations to close the execution gap

name: Chaos Engineering
objective: Verify resilience of distributed software by deliberately introducing failure and faulty scenarios.
description: Chaos Engineering is a practice where an organization tries to predict the unpredictable by deliberately introducing failures on production systems to see how they withstand disruptions. It addresses failures that are hard to replicate in standard CI/CD pipelines, such as server shutdowns, network latency injection, and resource exhaustion.

activities.name: Identify Failure Scenarios
activities.description: Select disruptions to test such as server shutdowns, latency injection, or resource exhaustion (CPU/disk).

activities.name: Introduce Disruption
activities.description: Intentionally introduce the chosen failure scenario into the production system.

activities.name: Verify Resilience
activities.description: Monitor and observe how the application responds to the unpredictable disruptions.

activities.name: Improve Application
activities.description: Implement improvements to the application code or infrastructure to withstand the specific failure scenario.

completion_criteria: The application is verified to withstand the introduced failure scenario or has been updated to handle it successfully.

pitfalls: Running chaos experiments without proper monitoring
Starting with high-blast radius experiments before small ones
benefits: Allows developers to improve applications to withstand failure scenarios that are hard to replicate in a CI/CD pipeline
Allows operations to test failure scenarios that are hard to implement in CI/CD pipelines
Leads to overall application and infrastructure improvement

name: Chase the State
objective: Assess progress and the current state of any type of software delivery work in a holistic way
description: A fast, structured assessment enabling teams to quickly understand their software development progress. It uses an international OMG standard (Essence) to provide work states and checklists, ensuring a holistic review of all aspects of software development beyond simple metrics like burn-up charts.

activities.name: Preparation
activities.description: Download and print or screenshot the Essence Alpha state cards for the session.

activities.name: Layout Alphas
activities.description: Organize the 7 Alpha groups in rows on a table or digital board, with the first state on the left and the final state on the right.

activities.name: Checklist Walkthrough
activities.description: Starting with the first state of the first Alpha, review the checklist and ask the team if that state has been achieved.

activities.name: State Marking
activities.description: If a state is achieved, move the card to the left and proceed to the next state until an unachieved state is reached.

activities.name: Progress Poker
activities.description: If the team cannot reach consensus on a state, use a quick round of Progress Poker to align the team.

activities.name: Iterate Alphas
activities.description: Repeat the process for all remaining Alphas, typically in the order: Opportunity, Requirements, Software System, Team, Way of Working, and Work.

activities.name: Identify Actions
activities.description: Review the gaps identified to uncover risks, issues, or actions needed to progress the work successfully.

completion_criteria: All seven Alphas have been assessed and the team has consensus on the current state and necessary risks/actions.

metrics.name: Alpha State Progress
metrics.formula: (States achieved / Total possible states) * 100

pitfalls: Rushing ahead without sufficient agreement about requirements
Losing sight of the big picture by focusing only on delivery metrics
Ignoring checklist items in a given state before moving forward
benefits: Provides a holistic view of software development
Uncovers hidden risks and issues early
Applicable to any methodology including Scrum, Kanban, or Waterfall
Helps pinpoint specific actions needed to deliver value

name: Check-ins
objective: Foster deeper connections and belonging with simple questions
description: Start each session or gathering by inviting everyone to speak in turn, uninterrupted, for an agreed amount of time. This practice gives everyone the opportunity to speak up and be heard, establishing a foundation for psychological safety and testing equipment in virtual environments.

activities.name: Initiate Interaction
activities.description: Invite everyone to speak in turn, uninterrupted, for an agreed amount of time at the start of a session.

activities.name: Ask Check-in Questions
activities.description: Prompt participants with questions such as 'How are you really doing?' or 'What do you hope to get out of this call?'

activities.name: Pass the Turn
activities.description: Participants pass the turn to someone else until everyone has had a chance to speak.

completion_criteria: Everyone present has been given the opportunity to speak uninterrupted within the agreed timebox.

guidelines.name: Check in as a core protocol - The McCarthy Show
Check-in, Check out - Hyper Island Toolbox
Checking In and Out: What’s That All About? - Richard Kasperowski

name: Code Review
objective: Improve quality and knowledge sharing through collaboration and peer review.
description: Code Review is a software quality assurance activity where someone other than the author checks a piece of code for cleanliness, design, functionality, complexity, and adherence to standards. It fosters collective code ownership, promotes ego-less programming, and helps catch defects at the lowest-value stage.

activities.name: Select Review Type
activities.description: Determine the scale of the review: Short review for small changes (10 mins), Walkthrough for mid-level teaching (working meeting), or Inspection for deep dives (approx. 1 hour).

activities.name: Peer Examination
activities.description: Reviewer evaluates the code for logic, functionality, complexity, and test coverage, ensuring it follows clean code principles.

activities.name: Feedback Loop
activities.description: Reviewer shares findings with the author, focusing on the code rather than the person, to facilitate mentoring and knowledge sharing.

activities.name: Revision and Approval
activities.description: The author addresses the feedback and updates the code until it meets the team's quality standards and is ready for integration.

completion_criteria: The code meets cleanliness and functionality requirements, all critical feedback is addressed, and the peer reviewer approves the changes.

metrics.name: Review Duration
metrics.formula: End Time - Start Time

metrics.name: Defect Capture Rate
metrics.formula: Total number of defects found during review

guidelines.name: The Ten Commandments of Ego-less Programming

pitfalls: Taking feedback personally (lack of ego-less programming)
Performance and attention-to-detail drop after one hour of inspection
Authoritative rather than collaborative tone in comments

name: Cognitive Loadometer
objective: Measure team cognitive load to improve productivity, focus, and motivation.
description: Cognitive Loadometer is a tool based on cognitive psychology and Team Topologies theory to measure the total mental effort or working memory resources being used by a team. It uses a Subjective Cognitive Load Scale to help teams identify when they are overwhelmed by information, juggling too many priorities, or losing focus, allowing for better management of team capacity.

activities.name: Copy
activities.description: Make a copy of the Google Forms tool to own the survey instance.

activities.name: Customize
activities.description: Change the Team Name placeholder in the form to reflect your specific team.

activities.name: Share
activities.description: Send the survey link to your team members.

activities.name: Analyse
activities.description: Analyze the team responses within the connected spreadsheet to assess current cognitive load.

completion_criteria: The survey has been distributed, responses collected, and the resulting data analyzed by the team.

metrics.name: Subjective Cognitive Load
metrics.formula: Average of individual subjective load scores

pitfalls: Using the tool to officially diagnose clinical burnout
Misinterpreting the results as a performance metric for individuals
Neglecting to act on results once high load is identified

name: Cognitive walkthrough
objective: Evaluate usability and accessibility to identify potential problems and improvements early in the development process.
description: Cognitive walkthrough is an effective method for evaluating usability and accessibility. It is used to get concrete feedback and suggestions on how to make a service better by identifying potential problems in all phases of the development process, helping to avoid costly post-launch corrections.

activities.name: Initial Accessibility Assessment
activities.description: Use a WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) assessment tool to evaluate the current service accessibility level and understand existing technical debt.

activities.name: Provision Prototype Access
activities.description: Provide a link to the prototype or test environment to the users selected for the evaluation.

activities.name: Schedule Review Session
activities.description: Book a review appointment (on-site, hybrid, or remote) with users who are new to the service or have specific accessibility needs.

activities.name: Step-by-Step Implementation
activities.description: The development team and selected users go through the service together step by step at a leisurely pace.

activities.name: Analysis and Perspective Review
activities.description: Review each step from a usability and accessibility perspective based on current requirements, guidelines, and recommendations.

activities.name: Report Compilation
activities.description: Compile a report containing the findings and a concrete action plan for improvements.

completion_criteria: The step-by-step review is finished and a report with an action plan has been compiled.

metrics.name: Accessibility Issues Found
metrics.formula: Total number of accessibility defects identified during the walkthrough

guidelines.name: Usability Body of Knowledge - Cognitive Walkthrough

pitfalls: Correcting deficiencies after the service has launched
Inefficient use due to poor usability
High training and support costs from accessibility gaps
benefits: Concrete feedback on usability and accessibility
Identify potential problems early in development
Cost-effective compared to other evaluation methods

name: Cohort Analysis
objective: To study the difference in behaviour between different groups of people (cohorts) to produce actionable insights and inform product decisions.
description: Cohort analysis is a subset of behavior analytics which segments the population into cohorts sharing common characteristics (such as skill level, sign-up date, or specific events) and then searches for patterns in their behaviors. Instead of treating all users as one population, it enables teams to understand how specific changes or demographics affect long-term metrics like retention.

activities.name: Use Annotations
activities.description: Start using Google Analytics Annotations or a marketing calendar to track marketing activities and campaigns to see their impact in reports.

activities.name: Test Marketing Activities
activities.description: Run experiments to test audiences, channels, and design by week or month, and schedule weekly reviews of the Cohort Analysis report.

activities.name: Drill Down on Unusual Cohorts
activities.description: Identify significant changes in metrics for specific cohorts and turn them into custom segments to investigate the behavior driving those movements.

completion_criteria: Actionable insights are identified and unusual patterns in user behavior are documented to inform product strategy.

metrics.name: User Retention
metrics.formula: Retained Users / Total Cohort Population

guidelines.name: A Beginner’s Guide to Cohort Analysis

pitfalls: Using gut feel instead of data-driven insights
Treating all users as part of a single population
Ignoring unusual increases or decreases in metric movements
benefits: Provides a feedback loop from real-life use of product changes
Enables better decisions regarding product overhaul or discontinuation
Drives improvements toward concrete, measurable user behavior goals

name: Collaborative Pathways
objective: Turning shared purpose into shared results by aligning individual and collective motivations.
description: Collaborative Pathways is a practice designed to recognize and align the diverse incentives that drive individuals, teams, and departments. By openly discussing motivations, aspirations, goals, and concerns, participants can build a foundation of trust and transparency. This mutual understanding facilitates the creation of a joint vision and measurable outcomes, turning disparate motivations into effective, unified collaboration.

activities.name: Individual Reflection
activities.description: Individuals, teams, or departments take time separately to document their specific motivations, aspirations (dreams), goals, and concerns.

activities.name: Mutual Sharing
activities.description: All parties come together to share and discuss their individual responses, building insight into each other's drivers and establishing trust.

activities.name: Define Joint Vision
activities.description: Distill individual goals into joint goals and draft a unified joint vision that serves the collaboration or common customer.

activities.name: Determine Outcomes
activities.description: Identify desired outcomes and define specific, measurable objectives that track progress toward the joint vision.

activities.name: Establish Governance
activities.description: Plan regular revisits to the pathway to discuss progress, incorporate new insights, and update terms as the partnership evolves.

completion_criteria: A joint vision and set of measurable outcomes have been agreed upon, and a schedule for regular review has been established.

metrics.name: Alignment Ratio
metrics.formula: (Joint Goals / Total Individual Goals) * 100

guidelines.name: A New Approach to Contracts - HBR

pitfalls: Treating terms and conditions as a negotiation rather than a problem-solving exercise
Failing to revisit the agreement as insights change
Overlooking individual concerns during joint visioning
benefits: Builds foundation of trust and transparency
Aligns disparate goals towards a unified vision
Turns shared purpose into shared results

name: Comparing Concept Maps
objective: Identify differences in team members' mental models to uncover friction points or potential oversights.
description: A whole-team activity where participants draw and compare concept maps of the project or problem domain. It helps reveal how different team members (e.g., Development vs. Marketing) perceive relationships between key ideas, identifying healthy perspective differences or problematic contradictions.

activities.name: Individual Drawing
activities.description: Each team member spends 5 minutes drawing a small concept map of what the team is working on, separately and without looking at each other's work.

activities.name: The Reveal
activities.description: On the count of 3-2-1, team members reveal their concept maps to each other simultaneously.

activities.name: Facilitated Discussion
activities.description: The meeting moderator leads a discussion focused on the differences, missing elements, or conflicting connections between the concept maps.

completion_criteria: All participants have revealed their maps and the moderator-led discussion of differences has concluded.

pitfalls: Contradictions between mental models can be a source of friction if not addressed.
Missing elements or connections in models can lead to project oversights.
benefits: Identifies healthy variations in role-based mental models.
Particularly useful for onboarding new team members.
Highlights missing connections in the team's understanding of a problem.

name: Confidence Voting
objective: Gains consensus around a team's agreement or disagreement on the current activity, event or questions
description: Confidence Voting, also known as Fist of Five, is a technique used to gauge the collective confidence or agreement of a group. It allows for a quick, democratic, and quantitative measurement of sentiment. Participants use their fingers to vote on a scale of zero (no confidence/block) to five (full confidence/championing), followed by a facilitated discussion based on the results to either check-in, build consensus, or move forward with a decision.

activities.name: Determine the goal
activities.description: Choose one specific goal for the vote: to check-in on a topic, to build consensus, or to move forward on a topic where the team is stuck.

activities.name: Suggest the practice
activities.description: Propose the practice to the group, explaining its purpose and checking for any objections to performing it immediately.

activities.name: Explain the practice
activities.description: Define the scoring system (0 to 5 fingers) and demonstrate the physical actions (e.g., a fist for zero). Explain that everyone will vote simultaneously on the count of three.

activities.name: State the question
activities.description: Clearly articulate the specific question or proposal that the team is voting on.

activities.name: Count and Vote
activities.description: The facilitator counts 1, 2, 3, and everyone raises their hands at the same time to show their score.

activities.name: Tally and Facilitate Discussion
activities.description: The facilitator tallies the results and guides the subsequent conversation based on the initial goal, such as hearing from outliers with low scores to understand their reservations.

completion_criteria: The facilitator has tallied the votes and the group has reached a decision, consensus, or a clear understanding of the team's current sentiment to proceed with the next agenda item.

metrics.name: Confidence Level
metrics.formula: Sum of all individual votes / Number of participants

guidelines.name: Lucid Meetings - Fist of Five voting
Learning with fist of five voting - Helping improve LLC

pitfalls: Allowing discussion before the vote which can bias participants
People looking at others to see how they vote instead of voting independently
The 'loudest voice in the room' dominating the post-vote discussion
Failing to explain that a fist (zero) acts as a block in consensus-seeking scenarios
benefits: Provides a quick and democratic way to hear from everyone
Captures quantitative data on qualitative feelings
Serves as a safety check for team agreement

name: Container
objective: A software package that contains everything the software needs to run.
description: A Linux container is a sandboxed process or processes isolated on a VM or bare-metal server using mechanisms such as Linux Namespacing, CGroups, and SELinux contexts. Containers package applications or services with only necessary files and libraries, sharing the underlying kernel. This creates an immutable container image that can be run consistently across different environments.

activities.name: Image Creation
activities.description: Package the application or service specific code along with its required files and libraries into an immutable container image.

activities.name: Security Scanning
activities.description: Perform security scans on the container image to ensure compliance and identify vulnerabilities before deployment.

activities.name: Execution
activities.description: Run the container image on a container engine across VMs or bare-metal servers to utilize infrastructure efficiently.

completion_criteria: The container image is built, scanned for security, and successfully started in less than a second on the target infrastructure.

metrics.name: Startup Speed
metrics.formula: Time to application ready - start command execution

metrics.name: Infrastructure Density
metrics.formula: Total containers running / Total number of servers

guidelines.name: Wikipedia entry for Bare Metal Server

pitfalls: Operations team requiring application-specific knowledge if images are not properly encapsulated
Security teams not integrated into the image scanning process
Ignoring kernel sharing limitations between container and host

name: Contract-First Development
objective: Get agreement upfront when creating reusable services to enable parallel development and clear intent.
description: Contract-First Development involves defining the programming API clearly at the outset using specifications like OpenAPI for synchronous or AsyncAPI for asynchronous interactions. The contract is updated before implementation changes occur, serving as the source of truth for code generation, testing, and documentation.

activities.name: Define Contract
activities.description: Create a clear definition of the programming API using tools like OpenAPI or AsyncAPI at the outset of the project.

activities.name: Establish Consensus
activities.description: Get agreement across team members, external teams, or third-party systems on the defined contract.

activities.name: Generate Mocks and Stubs
activities.description: Use the contract to generate mock services and client/server stubs to enable parallel development.

activities.name: Parallel Implementation
activities.description: Allow UI and service developers to work concurrently against the agreed contract rather than waiting for working services.

activities.name: Validate and Update
activities.description: Update the contract before implementing any code changes and use automated tools to validate the implementation against the contract.

completion_criteria: The API contract is defined, agreed upon by all stakeholders, and successfully passes automated validation and testing tools.

metrics.name: Parallel Productivity Gain
metrics.formula: Time saved by avoiding UI blocking - contract maintenance time

metrics.name: Contract Compliance Rate
metrics.formula: (Implementation matches contract / Total endpoints) * 100

pitfalls: Implementing code before updating the contract
Allowing the contract and implementation to drift apart
Ignoring feedback from consumers during the contract definition phase
benefits: Code generation for clients and servers
Simple mocking of services under development

name: Crazy 8s
objective: A fast sketching exercise to generate eight distinct solution ideas in eight minutes.
description: Crazy 8’s is a core Design Sprint method. It is a fast sketching exercise that challenges people to sketch eight distinct ideas in eight minutes. The goal is to push beyond your first idea, frequently the least innovative, and to generate a wide variety of solutions to your challenge.

activities.name: Distribute materials
activities.description: Give each participant a single sheet of printer paper and a sharpie.

activities.name: Prepare paper
activities.description: Have participants fold their sheet of paper in half 3 times to make eight sections.

activities.name: Set timer
activities.description: Set the timer for eight minutes (1 min per section to sketch an idea).

activities.name: Sketching
activities.description: Each team member sketches 8 variations of the same idea, or 8 different ideas into each section of the sheet of paper.

activities.name: End session
activities.description: When the timer goes off, put your pens down.

activities.name: Presentation
activities.description: Have team members spend 2 min presenting their 8 ideas to everyone.

completion_criteria: Eight sketches per participant completed and shared within the timebox.

metrics.name: Sketch count
metrics.formula: Total sketches / participants

guidelines.name: How to use Crazy 8s to generate design ideas

pitfalls: Developing the first solution without considering alternatives
Focusing too much on the quality of the drawing instead of the idea
benefits: Ensures a product team prioritizes user needs
Broadens the potential for finding the best idea
Allows teams to compare and contrast multiple solutions

name: Creativity Warmups
objective: Break down mental barriers and stretch creative thought before ideating solutions
description: Creativity Warmups are a set of quick, creative thinking activities based on the Torrance Test of Creative Thinking (TTCT). These activities act as a mental warmup to stretch the group's creative thinking by focusing on creative titles, expressions, imagery, and humor, helping participants move beyond obvious answers toward innovative ideas.

activities.name: Preparation and Distribution
activities.description: Pick one or a few exercises to run with your team and hand out sample questions or prepare digital templates on a whiteboarding tool for all participants.

activities.name: Exercise Explanation
activities.description: Explain the exercise to the group, emphasizing that big, wild, and unique ideas are most valuable to push past obvious answers.

activities.name: Silent Ideation
activities.description: Set a timer for 5 minutes and have participants work silently on their ideas, providing extra templates if they run out of space.

activities.name: Sharing and Wrap-up
activities.description: Close the exercise by moving directly into ideation or have team members share their ideas, potentially using dot-voting to award a creative trophy.

completion_criteria: The timebox for the exercises has been respected and participants are prepared to transition into the main ideation session.

metrics.name: Warmup Duration
metrics.formula: End Time - Start Time

guidelines.name: What is the Torrance Test of Creative Thinking? (TTCT)
Torrance Test of Creative Thinking Example Questions
Blog: LightBulbBites - Making the non-creatives more creative

pitfalls: Focusing on allocating points or scoring instead of warming up
Relying on obvious or traditionally taught answers
Running out of physical space or digital templates during silent ideation
benefits: Expands thinking beyond standard intelligence and reasoning
Leads to more creative and innovative product ideas
Stretches group creative thinking before critical ideation sessions

name: Dark Launches
objective: Release new features to a subset of end-users to capture behaviors and feedback before a full rollout.
description: Dark Launches are a continuous delivery practice which involves releasing new features to a small subset of the user base to understand real-life impact and validate product/market fit. This method allows teams to test if an application works as planned and observe user interactions—which may be unexpected—before committing to a full-scale launch. It provides safety by limiting the blast radius of new changes and helps in deciding whether to continue, modify, or discontinue a feature.

activities.name: Readiness Assessment
activities.description: Evaluate if users are able to find the feature, if they are aware of the change, and whether they need to know about it before proceeding.

activities.name: Limited Release
activities.description: Deploy the feature to a specific subset of end-users while keeping the majority on the existing version.

activities.name: Monitoring and Observation
activities.description: Track behavioral changes in the application and among users to determine if the deployment is achieving the intended success.

activities.name: Full Transition
activities.description: Disable old functionality by removing old code or updating configurations once positive findings are confirmed.

activities.name: Post-Launch Stabilization
activities.description: Keep old code available for a few days or weeks to ensure no critical bugs emerge that require a rollback.

completion_criteria: The feature is successfully enabled for all users, old code is decommissioned, and performance metrics indicate stability.

metrics.name: User Engagement
metrics.formula: Total interactions with new feature / total subset users

metrics.name: Technical Success Rate
metrics.formula: (Successful requests / Total requests) * 100

guidelines.name: Dark Launching: A Way to Test New Features Before Going Live

pitfalls: Negative user interactions surfacing that were not envisioned by the team
Assuming the application is 100 percent correct immediately after launch
Technical bugs appearing after the old code is removed too early
benefits: Limits the impact of new features to a subset of users
Provides prompt feedback from real-life usage
Validates product/market fit for new features
Enables discovery of novel user interaction patterns

name: Lightning Decision Jam
objective: To identify pain points, prioritize problems, ideate solutions, and assign actionable next steps for experiments in a short period.
description: The Lightning Decision Jam (LDJ) is a flexible workshop exercise created by AJ&Smart. It combines elements of Design Thinking, Gamestorming, and Design Sprints to help teams solve problems and focus on constant improvement. It is designed to prevent a few voices from dominating the conversation and ensures that every team member's ideas are considered, leading to fast follow-through and clear experimentation.

activities.name: Identify Successes
activities.description: Identify the things that are currently working well for the team.

activities.name: Capture Problems
activities.description: Capture all the problems or obstacles that are holding the group back.

activities.name: Prioritize Problems
activities.description: The team votes to prioritize the captured problems.

activities.name: Reframe as Challenges
activities.description: Reframe the prioritized problems as standardized challenges.

activities.name: Individual Ideation
activities.description: Individuals ideate solutions to the problems independently.

activities.name: Prioritize Solutions
activities.description: The team prioritizes the proposed solutions together.

activities.name: Selection
activities.description: Decide which specific solution the team will execute on.

activities.name: Actionable Planning
activities.description: Make the chosen solutions actionable using the S.M.A.R.T framework.

completion_criteria: The workshop is complete when solutions have been prioritized and converted into actionable S.M.A.R.T. steps with assigned owners.

metrics.name: Solution Conversion Rate
metrics.formula: Actioned Solutions / Total Identified Problems

metrics.name: Workshop Duration
metrics.formula: End Time - Start Time

guidelines.name: Lightning Decision Jam - a workshop to solve any problem

pitfalls: Small group of people dominating the conversation
Meetings that result in no follow-through or action items
benefits: Helps find areas of constant improvement
Extremely flexible for different group sizes

name: Defence in Depth
objective: Layering security controls through your application to build redundancies so that if one control fails, others remain to protect the system.
description: Defence in depth (layered security) is an approach to securing a system, service, or piece of software that focuses on including strong security controls on all layers of a stack or design, rather than focusing all effort on securing only the outward- or user-facing parts of the solution. The aim is to build redundancies into security controls so that, if one control fails (either temporarily or permanently), the other controls will still be able to protect the application.

activities.name: Threat Modelling
activities.description: Conduct a threat modelling exercise at the start of an iteration or during project kickoff to identify potential vulnerabilities.

activities.name: Principle of Least Privilege
activities.description: Ensure all users or accounts have only the minimum permissions required to perform their functions.

activities.name: Implement Authentication Controls
activities.description: Apply robust authentication mechanisms such as password policies and multi-factor authentication.

activities.name: Configure Network Security
activities.description: Implement firewalls, security groups, and strict ingress/egress rules to control traffic.

activities.name: Physical and Insider Threat Assessment
activities.description: Review physical access to infrastructure and address potential threats originating from within the organisation.

activities.name: Assume Breach Analysis
activities.description: Think about internal controls under the assumption that the outer perimeter has already been breached.

completion_criteria: Security controls are implemented across all layers of the stack and redundancies are verified to handle individual control failures.

metrics.name: Redundant Controls Count
metrics.formula: Number of security layers per critical system component

guidelines.name: Secure Design Concepts talk by Tanya Janca
The Wikipedia article on Defence in Depth
Cloudflare's overview of Defence in Depth

pitfalls: Focusing all effort only on user-facing security
Assuming the outer layer of defences will hold up against all attacks
Ignoring insider threats
Passing user-submitted strings directly as program variables
benefits: Provides protection even if one security control fails
Reduces the likelihood of thorough exploitation by attackers
Ensures security is a concern for the entire team

name: Design of Experiments
objective: To turn ideas, hypotheses, and assumptions into a concrete, well-defined set of experiments to provide valuable learning and validate product direction.
description: Design of Experiments is an alternative to traditional ROI-based investment analysis. It focuses on validating the most important assumptions as early as possible through structured tests. By implementing small changes and measuring outcomes (qualitatively or quantitatively), teams can pivot or proceed based on real-world proof rather than intuition. It is an essential component of the Build-Measure-Learn loop.

activities.name: Formulate Hypothesis
activities.description: State the assumption as a clear sentence that can be tested.

activities.name: Assess Current Condition
activities.description: Measure the current situation as accurately as possible to establish a baseline.

activities.name: Define Target Condition
activities.description: Determine the measurable outcome or goal the experiment is trying to achieve.

activities.name: Identify Obstacles
activities.description: List potential interferences, noise, or barriers that could prevent achieving the target condition.

activities.name: Define Pass Criteria
activities.description: Establish what constitutes a positive result or significant enough change to confirm the hypothesis.

activities.name: Determine Measures
activities.description: Identify specific metrics and data collection methods to track progress.

activities.name: Execute and Capture Learning
activities.description: Run the experiment, track data, and record outcomes to drive the next set of decisions or experiments.

completion_criteria: The experiment provides valid and reliable data that shows a statistically significant conclusion, and the learnings are captured to inform future actions.

metrics.name: Statistical Significance
metrics.formula: Correlation between experiment change and target outcome change.

guidelines.name: How to Measure Anything: Finding the Value of Intangibles in Business
Design of Experiments from Lean Six Sigma Perspective

pitfalls: Ambiguous outcomes that are too difficult to judge
Poor quality data leading to flawed conclusions
Ignoring obstacles that cause statistical noise
Failing to define a binary or measurable pass/fail criteria
benefits: Drives innovation in new and existing products
Enables autonomous teams to deliver on leadership intent

name: Design Sprint
objective: A five-day process for testing new ideas and rapidly solving key challenges.
description: It is a 5 day customer-centric process for rapidly solving a key challenge, creating new products, or improving existing ones. The process compresses work into one week and provides real user feedback in rapid fashion, enabling teams to clarify problems, explore solutions through sketching, and prototype ideas to find out if a product is worth undertaking.

activities.name: Monday: Map and Sketch
activities.description: Define the long-term goal and sprint questions, map the challenge, and conduct lightning demos and drawing ideas (Crazy 8's) to develop concepts.

activities.name: Tuesday: Decide and Storyboard
activities.description: The team decides on the solution through voting and creates a storyboard for the final walk-through while continuing user test recruiting.

activities.name: Wednesday: Prototype
activities.description: The day is dedicated to building the prototype, preparing for user tests, and setting up the interview room.

activities.name: Thursday: Test
activities.description: The prototype is tested with end-users to gather feedback, followed by an analysis of results and preparation of findings.

activities.name: Friday: Showcase
activities.description: A formal demonstration of the process and findings to stakeholders.

completion_criteria: Completion of end-user testing, analysis of results, and a final showcase demonstrating findings.

metrics.name: Time to Validate
metrics.formula: 5 Days

metrics.name: User Success Rate
metrics.formula: Successful tasks / Total tasks

pitfalls: Making expensive commitments before validation
Lack of real user feedback during testing phase
Failing to recruit the right users for testing
benefits: Reduces risks when bringing a new product to market
Rapidly identifies if a feature is worth the effort
Compresses months of work into a single week

name: Design The Box
objective: To translate product vision into tangible characteristics by imagining its physical packaging and core slogans.
description: This Design Thinking practice involves team members imagining the box or packaging for a new product. Participants develop slogans that capture the essence of the product and highlight its most valuable features. It encourages big-picture thinking from the perspective of potential customers and helps articulate the product goal without present-day constraints.

activities.name: Visualize Packaging
activities.description: Team members imagine the physical box or carton for the new product or service, often starting with a blank box.

activities.name: Develop Slogans
activities.description: Create slogans that correspond to the essence of the product and its primary value proposition.

activities.name: Highlight Features
activities.description: Identify and list the most important and valuable features that would be showcased on the packaging.

completion_criteria: A physical or conceptual box design is completed with slogans and prioritized features identified.

metrics.name: Feature Identification
metrics.formula: Total number of core features identified on the box

pitfalls: Getting bogged down in present-day technical constraints
Focusing too much on aesthetics rather than value and goals
benefits: Helps translate vision into felt characteristics
Allows easy articulation of product goals

name: Disagree and Commit
objective: Enable teams to transition from thinking to doing without needing consensus, ensuring commitment to execution.
description: An approach to enable teams to transition from thinking to doing, whilst ensuring the team are committed to the execution. It addresses 'analysis paralysis' by reducing the barrier between designing an experiment and executing it, moving away from forced consensus (which leads to compliance) towards commitment (an intrinsic motivator). This practice acknowledges that collaboration does not require consensus and that the team can commit to an action while holding differing opinions, provided there is a pathway for feedback and course correction.

activities.name: Provide Choice
activities.description: Ensure every team member has a voice and a choice in the decision-making process to foster inclusivity.

activities.name: Establish Review Points
activities.description: Agree on specific review points and define the evidence or tests required to evaluate the hypothesis during the delivery phase.

activities.name: Decompose Problems
activities.description: Break down large problems into a series of smaller, manageable experiments to allow for regular course correction.

activities.name: Set Short Delivery Iterations
activities.description: Ensure delivery periods are not too long to minimize the risk of executing a sub-optimal solution for extended periods.

activities.name: Commit and Inspect
activities.description: Team members commit to the execution regardless of initial disagreement, knowing they will inspect, evaluate, and adapt based on evidence at the next review point.

completion_criteria: The team moves from the thinking/options phase into execution with a clear commitment to the current experiment and an agreed-upon review date.

metrics.name: Decision Velocity
metrics.formula: Date of Commitment - Date Discussion Started

metrics.name: Team Commitment Score
metrics.formula: Average of individual confidence votes after the 'Commit' phase

pitfalls: Analysis paralysis
Mistaking consensus for collaboration
Long delivery cycles without feedback loops
Achieving compliance through coercion rather than commitment
benefits: Eliminates friction caused by forced consensus

name: Dissent Cards
objective: Create an environment in which people can disagree in a productive way.
description: A way to encourage greater diversity of ideas and increase psychological safety by assigning roles that mandate or allow dissent during decision-making. Taken from L. David Marquet's 'Leadership is language', it uses red and black cards to make dissent a required part of the process rather than a personal confrontation.

activities.name: Distribute Cards
activities.description: During an options generation session, hand out cards at random. Use a ratio of one red card for every five black cards.

activities.name: Identify Roles
activities.description: Establish that a red card holder MUST dissent during discussions, while a black card holder has the freedom to choose whether to dissent or agree.

activities.name: Conduct Discussion
activities.description: When an option is presented, the red card holder performs their role of challenging the idea. This provides safety because the dissent is seen as a role requirement rather than personal conflict.

activities.name: Practice Curiosity
activities.description: Black card holders must remain curious rather than compelling, using open questions to understand the dissenter's point of view and identified risks.

activities.name: Consolidate and Commit
activities.description: After hearing all challenges, the group uses 'Disagree and Commit' to arrive at a final decision and move forward as a unified team.

completion_criteria: The discussion is finished within the timebox and the team has reached a point of 'Disagree and Commit' for the selected options.

metrics.name: Outlier Rate
metrics.formula: (Outlier ideas proposed / total ideas proposed) * 100

guidelines.name: Leadership is Language by L. David Marquet
The Six Thinking Hats by Edward de Bono

pitfalls: Response to dissenter becomes defensive instead of curious
Dissent cards used to be obtuse without objective value
Group fails to move to commitment after the dissent phase
Lack of anonymity regarding who holds the red cards

name: Docs As Code
objective: Have the whole team deliver current, useful, documentation using the minimum of effort.
description: Docs As Code (also known as “Docs Like Code”) is an approach to delivering documentation for software projects that uses the same tools and processes as software development. It focuses on text-based content, developer workflows, machine-generated content, automated testing, and continuous delivery.

activities.name: Text-based Content Creation
activities.description: Use text-based file content with embedded, lightweight markup tags such as Markdown, reStructuredText, or AsciiDoc.

activities.name: Establish Developer Workflows
activities.description: Implement version control (Git), change control via tickets, and content reviews/merges.

activities.name: Automate Content Generation
activities.description: Use tools like Mermaid.js or PlantUML for diagrams, and run CLI programs to insert dynamic content or code fragments.

activities.name: Automated Testing and Verification
activities.description: Use tools like Vale for style guide validation or Spectral/Redocly for Open API Specification linting.

activities.name: Continuous Delivery
activities.description: Optionally use static site generators like Sphinx or Hugo to automate the publishing process.

completion_criteria: Documentation is merged into version control, passes automated verification tests, and is published via the delivery pipeline.

metrics.name: Documentation Linting Score
metrics.formula: Count of style guide and schema violations

metrics.name: Documentation Lead Time
metrics.formula: Time from content creation to publication

guidelines.name: Write the Docs: Docs as Code Guide
