pitfalls: Documentation being a bottleneck in the release cycle
Reliance on proprietary technical writing tools
Inconsistent documentation style without automated linting
benefits: Deliver valuable documentation faster with higher quality
Remove need for proprietary technical writing tools
Free technical writers for higher value tasks like information architecture

name: Document Review Process
objective: Efficiently review documents in complex environments, avoid loops, and get a more predictable timeline.
description: This practice describes how to review a document in a complex environment with possibly a lot of reviewers with diverging interests, and a strong requirement for a very formal review process. It ensures the review is straightforward, avoids loops, and optimizes effort.

activities.name: First Review
activities.description: The first review ensures all reviewers understand the purpose, scope, and structure of the document to focus their feedback on important aspects.

activities.name: Parallel Feedback Gathering
activities.description: Multiple reviewers provide feedback in parallel without blocking the author's time.

activities.name: Second Review
activities.description: Discuss feedback provided earlier and modifications prepared by the author. Only modified parts are reviewed; no new changes are discussed.

activities.name: Action Item Closure
activities.description: Follow-up action items are closed, leading automatically to the sign-off of the document.

completion_criteria: Closing of concrete action items leading to the final sign-off of the document.

metrics.name: Review Duration
metrics.formula: Actual End Date - Start Date

pitfalls: Endless loops of review of review of review
Reviewing the complete document in the second review
Introducing new changes in the second review session
Vague action items that do not lead to sign-off

name: Domain Storytelling
objective: Understand how people and/or software systems work together by recording domain stories using a pictographic language.
description: A workshop format that helps participants understand business processes. Domain experts share typical examples of how they work, known as Domain Stories. While listening, a moderator records the domain stories using a pictographic language, allowing domain experts to see immediately if the moderator understands correctly. It focuses on concrete examples (what actually happens) rather than abstract possibilities.

activities.name: Storytelling
activities.description: Domain experts share typical examples of how they work.

activities.name: Recording
activities.description: The moderator records the domain stories using a pictographic language while the experts speak.

activities.name: Guided Questioning
activities.description: The moderator guides the experts by asking specific questions like who, does what, with what, and why.

activities.name: Immediate Validation
activities.description: Domain experts review the pictographic representation immediately to ensure accuracy.

completion_criteria: Participants are able to talk about the people, activities, tools, work objects, and events in that domain and a shared understanding is reached.

metrics.name: Story Completion Time
metrics.formula: 10-30 min per Domain Story

metrics.name: Participant Engagement
metrics.formula: Number of domain experts actively contributing

pitfalls: Recording all things that could possibly happen instead of one concrete example
Modeling at the wrong level of detail for the objective
Lack of domain expert availability
benefits: Learn a new domain and its language
Find boundaries for microservices or bounded contexts
Show how new software systems will change current business processes

name: Dominating Forces
objective: Surface risks and issues, identify metrics, and define the impact of strategy execution before it starts.
description: Dominating Forces is a facilitated practice used to communicate or validate existing strategies or establish new ones. It focuses on reaching specified goals while considering detractors and challenges related to culture and execution. It helps surface risks for projects, programs, or products before development begins. Strategies cover what to do, what not to do, and how to compete, while culture focuses on people, skills, and team topologies, and execution addresses processes, tools, and metrics.

activities.name: Agree on Goals
activities.description: Ensure the goals are clearly defined and agreed upon by all participants. If goals are missing, start with the 'Start at the end' practice.

activities.name: Establish or Present Strategies
activities.description: Subject Matter Experts present existing strategies or the group uses a 10-for-10 exercise to create new strategies that support the agreed-upon goals.

activities.name: Flush out Challenges and Bright Spots
activities.description: Allow 3 to 7 minutes to identify detractors and positive aspects within the areas of execution (processes, tools) and culture (people, communication).

completion_criteria: Goals and strategies are agreed upon, and challenges and bright spots in execution and culture have been identified and documented.

metrics.name: Strategy/Goal Alignment
metrics.formula: (Strategies Linked to Goals / Total Strategies) * 100

pitfalls: Strategies failing to define what the team will NOT do
Ignoring cultural detractors or team topology issues
Lack of cohesive Mission, Vision, and Goals framework
benefits: Provides immediate reality checks for strategies
Identifies potential roadblocks and challenges early
Aligns lofty goals with the reality of execution capabilities

name: Dot voting
objective: Quickly build consensus to prioritise available options
description: Dot Voting is a collaborative decision-making technique. It helps people to prioritise or select options from a range of choices. Participants use sticky dots or simply draw dots with a marker to visually indicate their preferences or priorities. It is a simple and effective method for reaching consensus, making informed decisions, or allocating resources.

activities.name: Prepare the Options
activities.description: Write down the list of options or ideas that the group needs to prioritize. Ensure that the options are clear and well-defined.

activities.name: Explain the Process
activities.description: Inform the participants about the purpose of Dot Voting and the number of dots they will receive.

activities.name: Vote
activities.description: Participants place their dots next to the options they consider the most important or their top choices.

activities.name: Count the Dots
activities.description: After all participants have placed their dots, count the number of dots next to each option.

activities.name: Discussion
activities.description: Optional: Facilitate a discussion to explore the reasons behind the choices or to reach a final decision.

activities.name: Final Decision
activities.description: Based on the results, make a final decision, allocate resources, or prioritize tasks accordingly.

completion_criteria: The dots are counted and a visual consensus or prioritization of options is achieved to guide final decision making.

metrics.name: Consensus Level
metrics.formula: Number of dots on top 3 options / Total number of dots

guidelines.name: Dot Voting: How Many Dots? - NNgroup

pitfalls: Voters being influenced by seeing where others have already placed dots
Ambiguity in the options being voted on
Uneven distribution of voting power if dots are not counted correctly

name: Double Diamond
objective: A structured approach to tackle challenges in four phases, from exploring to taking focused actions.
description: This is a practice used in the design process or design workshop comprising four distinct phases: Discover, Define, Develop, and Deliver. It uses iterative cycles of diverging (expanding options) and converging (focusing on solutions) to innovate and solve problems in complex systems.

activities.name: Discover / Research
activities.description: Diverge by exploring the idea through analysis, brainstorming, and speaking to people affected by the issues to understand the problem.

activities.name: Define / Synthesis
activities.description: Converge by focusing on the right idea and defining the challenge in a different way based on the insights gathered during discovery.

activities.name: Develop / Ideation
activities.description: Diverge by generating potential solutions, seeking inspiration from various sources, and co-designing prototypes with a range of people.

activities.name: Deliver / Implementation
activities.description: Converge by testing out different solutions at a small scale, rejecting those that do not work and improving those that do through validation.

completion_criteria: The solution has been iterated upon and validated through small-scale testing, with a final improved version delivered.

pitfalls: Failing to reject solutions that do not work
Not putting the voice of the customer at the center
Lack of iteration during the deliver phase
benefits: Break down problems in complex systems

name: Dual Track Delivery
objective: Establish collaborative practices for developers and designers to build shared understanding and optimize delivery through parallel discovery and delivery tracks.
description: Dual Track Agile is a method for incorporating user experience designers into agile delivery teams to support cross-team collaboration, align design and development kanbans, and optimize the efficiency of both tracks. The practice focuses on running a 'Discovery' track and a 'Delivery' track simultaneously to ensure features are validated before full implementation.

activities.name: Team Integration
activities.description: Combine design and development teams into one cross-functional unit, co-locating them and setting up collaborative workspaces with wall space and whiteboards.

activities.name: Backlog Merging
activities.description: Merge design tasks into the same product backlog as delivery tasks to ensure visibility and shared priorities.

activities.name: DesignOps Implementation
activities.description: Enable DesignOps to set standards and refine processes for design tools, systems, communications, and participant recruitment for testing.

activities.name: Collaborative Story Mapping
activities.description: Execute story mapping as a collaborative exercise involving both designers and developers to build shared context.

activities.name: Time Allocation
activities.description: Ensure delivery team members dedicate 10% of their time per sprint to collaborate with the design team on stories currently in the design kanban.

completion_criteria: Features validated with customers prior to development, shared understanding established across design and development, and feedback loops reduced.

metrics.name: Rework Rate
metrics.formula: (Number of stories requiring rework / Total stories delivered) * 100

metrics.name: Feedback Loop Duration
metrics.formula: Time elapsed from design handover to developer feedback or validation

guidelines.name: Case Study of Customer Input For a Successful Product
Dual Track Agile: the secret sauce to outcome-based development

pitfalls: Designers becoming aware of developer needs for wireframes too late
Developers receiving designs that are technically impossible or inefficient to implement
Designers working on features without understanding delivery priority
benefits: Validating features with customers before development begins
Reducing protracted feedback loops between design and development teams

name: Eisenhower Box or Urgent-Important Matrix
objective: Help individuals and teams prioritize tasks by categorizing them based on urgency and importance.
description: The Eisenhower Matrix is a decision-making tool consisting of a four-quadrant square that visually organizes tasks based on level of urgency and importance. Tasks are categorized into: Quadrant 1 (Urgent & Important), Quadrant 2 (Not Urgent & Important), Quadrant 3 (Urgent & Not Important), and Quadrant 4 (Not Urgent & Not Important).

activities.name: List Your Tasks
activities.description: Start by doing a brain dump of all the tasks you need to complete, both professional and personal.

activities.name: Draw the Matrix
activities.description: Create a large square and divide it into four equal quadrants. Label columns 'Urgent' and 'Not Urgent', and rows 'Important' and 'Not Important'.

activities.name: Categorize Each Task
activities.description: Go through your list and place each task into one of the four quadrants by asking if it requires immediate attention and if it contributes to long-term goals.

activities.name: Take Action Based on the Quadrant
activities.description: Do Q1 tasks immediately, schedule Q2 tasks in your calendar, delegate Q3 tasks if possible, and delete Q4 tasks from your list.

completion_criteria: Every task from the initial list is categorized into one of the four quadrants with a corresponding action (Do, Schedule, Delegate, or Delete) assigned.

metrics.name: Time Allocation in Q2
metrics.formula: (Time spent on Not Urgent/Important tasks / Total work time) * 100

guidelines.name: Avoid the Urgency Trap with Dwight D.

pitfalls: Mistaking urgent tasks for important ones
Overloading Quadrant 1 with too many crises
Neglecting Quadrant 2 which contains long-term strategic value

name: Emerging Architecture
objective: Capture enough information to get started, build something, learn and iterate.
description: Emerging architecture is the practice of having just enough architecture so that product development keeps moving forward while remaining flexible enough for changes as more information becomes available. It involves experimenting and adapting the architecture as business, customer, or regulatory needs evolve, ensuring it addresses multiple perspectives like deployment, testing, and operations.

activities.name: Technical design group-think
activities.description: Conduct exercises in parallel thinking where team members contribute ideas collaboratively and simultaneously, focusing on potential ways forward rather than hierarchy.

activities.name: Reduce toil and automate
activities.description: Identify undifferentiated manual work and automate processes to improve product quality and feedback loops.

activities.name: Data-driven trade-off analysis
activities.description: Carefully consider the quantity and quality of data to make architectural engineering decisions, such as processing at the edge versus central processing based on latency or cost.

completion_criteria: The team has just enough knowledge of the business domain and logical architecture to deliver high-business value features for the current iteration.

pitfalls: Building a comprehensive architecture that is not yet adding business value
Following only the most senior person's way of thinking
Losing massive sources of opportunity by forgetting to think about lost data
Investing time in architecture that may never be used due to reprioritization
benefits: Allows for better quality and feedback
Ensures architecture fits current and potential future requirements
Avoids wasted time on unused comprehensive upfront designs

name: Establish Shared Principles
objective: Establishing a set of shared principles derived from purpose and values to guide everyday actions and practices.
description: This practice focuses on the 'underwater' portion of the organizational iceberg. It involves defining Purpose (why the organization exists), Values (shared beliefs), and Principles (rules for how to act) before deciding on Practices (specific actions). It emphasizes that while practices evolve and change, principles and values are enduring foundations that ensure a transformation's success.

activities.name: Define Purpose
activities.description: Articulate why the organization exists and what its core mission is.

activities.name: Identify Values
activities.description: Establish a set of shared beliefs that will guide the organization's behavior.

activities.name: Derive Principles
activities.description: Create rules for how the team will act, derived directly from the purpose and values.

activities.name: Align Practices
activities.description: Select specific actions and ceremonies (practices) that embody the established principles.

completion_criteria: The organization has clearly articulated its purpose, values, and principles, and these are documented to guide future practice adoption.

guidelines.name: Extreme Programming: Values, Principles, and Practices
Why We Fail to Change: Understanding Practices, Principles, and Values Is a Solution

pitfalls: Blindly following practices without understanding the underlying principles
Copying practices from successful organizations without adopting their values
Implementing practices before defining 'how we will act' (principles)
benefits: Increases the likelihood of successful organizational transformation
Provides a stable foundation that helps weather organizational 'storms'
Allows practices to evolve effectively while maintaining core identity
Improves transparency and understanding of work

name: Event Modeling
objective: To model Event-Driven Software Systems with clarity for all roles by linking UX/UI all the way through to persistence via a visual representation.
description: Event Modeling is a method to design a blueprint for an Information System of any size or scale. It facilitates clear communication across diverse organizational roles and ensures system completeness by tracing the single thread of data propagation throughout the system.

activities.name: Design System Blueprint
activities.description: Create a visual representation of the Information System that models the workflow and data at any scale.

activities.name: Link UI/UX to Persistence
activities.description: Map user interface experiences through the event flow and down to the persistence layer for a full system view.

activities.name: Verify Completeness
activities.description: Follow the single thread of data propagation through the model to check for system completeness and logic consistency.

completion_criteria: The system can be checked for completeness by following a single thread of data propagation through the entire model.

benefits: Clear communication across a large cross-section of roles
System can be checked for completeness
Visual clarity from UX to persistence layer

name: Tormenta de eventos (Event Storming)
objective: Un método rápido e interactivo de descubrir procesos de negocio y crear diseños que rinden modelos de alta calidad.
description: El Event Storming es una técnica de modelado colaborativo diseñada para descubrir procesos de negocio. Busca establecer un lenguaje común entre los equipos de negocio e ingeniería mediante la identificación de pasos, usuarios, interfaces de usuario y agregados. Se basa en un enfoque iterativo y visual, utilizando post-its y diagramas físicos o digitales.

activities.name: Preparación de materiales y entorno
activities.description: Organizar rollos de papel, post-its de colores y marcadores para sesiones físicas, o configurar un pizarrón virtual interactivo para sesiones remotas.

activities.name: Descripción del caso ideal
activities.description: El equipo de negocio describe el proceso de negocio desde la perspectiva de los usuarios enfocándose en el caso donde el usuario logra su objetivo.

activities.name: Identificación de datos
activities.description: El equipo de negocio identifica qué datos específicos se requieren para que el caso ideal pueda cumplirse.

activities.name: Integración de ingeniería
activities.description: El equipo de ingeniería se une para aportar detalles técnicos como Eventos, Datos e Interfaces de Usuario para establecer una comprensión compartida.

activities.name: Agrupación arquitectónica
activities.description: El arquitecto agrupa objetos comunes para definir los límites de los microservicios que se deben desarrollar.

work_products: Inventario de pantallas de interfaz de usuario
completion_criteria: Se ha logrado un entendimiento compartido del proceso y existe un diagrama (físico o digital) que documenta los pasos, usuarios, pantallas y agregados dentro del ámbito.

metrics.name: Tiempo de descubrimiento
metrics.formula: Suma de horas de las sesiones realizadas

pitfalls: Sesiones demasiado largas sin descansos cognitivos
Exceso de participantes en sesiones remotas que impiden la comunicación eficaz
Falta de participación activa de todos los integrantes
No repasar el Event Storm regularmente durante la sesión
benefits: Más rápido y divertido que las maneras tradicionales de modelado
Establece un lenguaje común entre negocio e ingeniería
Fija un objetivo de enfoque claro en el ámbito y límites del proyecto
Identifica preguntas importantes de UX al inicio del proceso
Forma eficaz de empezar con Domain Driven Design

name: Event Storming
objective: A rapid, interactive approach to business process discovery and design that yields high quality models.
description: Event Storming is a rapid, interactive approach to business process discovery and design that yields high quality models. It was introduced in a blog by Alberto Brandolini in 2013. It establishes a shared understanding of the business process including scope, users, UI screens, and Aggregates, resulting in a physical or digital diagram.

activities.name: Map Business Process
activities.description: The Business Team maps out the process from a User's perspective, starting with the 'happy scenario' where the user successfully reaches their goal.

activities.name: Identify Data Requirements
activities.description: The Business Team identifies which data is required in order to make the business flow successful (e.g., specific prices or session times).

activities.name: Technical Enrichment
activities.description: The IT team joins the business team to provide additional information in forms of Events, Data, and User Interfaces to establish a shared understanding.

activities.name: Architectural Grouping
activities.description: The architect groups common objects in order to define the microservices to be developed.

activities.name: Validation Walkthrough
activities.description: Walk through the Event Storm forwards and backwards regularly to ensure shared understanding and prompt for further input.

completion_criteria: A physical or digital diagram is produced, common objects are grouped for microservices, and a shared understanding of scope, users, and aggregates is reached by both Business and IT teams.

metrics.name: Session Duration
metrics.formula: Total hours spent modeling

metrics.name: Model Coverage
metrics.formula: Identified steps / Observed business steps

guidelines.name: Aggregates as Defined by Martin Fowler

pitfalls: Too many participants in remote sessions disrupting conversation flow
Participants disengaging or going into 'view only' mode in remote sessions
Participants getting tired without cognitive breaks
Sticky notes falling off walls if not using drawing paper
benefits: Faster than traditional process modeling techniques
Establishes common language between Business and IT
Sets focus on scope and boundaries
Forces important questions about the customer journey early
Effective way to kick start Domain Driven Design

name: Everything-as-Code
objective: Treat all parts of the system as code, including configuration, infrastructure, and pipelines, to ensure systems are traceable, repeatable, and easily recreated.
description: Everything as Code is the practice of treating all parts of the system as code. This means storing configuration along with Source Code in a repository such as git or svn. Storing the configuration from bottom to top (communication switches, bare metal servers, operating systems, build configurations, application properties and deployment configurations) as code means they are tracked and can be recreated at the click of a button. This allows virtual environments to be life-cycled and recreated whenever needed, moving away from old-world IT where infrastructure required specialized skills and physical hardware.

activities.name: Identify System Components
activities.description: Determine all infrastructure, configuration, and pipeline elements that define the product environment.

activities.name: Define as Code
activities.description: Write scripts or configuration files using tools like Terraform or Ansible to represent the identified components.

activities.name: Store in Version Control
activities.description: Commit all configuration and infrastructure code into a shared repository (e.g., Git) alongside the application source code.

activities.name: Automate Provisioning
activities.description: Enable automated workflows to build, update, or recreate environments directly from the stored code.

completion_criteria: All system components are version-controlled and the entire environment can be automatically recreated from the repository without manual intervention.

metrics.name: Time to Provision Environment
metrics.formula: Time at end of automated creation - Time at start of command

pitfalls: Configuration drift due to manual changes (Snowflake servers)
Storing secrets in plain text within the repository
Lack of shared language between Dev and Ops
benefits: Traceability: Track who changed config and why
Repeatability: Easy migration between cloud providers
Tested Infrastructure: Confidence in production behavior
Phoenix servers: No fear of server death or patching
Shared understanding: Developers and Operations speak the same language

name: Expand and Contract Pattern
objective: A pattern to help in modernisation and migration projects by allowing changes to be made incrementally without breaking dependencies.
description: The Expand and Contract pattern (also known as the Parallel Change pattern) is a technique used in software development to implement breaking changes to an interface, data schema, or API in a safe and incremental way. It prevents breaking existing consumers by temporarily supporting both the old and new versions of the logic or data structure.

activities.name: Expand
activities.description: Introduce the new version of the interface or data structure while maintaining the old one. Both versions exist simultaneously.

activities.name: Migrate
activities.description: Update all consumers or clients to transition from using the old version to the new version.

activities.name: Contract
activities.description: Once all consumers have successfully migrated, remove the old interface and the supporting 'bridge' code.

completion_criteria: The old code or data structure has been removed, and all systems are functioning correctly using the new implementation.

metrics.name: Migration Progress
metrics.formula: (Consumers updated / Total consumers) * 100

guidelines.name: Parallel Change on Martin Fowler's Blog

pitfalls: Leaving the 'expand' phase active indefinitely, leading to permanent technical debt.
Inconsistent data states if both versions are writing to separate storage without synchronization.
benefits: Reduces the risk of breaking changes across distributed systems.
Allows for smaller, more manageable pull requests.

name: Feature Flag
objective: Change software's functionality without changing and re-deploying code.
description: Feature Flags (also known as Feature Toggles, Bits, or Controls) involve wrapping bits of functionality in conditional statements (usually if-statements) that are evaluated at runtime. The value of the flag is typically managed via configuration files, databases, or specialized UIs, allowing developers and product owners to decouple code deployment from feature releases. This practice enables strategies like Canary Releases, Dark Launches, and A/B testing while supporting Trunk Based Development by allowing incomplete code to be merged safely into the main branch.

activities.name: Identify Feature Scope
activities.description: Determine which part of the software functionality needs to be toggled and define the criteria for its activation (e.g., release, experiment, ops, or permissioning).

activities.name: Implement Flag Logic
activities.description: Wrap the specific code path in a conditional statement (e.g., an 'if' block) that checks a flag variable.

activities.name: Externalize Control
activities.description: Configure the flag value in an external source such as a configuration file, environment variable, database, or feature management dashboard.

activities.name: Deploy and Toggle
activities.description: Deploy the code as latent functionality and then update the external configuration at runtime to activate or deactivate the feature for specific users or environments.

completion_criteria: The feature's behavior can be successfully modified at runtime by changing a configuration value without requiring a new deployment of the source code.

metrics.name: Time to Release
metrics.formula: Time of Flag Activation - Time of Deployment

metrics.name: Deployment Frequency
metrics.formula: Total deployments to production per time period

pitfalls: Stale toggles causing technical debt
Inconsistent state across distributed systems
Over-complicating testing logic due to multiple flag combinations
benefits: Separate code deployment from feature release
Allows for A/B testing and experimentation

name: Fire Drills
objective: Increase the confidence of your Team by gamifying Incident Management.
description: Fire Drills is a fun and safe way to practice Incident Management and Response in a real-world environment replica. An extension of Chaos Engineering, it focuses on the human element, using quest-like simulations to help teams adapt to cloud-native applications. It helps teams unite and develop skills to Detect, Identify, Communicate, and Resolve incidents within a psychologically safe environment.

activities.name: Establish Game Council and Moderators
activities.description: Assign roles for the Game Council (to ensure alignment with product goals) and Game Moderators (experts to create and execute scenarios).

activities.name: Define Scenarios
activities.description: Create a rulebook and set of patterns for incident simulations based on real-world scenarios.

activities.name: Prepare Environment
activities.description: Setup a production replica where incidents can be triggered without impacting live users.

activities.name: Run Game Days
activities.description: Moderators trigger incidents that the engineering team must Detect, Identify, Communicate, and Resolve.

activities.name: Real-time Communication
activities.description: Players report phases, communicate escalations, and talk to vendors through shared channels like Slack or Teams.

activities.name: Review and Post-mortem
activities.description: Moderators assess players' actions and collaboration; the team shares post-mortems and identifies system gaps.

completion_criteria: All scheduled scenarios have been executed, the Engineering team has completed the Detect-Identify-Communicate-Resolve loop for each, and post-mortems have been shared with the organization.

metrics.name: Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)
metrics.formula: Time incident detected - Time incident triggered

metrics.name: Mean Time to Resolve (MTTR)
metrics.formula: Time incident resolved - Time incident detected

pitfalls: Moderators lacking didactic skills to handle the aftermath
Scenarios not aligning with the Product Owner's wishes
Inadequate communication platform setup preventing realistic reporting
benefits: Increases team confidence in managing incidents
Identifies gaps in system metrics and alerts
Provides a psychologically safe environment to fail and learn

name: Fly on the Wall
objective: A crowdsourced approach to problem solving where the problem owner listens to group discussion without interrupting.
description: This practice is designed to provide a crowdsourced approach to problem solving. The problem is posed to the group by 'the fly' and the group then discusses the problem without any interruption from 'the fly'. It is intended to stop the problem owner from prematurely dismissing ideas (e.g., 'I've tried that') and forces them to remain curious while ideas fully form. It is particularly suited for teams working as individuals or pairs to demonstrate the power of group thinking and inclusive problem solving.

activities.name: Outline the Problem
activities.description: The 'Fly' outlines the problem, ideally without discussing solutions or previous efforts to date to avoid biasing the group (3-5 minutes).

activities.name: Clarification Questions
activities.description: The team asks questions about the problem to clarify their understanding of the problem space.

activities.name: Group Discussion
activities.description: The team discusses the problem in depth and starts to come up with possible solutions. The 'Fly' listens curiously and makes notes without interrupting the flow (10-20 minutes).

activities.name: Fly Response
activities.description: The 'Fly' thanks the group and responds to what they have heard. This may involve identifying next steps, requesting a deep dive, or providing further information based on the discussion.

completion_criteria: The 'Fly' provides feedback on the group's discussion and determines if further iteration or next steps are required within the timebox.

metrics.name: Timebox Adherence
metrics.formula: Total Session Time - Allocated Time

metrics.name: Idea Generation Volume
metrics.formula: Count of unique solutions proposed during discussion

pitfalls: The 'Fly' interrupting the group discussion
Dismissing ideas prematurely with 'I've tried that'
Failing to make notes while listening
benefits: Reduces cognitive bias in problem solving
Leverages group think for complex problems

name: Force Field Analysis - Force Field Map
objective: Evaluate the forces pro and con that are at play with regards to a specific organizational change.
description: A social science method developed by Kurt Lewin and adapted into a visual map by Dave Gray. It is used to facilitate change by building a systemic understanding of the driving and restraining forces within an environment.

activities.name: Prepare the Visual
activities.description: Draw a representation of the potential change in the center of a whiteboard and label it clearly.

activities.name: Define Force Sections
activities.description: Write Forces FOR Change on the top left and Forces AGAINST Change on the top right.

activities.name: Draw Visual Cues
activities.description: Draw large arrows on both sides pointing toward the central image to house category names.

activities.name: Introduction
activities.description: Gather the group, introduce the topic, and explain that the goal is to evaluate change feasibility.

activities.name: Generate Driving Forces
activities.description: Players spend 5-10 minutes silently generating ideas for forces driving the change on sticky notes.

activities.name: Generate Restraining Forces
activities.description: Players spend 5-10 minutes silently generating ideas for forces restraining the change on sticky notes.

activities.name: Weight the Forces
activities.description: Players assign a value from 1 (weak) to 5 (strong) to each sticky note.

activities.name: Affinity Mapping
activities.description: Gather and sort the FOR change sticky notes into clusters based on affinity.

activities.name: Categorize Clusters
activities.description: Create overarching categories for each cluster and write them inside the arrows on the map.

activities.name: Score Categories
activities.description: Calculate the average numeric score for each cluster and write it next to the category in the arrow.

activities.name: Process Opposite Side
activities.description: Repeat the clustering, categorization, and scoring steps for the AGAINST change sticky notes.

activities.name: Calculate Totals
activities.description: Sum the quantities for and against change and write the totals at the bottom of the sheet.

activities.name: Discussion
activities.description: Summarize findings and discuss the implications of whether the change should proceed.

completion_criteria: The map is completed with weighted categories for both driving and restraining forces, and the group has discussed the implications of the final totals.

metrics.name: Net Force Difference
metrics.formula: Total Forces FOR - Total Forces AGAINST

guidelines.name: Wikipedia Entry for Force Field Analysis

pitfalls: Lack of participant diversity
Inaccurate weighting of forces
Bias in clustering
benefits: Provides visual clarity on complex organizational dynamics

name: Formal Relational Contract
objective: Govern highly complex relationships that demand collaboration and flexibility which exist in an ecosystem
description: The formal relational contract is a new approach to governing complex relationships that demand collaboration and flexibility within an ecosystem. While transactional legal contracts are still relevant, they do not cover all required parts essential for collaboration based on transparency and trust. It provides a written framework for smooth collaboration, determining operating models, and defining rules of engagement between two or more parties.

activities.name: Social Contract
activities.description: Establish a social contract to enable team autonomy and self-accountability as a foundation for psychological safety.

activities.name: Build Foundation based on Trust
activities.description: Be transparent about aspirations, individual goals, and concerns while building multi-level relationships between parties.

activities.name: Set Vision and Goals
activities.description: Define a joint vision for the ecosystem along with desired outcomes, goals, and tactical, measurable objectives.

activities.name: Adhere to Guiding Principles
activities.description: All parties share interpretations of core principles: reciprocity, autonomy, honesty, loyalty, equity, and integrity.

activities.name: Align Terms and Conditions
activities.description: Define responsibilities, pricing, and metrics as a joint problem-solving exercise aligned to the defined principles.

activities.name: Establish Governance Mechanisms
activities.description: Form governance teams (e.g., relationship, excellence, sustainability, and best-value teams) to manage the ongoing relationship and scope updates.

activities.name: Consolidate and Iterate
activities.description: Gather all learnings into a canvas for an overview and regularly revisit individual and joint goals, concerns, and processes.

completion_criteria: The formal relational contract canvas is completed, governance teams are established, and all parties have agreed to the shared guiding principles and measurable objectives.

pitfalls: Relying solely on transactional (legal) contracts which do not cover trust-based collaboration
Struggling to define rules of engagement without a formal relational framework
Failing to iterate on goals and concerns as the ecosystem reality changes
benefits: Smooths out collaboration in complex ecosystems
Provides right level of trust and transparency
Turns shared purpose into shared results

name: Funnel Analysis
objective: Analyze user conversion and fallout rates through a series of steps in a user journey to achieve specific goals.
description: Funnel Analysis is an analytical practice studying the changes over a course of events or steps in a user journey. It frequently follows the flow inside an application towards achieving a specific goal, such as purchasing an item. The analysis shows how many users complete the full flow and where they drop off (fallout rates).

activities.name: Define Funnel Steps
activities.description: Identify the sequence of events or steps in a user journey towards a specific goal.

activities.name: Data Integration
activities.description: Feed the analysis with data from A/B testing, Multi-vari testing, and feature toggles.

activities.name: Analyze Performance
activities.description: Measure conversion and fallout rates at each step of the journey.

activities.name: Actionable Insight Generation
activities.description: Produce insights to improve product performance, user experience, and business outcomes.

completion_criteria: Production of actionable insights used for data-informed decision-making on product design and features.

metrics.name: Conversion Rate
metrics.formula: (Users reaching the goal / Users starting the funnel) * 100

metrics.name: Fallout Rate
metrics.formula: (Users dropping off at step X / Users entering step X) * 100

guidelines.name: The Lean Startup by Eric Ries

pitfalls: Misinterpreting drop-off points without qualitative context
Using insufficient data samples for decision making
benefits: Provides a feedback loop from real-life usage
Allows for benchmarking of similar products

name: Futurespective
objective: Place the team in the future by imagining that goals have been reached to build a common understanding and identify necessary actions.
description: Futurespective is an exercise where a team imagines a future state where their goals have already been achieved. It begins by discussing and documenting team goals to ensure shared understanding. Teams may also identify the benefits of reaching these goals and even perform a mock celebration to emphasize their importance. This practice helps teams learn from their hopes and positivity while identifying the vital few actions needed to bridge the gap between the present and the desired future.

activities.name: Setup your board
activities.description: Prepare the workspace or digital board for the exercise.

activities.name: Step forward
activities.description: Imagine moving forward to a specific point in the future (e.g., end of a sprint or project).

activities.name: Look back at the past
activities.description: From the future perspective, look back at what was achieved and how.

activities.name: Analyze the gap
activities.description: Identify the difference between the current state and the imagined successful future.

activities.name: Step forward into the future again
activities.description: Re-engage with the future vision to refine details.

activities.name: Look back at the past and find your improvements
activities.description: Identify specific actions and improvements that led to the success.

activities.name: Vote
activities.description: Prioritize the actions to select the vital few that need to be started now.

completion_criteria: Team has agreed on the vital few actions needed to get started and recorded them for the next cycle.

metrics.name: Action Item Count
metrics.formula: Count of prioritized actions to be implemented immediately

pitfalls: Defining too many action items
Lack of focus on the vital few actions needed now
benefits: Increased team awareness of goal importance
Identifies improvements based on positive future states
Helps predict potential obstacles by looking back from success

name: GEMs
objective: Provide an approach to objective setting and measurement focused on experimentation as an alternative to OKRs and SMART goals.
description: Created by Kathy Keating, GEMs (Goal Experiments Measures) is an objective-setting framework that explicitly links goals to the experiments intended to achieve them. It is designed for teams that value a culture of experimentation, allowing goals to evolve based on learning rather than rigid, top-down cascading. GEMs can be easily mapped to OKRs (Goal=Objective, Measures=Key Results) or combined into SMART statements, providing flexibility for teams operating within larger corporate frameworks.

activities.name: Set a goal
activities.description: Identify a relevant and achievable goal that individuals and teams can influence. Focus on the desired future state without defining measures yet.

activities.name: Identify experiments
activities.description: Brainstorm potential experiments that could help the team move closer to the goal.

activities.name: Prioritize ideas
activities.description: Sort experiments based on impact, effort, and confidence using prioritization matrices or voting techniques.

activities.name: Define measures
activities.description: Establish how success will be measured for each experiment and determine how and when the data will be gathered.

activities.name: Keep score
activities.description: Maintain a scorecard and update it weekly or at the end of each sprint to track progress and data assessments.

completion_criteria: The scorecard is updated regularly (weekly or per sprint) and experiments are assessed against defined measures.

metrics.name: Goal Progress
metrics.formula: Current Value - Baseline Value

metrics.name: Experiment Confidence
metrics.formula: Average of Team Confidence Votes

guidelines.name: Get your OKRs out of my GEMs

pitfalls: Failing to update the scorecard regularly
Treating experiments as fixed tasks rather than learning opportunities
Setting goals that are outside the team's ability to influence
benefits: Bridges the gap between high-level objectives and actionable results
Allows for rapid adaptation as the situation changes
Compatible with existing OKR or SMART frameworks

name: Generative AI Cost Equation
objective: Validate viability by calculating cost of AI tooling, adaptation, and supervision compared with manual efforts.
description: A strategic framework created by George Westerman of MIT to determine if automating a specific task with generative AI is economically and practically viable. The equation evaluates viability by comparing the Total AI Costs (Usage + Adaptation + Error Fixing) against the Manual Process Cost.

activities.name: Calculate the Cost of the Manual Process
activities.description: Determine the baseline status quo costs including employee hours, salaries, and overhead associated with the manual workflow.

activities.name: Determine the Cost of Using AI
activities.description: Estimate direct technology expenses such as API usage fees, subscriptions, or infrastructure costs for running models.

activities.name: Estimate the Cost of Adaptation
activities.description: Calculate investment required for fine-tuning, prompt engineering, or specialized expertise like RAG setup to achieve required accuracy.

activities.name: Calculate the Cost of Detecting and Fixing Errors
activities.description: Estimate the cost of human-in-the-loop supervision required to review and correct AI outputs/hallucinations.

activities.name: Assess the Cost of an Error
activities.description: Evaluate the consequences (liability, safety, regulatory) if an AI error is not detected; serves as a Go/No-Go gate.

activities.name: Execute the Comparison
activities.description: Sum the AI-related costs and compare them to the manual cost to make a final implementation decision.

completion_criteria: Total AI Cost (Usage + Adaptation + Supervision) is compared against Manual Cost and a formal Go/No-Go decision is documented by the Sponsor.

metrics.name: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Delta
metrics.formula: Manual Process Cost - (AI Usage + Adaptation + Supervision)

metrics.name: Supervision Time Ratio
metrics.formula: (Human Review Time / Total Task Time) * 100

guidelines.name: Find the AI Approach That Fits the Problem You’re Trying to Solve

pitfalls: Underestimating the time required for human-in-the-loop review
Overlooking 'hidden' costs of prompt engineering and fine-tuning
Ignoring high-impact/low-probability risks (Cost of an Error)
Implementing AI purely based on hype without economic sense
benefits: Determines true economic sense of automation
Prevents spending more on automation than is saved on labor
Identifies hidden technical and human costs
Ensures alignment with organization's risk appetite

name: GitOps
objective: To manage the flow of work from development to production through Git operations, using Git as the single source of truth.
description: GitOps is a pattern where the state of a system is defined in Git and automatically synchronized with the actual environment. It relies on the principle of Everything as Code, meaning deployments, tests, and rollbacks are exclusively controlled through Git operations like commits and pull requests. By removing manual interaction with the platform, GitOps ensures visibility, traceability, and consistency across environments, typically using tools like ArgoCD or Flux to detect and remediate configuration drift.

activities.name: Define team standards
activities.description: Discuss and agree on what the GitOps approach means in practice for the team's specific context.

activities.name: Structure Git repositories
activities.description: Define the structure of Git repositories and determine where to store application code versus configuration and manifest files.

activities.name: Select and configure tools
activities.description: Identify and deploy the right GitOps tooling (e.g., ArgoCD or Flux) to monitor repositories and sync state.

activities.name: Implement CI/CD pipeline
activities.description: Configure a high-level flow where source code changes trigger manifest updates, which the GitOps tool then applies to the environment.

completion_criteria: The environment state matches the state defined in the Git repository without manual intervention, and all changes are traceable through Git history.

metrics.name: Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR)
metrics.formula: Time to rollback to a previous Git commit

metrics.name: Lead Time
metrics.formula: Time from code commit to successful production deployment

guidelines.name: Ubiquitous Journey - an implementation of GitOps
Argo CD - a popular GitOps tool

pitfalls: Making manual changes directly to the platform (bypassing Git)
Configuration drift if the sync tool is disabled or ignored
Overly complex repository structures that confuse developers
benefits: Minimized configuration drift through automated sync
Improved security via single source of truth

name: Group Launch Facilitation
objective: Create alignment with shared purpose, clear work flows, decision-making authority, and effective collaboration for teams of teams.
description: A structured approach to forming and aligning groups (teams of teams) using synchronous and asynchronous facilitation to define context, design workflows, establish behavioral agreements, and streamline communication channels.

activities.name: Define Group Context
activities.description: Map internal/external relationships, clarify strategic intent, identify dependencies/risks, and draft a purpose statement.

activities.name: Design Group Workflow
activities.description: Categorize work types (Planned, Unplanned, Expedite), map workflow stages, define success metrics, and clarify decision-making authority.

activities.name: Establish Group Agreements
activities.description: Co-create a social contract covering values and conflict resolution, map handoffs, and create an improvement backlog.

activities.name: Optimize Meetings
activities.description: Audit existing meetings, eliminate redundancies, and design a streamlined cadence for planning and dependency management.

completion_criteria: The group's context, workflow, and agreements are documented in knowledge management tools and action items from the session are assigned.

metrics.name: Customer Satisfaction
metrics.formula: Average of stakeholder survey results

metrics.name: Flow Efficiency
metrics.formula: (Active Work Time / Total Lead Time) * 100

pitfalls: Process bloat
Organizational entropy
Friction in handoffs
Meeting redundancy
Coordination overhead

name: GROW Model for 1-2-1 Coaching
objective: To unlock people's potential to maximise their performance and facilitate personal and professional development through structured coaching interactions.
description: Professional coaching is an ongoing relationship that helps people produce extraordinary results. GROW is a framework created and evolved by Sir John Whitmore for structuring coaching conversations to solve problems, set goals, and improve performance.

activities.name: Goals
activities.description: Identify objectives, desires, or aspirations the coachee wants to achieve.

activities.name: Reality
activities.description: Examine the current situation, challenges, roadblocks, and efforts taken so far.

activities.name: Options
activities.description: Explore opportunities, possibilities, available resources, and influencers.

activities.name: Way Forward
activities.description: Define specific actions, commitment, accountability, and the will to proceed.

completion_criteria: The coaching session is completed within the allocated 30-60 minute timebox with a defined set of actions and commitment from the coachee.

metrics.name: Session Duration
metrics.formula: End Time - Start Time

metrics.name: Action Item Count
metrics.formula: Sum of defined actions

pitfalls: Lack of psychological safety
Failing to listen actively
Making judgements instead of asking questions

name: Guerilla Testing
objective: A low-cost, lean, and agile method of quickly capturing user feedback and validating hypotheses through short, task-focused sessions with participants approached in public environments.
description: Guerrilla testing is a technique for collecting usability data by approaching individuals in public environments—such as coffee shops or office lobbies—who match a specific demographic. It avoids the time and expense of formal recruitment, focusing on rapid qualitative feedback and observation of how users interact with a prototype or application flow.

activities.name: Preparations
activities.description: Agree on learning outcomes, create easy-to-follow scenarios/tasks, develop a prototype (low-fidelity to interactive), and define the target participant demographics.

activities.name: Logistics
activities.description: Identify a location relevant to the target demographic, obtain venue permission, prepare recording equipment, and designate a note-taker/observer.

activities.name: Approach and Screen
activities.description: Introduce yourself to potential participants and verify they meet the required demographic criteria (e.g., tech knowledge, device usage).

activities.name: Explain Intent
activities.description: Clarify that the purpose is to test the application flow rather than the user, and explain how their feedback will improve the product.

activities.name: Obtain Consent
activities.description: Ask permission to film or photograph the session for observation and clarify if the assets can be used internally or externally.

activities.name: Explain Think Out Loud
activities.description: Instruct the participant to vocalize their thoughts, expectations, and goals throughout the interaction.

activities.name: Observe and Inquire
activities.description: Watch the user interact with the prototype. Ask non-leading questions when they appear stuck to understand their mental model.

activities.name: Capture and Share Insights
activities.description: Record findings using a rating scale (1-Completed, 2-Some problems, 3-Not completed) and qualitative notes to share with the wider team.

activities.name: Conclude
activities.description: Thank the participant for their time and provide any predetermined incentives.

completion_criteria: The usability session is completed, data is recorded, and insights are shared with the product team and stakeholders.

metrics.name: Task Success Rate
metrics.formula: 1 (Completed), 2 (Partial), 3 (Failure)

guidelines.name: What is guerrilla testing and how do you use it?
Guerrilla Usability Testing: How To Introduce It In Your Next UX Project

pitfalls: Leading the user toward a specific action
Failing to get permission from the venue management
Not having a second person to take notes
Testing the person's intelligence instead of the application's usability
benefits: Accessible to all team members without research expertise
Improves stakeholder understanding of user needs
Highly flexible at any development stage

name: Hello, World! networking event
objective: To facilitate peer-to-peer networking and accelerate the onboarding process by helping individuals grow their internal networks faster.
description: A 'Hello, World!' event is a simple peer-to-peer networking activity designed to help individuals, particularly new hires, meet others within an organization. It serves as a practical measure of an organization's culture and openness; the ease of initiating such an event indicates a more open, network-based environment.

activities.name: Identification
activities.description: Notice a person in a meeting or discussion who is talking about interesting or important topics.

activities.name: Instant Invitation
activities.description: Without over-analyzing or creating a 'to-meet' list, send an invitation for a 'Hello World!' meeting to have a small talk or discuss a specific subject.

activities.name: Execution
activities.description: Conduct a brief 20-30 minute session to get to know the peer and exchange information.

activities.name: Continuous Expansion
activities.description: Repeat the process whenever a new potential contact is identified to continuously grow the network.

completion_criteria: The meeting is held within the 20-30 minute timebox and the network is expanded.

metrics.name: Networking Frequency
metrics.formula: Number of Hello World invitations sent per month

pitfalls: Over-analyzing the pros and cons of reaching out
Creating a To-Meet-List instead of sending the invitation immediately
Stopping the practice once a basic network is established

name: Heuristic Evaluation
objective: Provide expert review of designs using standardized heuristics to generate concrete, actionable feedback.
description: Heuristic evaluation is a method for reviewing designs based on ten basic principles. Originally coined by Jakob Nielsen, it involves usability experts inspecting a user interface against established usability principles (the heuristics) to identify potential issues and suggest improvements. It serves as an effective companion to usability testing.

activities.name: Establish heuristics
activities.description: Identify an appropriate list of heuristics using Nielsen and Molich's 10 heuristics or Shneiderman’s 8 golden rules as a baseline, combined with specific domain research.

activities.name: Select evaluators
activities.description: Choose usability experts with domain expertise in the relevant industry. Evaluators should not be the end users.

activities.name: Brief evaluators
activities.description: Provide a standardized briefing to all evaluators to ensure they receive the same instructions and focus tasks without bias.

activities.name: First evaluation phase
activities.description: Evaluators use the product freely to gain a feel for interaction methods and scope, identifying specific elements for further evaluation.

activities.name: Second evaluation phase
activities.description: Evaluators perform a second run-through, applying chosen heuristics to the specific elements identified in the first phase.

activities.name: Record problems
activities.description: Evaluators record specific, detailed problems as they encounter them during tasks.

activities.name: Debriefing session
activities.description: Evaluators collaborate to collate findings, establish a complete list of problems, and suggest solutions based on the heuristics.

completion_criteria: Completion of the debriefing session with a collated list of usability problems and suggested solutions mapped to heuristics.

metrics.name: Usability Issues Identified
metrics.formula: Total count of distinct problems recorded

metrics.name: Evaluation Duration
metrics.formula: End Time - Start Time

guidelines.name: How to Conduct a Heuristic Evaluation

pitfalls: Using end users as evaluators instead of usability experts
Non-standardized briefing leading to evaluator bias
Recording vague or non-specific problems
benefits: Provides concrete and actionable design feedback
Faster than full usability testing for identifying common issues

name: How Might We _____ ?
objective: Turning insights into questions that spur creative solutioning
description: HMW is an analytical technique relying on actionable questions asked from a particular point of view on a specific problem. They are used to spur creative problem solving and produce insights, which lead to significant innovation. It is frequently used as part of Design Thinking sessions to break group think and innovative solutions.

activities.name: Formulate Challenge
activities.description: Develop a challenge or problem statement that does not imply a solution and is not too broad.

activities.name: Define Point of View
activities.description: Identify key actors and apply empathy to establish a specific point of view.

activities.name: Generate HMW Questions
activities.description: Ask multiple questions to look at the challenge through different perspectives like amplifying the good, removing the bad, or questioning assumptions.

completion_criteria: A defined challenge statement and a set of relevant questions starting with 'How Might We...?' have been produced.

metrics.name: Ideation Velocity
metrics.formula: Total HMW questions / Time taken

metrics.name: Session Duration
metrics.formula: End time - Start time

guidelines.name: The Secret Phrase Top Innovators Use
Stanford D School: HMW Intro and Guidance For Use

pitfalls: Challenge statement implies a solution
Challenge is too broad (boiling the ocean)
Falling into group think

name: Hopes and Fears
objective: Reveal your team's expectations and doubts before starting a new journey
description: Hopes and Fears is a practice used to discover the expectations, desires, and concerns of team members and stakeholders. It aims to identify positive aspirations about what can be accomplished and potential challenges or apprehensions associated with the project. By putting everything in the open, teams can proactively address challenges, align on outcomes, and cultivate team spirit.

activities.name: Ensure Safety
activities.description: Create a psychologically safe environment where participants feel comfortable sharing and discussing their honest thoughts.

activities.name: Prepare the Board
activities.description: Divide a physical or virtual whiteboard into two distinct areas labeled Hopes and Fears.

activities.name: Write Sticky Notes
activities.description: Have every participant write their hopes or fears on individual sticky notes, with one hope or fear per note.

activities.name: Organize Notes
activities.description: Place the sticky notes into the appropriate Hopes or Fears areas on the board.

activities.name: Playback and Discussion
activities.description: Discuss the notes as a group to identify emerging and common themes among the participants.

activities.name: Track and Update
activities.description: Keep the board visible and refer back to it frequently to celebrate realized hopes and address or remove fears as they evolve.

completion_criteria: The board is created, common themes are identified and discussed, and the artifact is posted in a visible location for tracking.

metrics.name: Realized Hopes
metrics.formula: Total number of hopes achieved

metrics.name: Resolved Fears
metrics.formula: Total number of fears mitigated or removed

guidelines.name: Hopes and Fears - Easy Retro Inception Template
Hopes and Fears Workshop Tactic - Pip Deck

pitfalls: Lack of psychological safety preventing honest input
Fears persist for too long without being addressed
Forgetting to refer back to the board after the initial session
benefits: Focused and aligned approach to outcomes
Cultivates team spirit and mutual understanding

name: I Have a Dream
objective: Build deeper team relationships and get to know each other beyond professional roles.
description: A team building or icebreaker activity where members share a personal dream and an action taken toward it, fostering deeper connections and celebrating individual aspirations. It was inspired by Martin Luther King Jr. Day and is intended for teams that already know each other's names and basic roles.

activities.name: Explain Context
activities.description: Explain to the team that this practice was created so that people can get to know each other in a deeper way beyond just names and roles.

activities.name: Write Dreams
activities.description: Ask every team member to write two things on a post-it: their dream starting with 'I Have a Dream that...' and something they have already done to achieve it.

activities.name: Share and Explain
activities.description: After everyone finishes writing, each team member takes turns explaining their dream and actions to the rest of the group.

completion_criteria: Every participating team member has shared their dream and the 10-20 minute timebox is respected.

metrics.name: Participation Rate
metrics.formula: (Number of team members sharing / Total number of team members) * 100

guidelines.name: Wikipedia - Martin Luther King Jr. Day

pitfalls: Using this as a first-time icebreaker before participants know each other's names.

name: Ice Breakers
objective: Breaking barriers and connecting people
description: A simple and effective practice to break the barriers in a human group to create a safe starting point for a workshop or session. This practice focuses on building good relationships between attendees using enjoyable topics and activities.

activities.name: Initial Introduction
activities.description: Attendees introduce themselves by sharing their name and role before the main Ice Breaker activity starts.

activities.name: Context Adaptation
activities.description: The facilitator chooses an ice breaker activity that best fits the audience's context and background.

activities.name: Ice Breaker Execution
activities.description: Perform the chosen activity, such as Agile Faces, Spirit Animals, Our Superpowers, or World Map, to foster bonding.

completion_criteria: Participants have successfully introduced themselves and engaged in the collective activity, reducing initial social barriers.

metrics.name: Participant Engagement
metrics.formula: Number of participants active / total attendees

pitfalls: Choosing an activity that does not match the audience context
Overextending the timebox allotted for the ice breaker
benefits: Creates a safe starting point for workshops
Helps people bond quickly towards a common goal
Effective for groups with different backgrounds or organizations

name: Idea Cyclone
objective: Generate a massive volume of ideas in a structured, time-bound manner.
description: Idea Cyclone is a high-energy, facilitator-led brainstorming methodology that moves participants through three distinct, progressively challenging rounds of idea generation. Unlike traditional brainstorming, it emphasizes a rapid, out-loud, collaborative process within small teams, where ideas are immediately shared to inspire others and create a cyclone of creativity.

activities.name: Setup & Ground Rules
activities.description: Form teams of 4-5, explain the problem challenge, and set core principles: defer judgment, build on others' ideas, be brief, and encourage disruption.

activities.name: Phase 1: The Initial Burst
activities.description: Engage in collective out-loud idea generation for 5-7 minutes aiming for 8-10 ideas per person, post them on the board, and share key themes.

activities.name: Phase 2: The Deepening Wave
activities.description: Generate a new wave of 4-5 completely new concepts per person that are not variations of what is already on the board.

activities.name: Phase 3: The Unexpected Twist
activities.description: Push boundaries by thinking of ideas that break current rules or project constraints, generating 2-3 disruptive ideas per person.

activities.name: Phase 4: Convergence & Prioritization
activities.description: Group similar ideas into thematic clusters and use dot voting to identify and prioritize the winning ideas.

completion_criteria: The session is complete when the winning ideas have been identified through dot voting and the timebox has been respected.

metrics.name: Idea Volume
metrics.formula: Sum of all ideas generated across all rounds

metrics.name: Idea Diversity
metrics.formula: Number of unique thematic clusters identified

pitfalls: Judging ideas too early in the process
Failing to build on others' contributions
Dominant participants suppressing quieter voices
Ignoring the timebox constraints

name: Independent Service Heuristics (ISH)
objective: Spark collaborative conversations to identify software boundaries that facilitate fast flow.
description: Independent Service Heuristics (ISH) is a set of 10 questions used to find where to set team and software boundaries that will enable Fast Flow of value. It is designed to stimulate conversation and provide a frame for thinking about independent domains, services, or value streams, helping teams avoid analysis paralysis and create alignment across organizational boundaries.

activities.name: Identify Candidates
activities.description: Ask the team to identify business aspects, tasks, software applications, and customer journeys that could potentially become independent domains or services.

activities.name: Refine and Cluster
activities.description: Remove duplicate ideas and cluster similar concepts to create a shortlist of candidate streams.

activities.name: Evaluate Heuristics
activities.description: Apply the 10 ISH checklist items (Sense-check, Brand, Revenue, Cost tracking, Data, User Personas, Teams, Dependencies, Impact, and Product Decisions) to each candidate.

activities.name: Select Boundaries
activities.description: Determine the viability of each stream based on the number of 'yes' or 'maybe' answers to identify suitable software and team boundaries.

completion_criteria: All identified candidate streams have been evaluated against the 10 Independent Service Heuristics.

metrics.name: Heuristic Viability Score
metrics.formula: Number of Yes or Maybe answers across the 10 heuristics per candidate stream

guidelines.name: Finding good stream boundaries with Independent Service Heuristics

pitfalls: Analysis paralysis during the discovery phase
Treating the heuristics as a perfect catch-all tool rather than a conversation starter
benefits: Helps identify viable independent product roadmaps

name: InnerSource
objective: Re-use code, collaborate, and use open practices inside your organisation.
description: InnerSource is an approach to software development inside an organisation that is inspired by the success of the Open Source Software movement. It aims to promote collaboration and code-reuse by opening organisational silos and facilitating the flow of useful content for those who need it.

activities.name: Start as an experiment
activities.description: Begin implementing InnerSource patterns on a small scale to test viability and build initial momentum.

activities.name: Agree to and publish standards
activities.description: Define and make available base documentation and coding standards to ensure consistency across the organization.

activities.name: Use asynchronous communication tools
activities.description: Implement and use communication tooling that supports collaboration across different teams and time zones.

activities.name: Grant access to source code repository
activities.description: Ensure the issue tracker and source code are accessible to members outside the primary development team.

activities.name: Make documentation available
activities.description: Establish an InnerSource portal or central repository where documentation is easily searchable and accessible.

activities.name: Automate testing
activities.description: Implement automated testing to maintain quality and provide fast feedback to contributors.

completion_criteria: Source code is accessible to the organization, contribution standards are published, and a collaborative environment is established.

guidelines.name: Adopting InnerSource - Danese Cooper & Klaas-Jan Stol
InnerSource, understand the benefits in 5 minutes
Does adopting inner source increase job satisfaction? - Paper

name: Inter-Team Agreement
objective: Enhance collaboration and value delivery across teams by designing, analyzing, and improving interactions to bridge silos and streamline workflows.
description: A structured framework to design, analyze, and improve interactions between teams. It focuses on clarifying dependencies, reducing bottlenecks like hidden queues or misaligned priorities, and creating actionable agreements for smoother collaboration. It is particularly effective for launching new teams, addressing recurring conflicts, or refreshing operational cadences in remote/hybrid environments.

activities.name: Preparation
activities.description: Gather context from artifacts like Team Launch Canvases and select 1–3 representatives per team such as leaders or domain experts.

activities.name: Define Collective Purpose
activities.description: Draft a purpose statement following the format: We interact when [trigger] to [action] so that [benefit].

activities.name: Reflect on Interactions
activities.description: Conduct a retrospective based on Norm Kerth’s Prime Directive to identify what works, what doesn't, and future risks.

activities.name: Design Team Interfaces
activities.description: Brainstorm ways to eliminate (automate), simplify (communication channels), or optimize (cadences) interactions, then vote on the top 3 actions.

activities.name: Async Facilitation
activities.description: Optional step to pre-capture data via surveys or documentation to identify pain points and constraints before live sessions.

activities.name: Follow-Up
activities.description: Track actions using a visible board, update the team knowledge base for onboarding, and schedule quarterly reflection sessions.

completion_criteria: Completion of the facilitation session, documentation of top 3 action items with owners, and scheduling of the next quarterly review.

metrics.name: Inter-team Delay Reduction
metrics.formula: Reduction in wait time measured at handoff points between teams.

metrics.name: Interaction Efficiency
metrics.formula: Number of automated or self-service handoffs created.

pitfalls: Over-analysis during async phases
Hidden queues between teams
Misaligned priorities
Delayed approvals
Ignoring the Prime Directive during retrospectives

name: Is – Is not – Does – Does not
objective: Provide a structured approach for clearly understanding and defining a problem, object, concept, or situation.
description: This method helps in breaking down complex or ambiguous scenarios into more manageable and distinct components by identifying what a subject is, is not, does, and does not do. It is specifically designed to set clear boundaries, reduce scope creep, and ensure stakeholders share a unified understanding of the subject's characteristics and functions.

activities.name: Preparation
activities.description: Define the session objective, select a diverse group of participants with different perspectives, and set up a physical or virtual collaborative space.

activities.name: Introduction
activities.description: Explain the technique and its importance to the participants, and set ground rules to encourage openness and a non-judgmental environment.

activities.name: Define 'Is'
activities.description: Prompt the team to describe the characteristics, attributes, and qualities that the subject definitely possesses.

activities.name: Define 'Is Not'
activities.description: Guide the conversation to clarify what the subject is explicitly not, helping to set boundaries and avoid confusion with similar entities.

activities.name: Define 'Does'
activities.description: Identify and record the actions, functions, behaviors, or roles that the subject performs.

activities.name: Define 'Does Not'
activities.description: Discuss the limitations and non-functions of the subject to set realistic expectations and identify constraints.

activities.name: Synthesis and Action Plan
activities.description: Compile the points into a coherent document, review it with the team, and develop an action plan based on the insights gained.

completion_criteria: The team has reached a consensus on all four quadrants, the session results are synthesized into a document, and an action plan is established.

metrics.name: Time to Consensus
metrics.formula: Time at end of Does Not - Time at start of Is

guidelines.name: The product Is – Is not – Does – Does not - MartinFowler.com

pitfalls: Scope creep during the discussion
Dominant voices suppressing quieter team members
Ambiguity between categories leading to overlap
Lack of diverse perspectives in the participant group
benefits: Reduces scope creep by setting clear boundaries
Improves problem-solving through root cause identification
Enhances collaboration by providing a common language

name: Jobs to be Done
objective: Focus on understanding the fundamental needs and motivations that lead consumers to use a particular product or service.
description: Jobs to be Done (JTBD) is an approach in product and service development that focuses on understanding the fundamental needs that motivate consumers to purchase or use a particular product. Instead of focusing on the features of the product itself, the JTBD approach concentrates on what customers are trying to accomplish when they "hire" a product or service to perform a specific task in their lives.

activities.name: Identify Target Customers
activities.description: Define the specific customer segment or market you want to focus on, involving demographic or psychographic factors.

activities.name: Define the Job
activities.description: Identify the fundamental task or goal that customers are trying to achieve within the chosen market segment.

activities.name: Conduct Customer Interviews
activities.description: Engage with target customers through interviews or surveys to uncover challenges, frustrations, and the context of the job.

activities.name: Identify Job Steps and Outcomes
activities.description: Break down the job into actions and identify the desired outcomes or goals customers aim to achieve by completing the job.

activities.name: Analyze Customer Data
activities.description: Analyze data gathered from interviews to identify patterns, themes, and insights related to the jobs and associated needs.

activities.name: Develop Customer Personas
activities.description: Create fictional characters representing the characteristics, goals, and challenges of your target customers.

activities.name: Generate Solutions
activities.description: Brainstorm potential solutions or product concepts that address the identified jobs and alleviate customer pain points.

activities.name: Prototype and Test
activities.description: Develop prototypes or minimum viable products (MVPs) and test them with real customers to gather feedback and validate assumptions.

activities.name: Iterate and Refine
activities.description: Continuously iterate on the product or service based on customer feedback and market insights to improve usability.

activities.name: Launch and Monitor
activities.description: Launch the product in the market and gather data on customer usage, satisfaction, and outcomes to identify opportunities for optimization.

completion_criteria: The product or service is launched in the market and performance is monitored through gathered usage, satisfaction, and outcome data.

metrics.name: Customer Satisfaction
metrics.formula: Average rating from customer satisfaction surveys

metrics.name: Customer Usage
