metrics.formula: Total number of users performing the specific job with the product

guidelines.name: How To Use Jobs To Be Done Framework

name: Kanban Priorities
objective: Prioritise tasks on the board before daily standup to facilitate better communication and team work
description: A practice focused on preparing the Kanban board's task priority in the evening before the next day's standup. This helps diverse teams, including introverted members, understand which 'hot topics' should be addressed first, ensuring tasks are picked up in the most efficient order.

activities.name: Evening Review
activities.description: The Product Owner reviews the Kanban board in the evening to evaluate what has been completed and identify shifting priorities.

activities.name: Priority Update
activities.description: The list of open tasks is updated daily in the evening to reflect the correct order of high-priority 'hot topics' on the board.

activities.name: Team Alignment
activities.description: In the morning, team members review the updated priorities to stay informed of the latest changes and select tasks according to the high-priority order.

completion_criteria: The Kanban board is ordered by priority before the morning standup, and team members select the highest priority tasks first.

metrics.name: Task Priority Adherence
metrics.formula: (Tasks picked in priority order / Total tasks started) * 100

pitfalls: Introverted team members being unsure of which task to pick up
Team picking up tasks in the wrong order
benefits: Ensures work is done in the best possible order

name: Kano Model
objective: Easily visualize and explain the impact and priority of different features based on customer satisfaction.
description: The Kano model is a technique used in product development and customer satisfaction assessment to categorize product features into five distinct categories: Must-be, One-dimensional, Attractive, Indifferent, and Reverse. It helps teams decide which features to prioritize to ensure basic needs are met while also providing 'delighters' that make a product stand out.

activities.name: Feature Identification
activities.description: Generate a list of ideas, hypotheses, or features for the product.

activities.name: Categorization
activities.description: Classify each feature into one of the five Kano categories: Must-be (Basic Needs), One-dimensional (Satisfiers), Attractive (Delighters), Indifferent (Neutrals), or Reverse.

activities.name: Mandatory Selection
activities.description: Ensure all Must-have features are included in the requirements as they are taken for granted by customers.

activities.name: Balance Performance and Delighters
activities.description: Select a satisfactory level of One-dimensional features and consider adding a Delighter if the market is competitive.

activities.name: Plotting and Discussion
activities.description: Use the Kano Matrix to plot features and facilitate a team discussion to align on the category and priority of each feature.

completion_criteria: All proposed features are categorized on a Kano Matrix and a prioritized backlog is established based on these categories.

pitfalls: Over-optimizing Attractive features too early
Failing to include all Must-be features
Spending too much time on Indifferent features
benefits: Easy visualization of impact and priority
Simple and easy to understand for the whole team

name: Knowledge Sharing
objective: Share what we know, and what we wish to learn, and prioritize among possible knowledge sharing topics.
description: This is a tool for a team to collect topics: What we know, what we want to learn, and then prioritize among possible knowledge sharing topics. This is a practical way to quickly gather knowledge among the team and gauge what the team knows and where are the knowledge gaps other team members can fill. Done regularly, this will help the team to gain knowledge and create an open culture.

activities.name: Initialize Workspace
activities.description: Participants select a column on the board and fill in their name at the top.

activities.name: List Teaching Topics
activities.description: At the top section of their space, participants fill in sticky notes for topics they wish to share, can teach, or recently learned about.

activities.name: List Learning Interests
activities.description: At the bottom section of their space, participants fill in topics they are interested in learning more about.

activities.name: Match Knowledge
activities.description: Participants review the learning wish lists; if a participant can share on a subject, they put their name and emoji on that sticky note.

activities.name: Consolidate Topics
activities.description: Review the requested knowledge gap topics and group together similar topics or stickers.

activities.name: Prioritize Topics
activities.description: Use dot voting to decide the next topic. Each participant gets four dots to vote on possible next topics to be shared or learned.

activities.name: Manage Agenda
activities.description: Pull the highest-voted topic into the 'next area'. Move items covered in previous sessions to 'done' and remove items of no interest to the 'discard area'.

completion_criteria: A prioritized topic is identified and moved into the 'next area' for the upcoming session.

metrics.name: Knowledge Gap Coverage
metrics.formula: Topics with assigned experts / Total requested learning topics

metrics.name: Sharing Frequency
metrics.formula: Number of topics moved to the Done area per month

pitfalls: Topics are too broad to be shared in a short session
Lack of follow-up on prioritized items

name: Leaders Eat Last
objective: Building Trust Between Leaders and Teams
description: Based on the United States Marine Corps' practice of placing the needs of subordinates before your own. This practice fosters an atmosphere where the team trusts one another and feels like they belong by ensuring leaders take care of their teammates and ensure their success, assessing physical and mental wellbeing under pressure.

activities.name: Mindset Shift
activities.description: Transition from a mindset of self-preservation to one of team preservation, prioritizing the team's needs over personal convenience.

activities.name: Small Gestures
activities.description: Develop habits like showing up five minutes early, offering drinks/support, or volunteering for extra work.

activities.name: Support and Help
activities.description: Actively ask the team what can be done to support their progress and success.

activities.name: Credit and Accountability
activities.description: Give the team credit for successes and assume personal responsibility when the team fails.

completion_criteria: A culture of trust and belonging is established where team members feel safe and supported by leadership.

metrics.name: Team Trust Score
metrics.formula: Average of periodic anonymous team trust surveys

guidelines.name: Leaders Eat Last Book by Simon Sinek

pitfalls: Inconsistency when under stress
Self-preservation during failures
Performing the actions as a checklist rather than a genuine shift in mindset
benefits: Reduces internal competition and feelings of threat

name: Leadership Launch
objective: Align leadership teams, shift focus to strategic leadership, and establish collaborative frameworks for decision-making, communication, and accountability.
description: A structured facilitation process designed for leadership teams to align priorities, clarify roles, and delegate effectively. The practice combines synchronous workshops and asynchronous activities to create a living document that evolves with the team, emphasizing intentional collaboration and vulnerability.

activities.name: Async Setup
activities.description: Prepare digital boards (e.g., Miro) with predefined activities and share pre-work like SCARF assessments or delegation policies.

activities.name: Team Briefing
activities.description: Conduct a 15-minute recorded briefing to communicate goals and pre-work requirements to the team.

activities.name: Lifting Our Eyes
activities.description: A 90-minute live session discussing time allocation across strategic direction, building systems, improving systems, and operational tasks.

activities.name: Decision Design
activities.description: Map decisions to four tiers (Executive, Leader, Team, Individual) and define necessary support frameworks and escalation policies.

activities.name: Measures Definition
activities.description: Agree on and stress-test metrics for continuous improvement across customer, group, and team levels.

activities.name: SCARF Model Collaboration
activities.description: Rank dimensions of Status, Certainty, Autonomy, Relatedness, and Fairness to understand and align team motivations.

activities.name: Leadership Behaviors
activities.description: Establish team norms regarding vibe, difficulty, growth, and support to model desired organizational behaviors.

activities.name: Communication Channels & Scheduling
activities.description: Align on communication tools, response protocols, and schedule recurring strategy reviews or events.

activities.name: Documentation & Review
activities.description: Convert outputs into a living document and schedule quarterly reviews to update agreements and metrics.

completion_criteria: Outputs are converted into a living document accessible via a knowledge platform, follow-up actions are assigned, and the first quarterly review is scheduled.

metrics.name: Time Distribution Alignment
metrics.formula: Actual Strategic Time / Target Strategic Time

metrics.name: Meeting Reduction
metrics.formula: Baseline Meeting Hours - Post-Launch Meeting Hours

pitfalls: Leadership fatigue
Excessive time spent in operational 'weeds'
Lack of transparency between leaders
Misuse or stress-testing failure of metrics
Misalignment during restructuring
benefits: Stronger alignment on roles and priorities

name: Leadership Map
objective: Create actionable improvement backlogs and foster leadership development through dialogue and problem-solving.
description: The Leadership Map is a facilitation framework based on Ken Wilber's Integral Theory that helps leadership teams navigate organizational change through four interconnected quadrants. The practice explores Leading Our Teams, Leading Our Systems, Leading Together, and Leading Ourselves, combining assessment data and structured dialogue to create prioritized action plans for remote and hybrid environments.

activities.name: Phase 1: Insight Gathering
activities.description: Gather insights through remote assessments, engagement surveys, performance data, and stakeholder interviews to create a thematic summary for the synchronous session.

activities.name: Phase 2: Synchronous Facilitation
activities.description: Facilitate a 90-minute minimum session exploring the four quadrants (Teams, Systems, Together, Ourselves) using structured prompts, affinity mapping, and timeboxing.

activities.name: Phase 3: Improvement System Launch
activities.description: Synthesize insights into actionable themes, draft interventions, and prioritize them using effort-impact matrices to create a published improvement backlog.

completion_criteria: A published improvement backlog is created, baseline metrics are captured, and a reassessment schedule is set for every 3-6 months.

metrics.name: Reassessment Frequency
metrics.formula: Current Date - Last Assessment Date

pitfalls: Over-indexing on familiar areas while neglecting uncomfortable topics
Top-down mandates instead of collaborative ownership
Addressing symptoms rather than root causes
Failing to capture baseline metrics for improvement
benefits: Prevents neglect of invisible organizational elements like psychological safety
Translates theoretical insights into measurable improvements

name: Lean Canvas
objective: A 1-page business plan template to help you focus on problems, solutions, key metrics and competitive advantages.
description: A Lean Canvas is a one-page document that captures key information about your future product. Frequently used by Entrepreneurs and Lean Startups, it aims to shorten the product development lifecycle and rapidly discover if a product idea is viable. It breaks down a business model into nine distinct sections to structure and validate ideas quickly.

activities.name: Problem Identification
activities.description: Identify the core problems for the future customer and why these problems exist for them.

activities.name: Target Customer Definition
activities.description: Specify early adopters in terms of demographics, situation, and interests.

activities.name: Unique Value Proposition (UVP)
activities.description: Define what makes the product or service special to the customer and the specific benefits it provides.

activities.name: Solution Mapping
activities.description: Focus on key features of the product or service, ensuring each aligns with one of the identified problems.

activities.name: Channel Selection
activities.description: Plan how to promote the product, such as through word of mouth, social media, or other communication paths.

activities.name: Revenue Streams
activities.description: Determine the monetization strategy, for example using a freemium model.

activities.name: Cost Structure
activities.description: Identify occurring costs like store fees or payment processing charges.

activities.name: Key Metrics
activities.description: Define how success will be measured, such as the number of downloads or paid subscribers.

activities.name: Unfair Advantage
activities.description: Identify what makes the product unique and difficult for competitors to copy or buy.

completion_criteria: The canvas is fully populated with every box filled using concise bullet points and is understandable to a reader within a few minutes.

metrics.name: Success Validation
metrics.formula: Number of downloads or subscribers vs target

guidelines.name: Lean UX canvas by Jeff Gothelf

pitfalls: Writing long sentences instead of concise bullet points
Leaving boxes empty due to uncertainty
Not focusing on the early adopter demographic
benefits: Helps identify potential customers and revenue models

name: Lean Inception
objective: Align people and build the right product via the MVP
description: Lean inception is the effective combination of Design Thinking and Lean StartUp to decide the Minimum Viable Product (MVP). It is a collaborative workshop that will help a group of people — typically an agile team, a squad, or a product team — understand, align and plan the building of the lean product.

activities.name: Collaborative Discovery
activities.description: Engage business, UX, and technical team members in a series of alignment activities over five days.

activities.name: MVP Identification
activities.description: Determine the Minimum Viable Product by deciding on the hypothesis to be tested and goals to be reached.

activities.name: Relationship Building
activities.description: Foster team relationships from the first minute of the workshop to improve long-term collaboration.

completion_criteria: The workshop concludes when the team has a validated definition of the MVP and alignment on the construction plan.

guidelines.name: How to prepare for a Lean Inception

pitfalls: Lack of cross-functional representation
Skipping activities that foster team alignment

name: Lean UX Workshop
objective: Solve real problems for real users through interviewing, prototyping, and refining using Lean UX principles.
description: In a Lean UX Workshop, participants practice interviewing and learning about a user. Then, they prototype and refine a product that solves a real problem faced by that person. It builds a culture of user-centered design and lean UX, serving as a hands-on activity or icebreaker.

activities.name: Introduction
activities.description: Introduce Lean UX and user-centered design via demonstration, video, or experience sharing (10-15 min).

activities.name: Team Formation
activities.description: Self-organize into groups of three and assign roles: User, Designer, and Developer.

activities.name: User Interview
activities.description: The designer interviews the user about their morning routine, using open-ended questions to identify a problem (3 min).

activities.name: Prototyping
activities.description: The developer and designer prototype a solution to the identified problem using Lego bricks (7 min).

activities.name: Feedback Session
activities.description: Present the solution to the user, allowing them to try the product and provide feedback (3 min).

activities.name: Refinement
activities.description: Refine the Lego solution based on feedback and gather additional insights (7 min).

activities.name: Final Presentations
activities.description: Two or three teams present their final solutions to the wider group (5 min).

completion_criteria: Refined Lego solution presented and user feedback gathered within the timebox.

metrics.name: User Satisfaction Rating
metrics.formula: Average of individual user ratings

pitfalls: Asking closed yes/no questions instead of open-ended ones
Participants treating feedback ratings like school grades
benefits: Builds a culture of user-centered design
Hands-on learning of Lean UX principles

name: Lean Value Tree
objective: Align people and initiatives across all levels of the business with a company's strategy and vision.
description: The Lean Value Tree (LVT) is a conceptual tool to facilitate, share, and drive a company's strategy and vision across all levels and teams of an organization. It functions as an enhancement to Lean Portfolio Management, encouraging collaboration and value-based delivery by guiding decisions through a visual hierarchy of vision, goals, bets, and initiatives.

activities.name: Establish Vision
activities.description: Define the overarching company vision that sets the direction for the entire organization.

activities.name: Define Strategic Goals
activities.description: Break down the vision into ambitious, high-level strategic goals focused on outcomes rather than specific solutions.

activities.name: Identify Bets
activities.description: Associate each goal with strategic bets, which are value hypotheses the organization believes will help achieve the goals.

activities.name: Create Initiatives
activities.description: Define specific initiatives as smaller hypotheses that can be tested and measured to validate the associated bets.

activities.name: Continuous Review
activities.description: The Lean Portfolio Steering Committee regularly reviews the tree to remove, incorporate, or correct initiatives, bets, and goals based on performance.

completion_criteria: The Lean Value Tree is visible throughout the organization and is reviewed regularly by the Lean Portfolio Steering Committee to guide resource allocation.

metrics.name: Hypothesis Validation Rate
metrics.formula: Validated Bets / Total Bets

metrics.name: Strategic Focus
metrics.formula: Strategic Initiatives / Total Portfolio Items

pitfalls: Treating initiatives as fixed-deadline projects rather than evolving hypotheses
Excluding Business As Usual (BAU) initiatives in large organizations which can cause disharmony
Failing to review the tree regularly resulting in outdated strategy
benefits: Avoids wasting time and budget on initiatives that don't align with corporate vision
Promotes a VUCA-aware mindset through the use of bets
Creates consistency in the approach to organization-wide initiatives
Provides a visual tool for digital transformation collaboration

name: Lego Labs
objective: Experiment with processes for rapidly developing digital products through building a Lego Colony on Mars.
description: Lego Labs is a workshop designed for groups to experiment with software processes in a pressure-free environment. It uses Legos as a medium to teach human-centered design, lean product development, and agile delivery. Participants go from discovery to building prototypes, helping gain buy-in for new ways of working and fostering a culture of experimentation.

activities.name: Workshop Setup
activities.description: Assign facilitators, define participant groups (teams and users/colonists), schedule the session, and prepare materials including Legos, Mars maps, and handouts.

activities.name: Intro & Scenario
activities.description: Introduce the Martian colonization scenario, set expectations, and conduct a Social Contract exercise to build trust among participants.

activities.name: Discovery to Roadmap
activities.description: Conduct qualitative research with 'Colonists' to understand challenges, create an Empathy Map, and brainstorm Epics for the Product Roadmap.

activities.name: Teams to Ideation
activities.description: Form cross-functional teams (Product Manager, Designer, Engineer) and brainstorm specific Features for the chosen build areas.

activities.name: Sprint 1 Planning
activities.description: Prioritize features based on User Value and set up a Kanban board to manage the backlog of user stories.

activities.name: Sprint 1 Delivery
activities.description: Iteratively build Lego prototypes based on the backlog using Build-Measure-Learn cycles and deploy them to the Mars Map.

activities.name: Feedback & Options
activities.description: Collect feedback from Colonists and Product Owners on the prototypes and identify actionable insights for the next sprint.

activities.name: Sprint 2 Planning & Delivery
activities.description: Plan iterations based on feedback and any incomplete work from the previous sprint, then continue building.

activities.name: Reflect & Admire
activities.description: Conduct a retrospective to reflect on process effectiveness and celebrate the completed Martian colony.

completion_criteria: Completion of the retrospective and placement of feature prototypes on the Mars colony map within the specified timebox.

guidelines.name: 8 Significant Ways to Foster a Culture of Experimentation

pitfalls: Too much change too fast can deter adoption
Failing to look at prototypes holistically across teams
Ignoring hangover work from previous sprints
benefits: Pressure-free environment for failure and learning
Gaining buy-in for new ways of working
Fostering camaraderie within and across teams

name: Lightning Talks
objective: To deliver short, highly focused, and effective presentations that communicate the most important information in a limited timeframe.
description: Lightning talks are a presentation format where time restrictions force speakers to edit their message down to only the critically important elements. Common formats include traditional Lightning talks (5-10 minutes), PechaKucha (20 slides, 20 seconds each), and Ignite (20 slides, 15 seconds each). They are used for introducing new topics, sharing knowledge, or providing refreshing status updates.

activities.name: Confirm Format
activities.description: Determine the specific format to follow (e.g., 5-minute Lightning talk, PechaKucha, or Ignite) and the required length.

activities.name: Write Title and Conclusion
activities.description: Identify the title and the final destination or message you want the audience to walk away with.

activities.name: Define Idea and Objection
activities.description: Write down the single main idea and the single main objection or problem faced in one line each.

activities.name: Synthesize Tension
activities.description: Bring the idea and main objection together, acknowledging the tension and focusing on the desired outcome.

activities.name: Develop Crucial Content
activities.description: Develop only the absolutely crucial supporting points needed to guide the audience from start to destination.

activities.name: Prepare Slides
activities.description: If using slides, select minimal words and use them only when absolutely necessary.

activities.name: Practice and Rehearse
activities.description: Rehearse until the presentation can be given without looking at notes or slides.

completion_criteria: The presentation is delivered within the predetermined timebox and the core message is communicated.

metrics.name: Timebox Adherence
metrics.formula: Planned Time - Actual Time

metrics.name: Slide Count
metrics.formula: Number of slides used (Target: 20 for PechaKucha/Ignite)

pitfalls: Including too much detail
Exceeding the time limit
Relying too heavily on notes
Logistical failure of automatic slide transitions

name: Manage Flow
objective: Optimize the processing of work items
description: Direct the focus of optimization on work items, e.g. tasks, problems, process steps, instead of the people. This approach ensures that metrics focus on work delivery rather than individual utilization, preventing situations where a team is busy but work output is slow.

activities.name: Visualize work
activities.description: Create a visual representation of all work items currently being handled by the team.

activities.name: Limit Work in Progress
activities.description: Set limits on how many items can be in progress to highlight bottlenecks and free up resources for handling exceptions.

activities.name: Track throughput
activities.description: Measure and track how quickly items move through the pipeline.

activities.name: Process modification
activities.description: Analyze why items get stuck and modify the process to prevent recurrence.

completion_criteria: The team focuses on item completion, WIP limits are observed, and cycle time is consistently monitored and optimized.

metrics.name: Cycle Time
metrics.formula: Finish Date - Start Date

metrics.name: Work In Progress
metrics.formula: Count of active items in the pipeline

pitfalls: Focusing on employee utilization instead of item throughput
Ignoring stuck items
Failing to set or respect WIP limits
benefits: Improved team focus on value delivery

name: Means to End
objective: Define and communicate Vision, Mission, Strategy, Goals, Tactics & Objectives to create organizational clarity and alignment.
description: The Means to End practice helps define, differentiate, and communicate an organisational Vision, Strategy, and the associated tactical implementations through projects and initiatives (Tactics). Participants define and explore the relationship and dependencies between the Visionary, Strategic and Tactical layers to ensure alignment across the organization.

activities.name: Clarify definitions
activities.description: Clarify the differences between Objective (Tactical layer, short term) and Goal (strategic layer, longer term) to level set prior to starting.

activities.name: Define Visionary Layer
activities.description: Identify what the organization will look like in 2-5 years and what daily actions achieve that vision using brainstorming techniques like 'Start with Why'.

activities.name: Consolidate Vision and Mission
activities.description: Remove duplicates and consolidate into one Vision statement and a maximum of 5 Mission statements that clearly link to the Vision.

activities.name: Establish Strategic Goals
activities.description: Use 'Start at the End' to establish strategic goals and map them back to the Vision statement.

activities.name: Impact Mapping and Strategy
activities.description: Conduct an Impact Mapping exercise to establish strategies linked to each goal and ensure they align with the Mission.

activities.name: Define Tactical Layer
activities.description: Establish short-term objectives or OKRs to hit in support of the long-term goals.

activities.name: Map Existing Projects
activities.description: Validate your strategy by mapping existing initiatives to the tactics section to ensure alignment.

completion_criteria: All layers (Visionary, Strategic, and Tactical) are defined, deduplicated, and clearly linked to one another in a coherent framework.

metrics.name: Goal Alignment Rate
metrics.formula: Number of aligned projects / Total projects

guidelines.name: Ron Westrum - A typology of organisational cultures

pitfalls: Ambiguous definitions of Goals vs Objectives
Mission statements that do not link to the Vision
Strategic goals that are out of date or incomplete
Existing projects not aligning with new strategy

name: Measuring Psychological Safety
objective: To measure the degree of psychological safety in a team to identify strengths, weaknesses, and opportunities for cultural improvement.
description: This practice involves using a structured 10-statement survey to assess the psychological health of a team. It provides a barometer for the team's culture, facilitating explicit discussions about safety. Regular measurement helps teams in early development stages (Forming/Storming) or mature stages to adapt their behaviors and utilize practices that foster a high-performance environment.

activities.name: Create Survey
activities.description: Set up a survey tool (e.g., Google Forms) containing the 10 specific statements regarding expectations, mistakes, risk-taking, and mutual support.

activities.name: Define Scoring Scale
activities.description: Configure a 5-point Likert scale ranging from '1: Strongly disagree' to '5: Strongly agree' for each statement.

activities.name: Ensure Accessibility and Anonymity
activities.description: Translate statements into native languages if necessary and ensure responses can be submitted anonymously to encourage honesty.

activities.name: Add Qualitative Feedback
activities.description: Include an open-ended question at the end of the survey to allow team members to elaborate on their scores.

activities.name: Analyze Results
activities.description: Identify statements with the lowest average scores and those with high variance, which indicates team disagreement.

activities.name: Action Planning
activities.description: Review the context and rationale for the statements to steer leadership behaviors and adopt practices that address the identified gaps.

completion_criteria: Survey responses collected, analyzed for averages and variance, and discussed with the team to identify actionable improvements.

metrics.name: Average Psychological Safety Score
metrics.formula: Sum of all scores / Number of responses

metrics.name: Score Variance
metrics.formula: Statistical variance of scores per statement

pitfalls: Not following up on results
Poor timing of the survey
Using results to punish or compare managers/teams
Lack of anonymity leading to dishonest responses

name: Meditación (Metta Bhavana para equipos)
objective: Generar emociones positivas para el equipo y ayudar a desarrollar una mentalidad empática hacia los miembros del equipo.
description: Esta práctica consiste en una meditación de 10 minutos basada en la práctica budista Metta Bhavana. Se utiliza para calmar pensamientos, estimular emociones positivas, reducir el nivel de estrés y aliviar la ansiedad, promoviendo la integración y empatía grupal antes de eventos como retrospectivas.

activities.name: Introducción y Explicación
activities.description: Invitar al equipo y explicar que la meditación tendrá 5 etapas de 2 minutos cada una: Yo, Amigo, Persona neutral, Persona difícil y Todos en el equipo.

activities.name: Selección de Personas
activities.description: Pedir a los participantes que elijan mentalmente a un amigo, una persona neutral y una persona difícil del equipo antes de comenzar para evitar distracciones.

activities.name: Preparación y Recomendaciones
activities.description: Dar recomendaciones sobre el uso de frases positivas, recuerdos de bondad amorosa, imaginación y atención a sensaciones corporales.

activities.name: Configuración de Entorno
activities.description: Asegurar que todos estén sentados cómodamente y configurar un temporizador de 2 minutos repetido 5 veces o usar una aplicación de meditación.

activities.name: Inicio de Meditación
activities.description: Cerrar los ojos y concentrarse en la respiración inhalando y exhalando por la nariz lentamente.

activities.name: Etapa 1: El Yo
activities.description: Generar un sentimiento de bondad amorosa hacia uno mismo, enfocándose en la paz, calma y tranquilidad.

activities.name: Etapa 2: El Amigo
activities.description: Visualizar a un amigo del equipo, pensar en sus buenas cualidades y enviarle deseos de felicidad.

activities.name: Etapa 3: Persona Neutral
activities.description: Extender los buenos deseos hacia una persona del equipo por la que no se tiene un sentimiento particular de agrado o desagrado.

activities.name: Etapa 4: Persona Difícil
activities.description: Incluir a una persona con la que se tengan dificultades, enviándole emociones positivas sin juzgar sus comportamientos específicos.

activities.name: Etapa 5: El Equipo Completo
activities.description: Ampliar el deseo de bienestar para incluir a todos los miembros del equipo simultáneamente.

activities.name: Cierre
activities.description: Relajarse gradualmente, poner fin a la práctica y abrir los ojos lentamente para continuar con las actividades del equipo.

completion_criteria: Respetar el tiempo de 10 minutos completando las cinco etapas de la meditación y logrando un estado de calma en el equipo.

pitfalls: Distraer la mente durante la práctica por no elegir a las personas de las etapas de antemano.
Dejarse atrapar por sentimientos negativos en la etapa de la persona difícil.
Interrupciones externas o micrófonos abiertos en sesiones remotas.
benefits: Reduce el nivel de estrés y la ansiedad.
Ayuda a resolver malentendidos y falta de integración.
Genera emociones positivas antes de sesiones críticas como retrospectivas.

name: Meditation
objective: Generate positive emotions for the team and help to develop an empathetic mindset towards team members.
description: This practice consists of a 10-minute meditation based on the Metta Bhavana Buddhist practice. It is designed to develop empathy, calm thoughts, stimulate positive emotions, and reduce stress/anxiety within a team setting. It emphasizes individuals and interactions over processes and tools by helping to resolve misunderstandings and generate a collaborative atmosphere.

activities.name: Introduction and Explanation
activities.description: Invite the group to the meditation and explain the five stages: Self, Friend, Neutral person, Difficult person, and Everyone in the team.

activities.name: Target Selection
activities.description: Ask participants to mentally choose three people: a friend/well-liked team member, a neutral person with whom they have little contact, and a difficult person they may have had disagreements with.

activities.name: Preparation and Body Awareness
activities.description: Provide recommendations for phrases (e.g., 'may you be well') and imagery. Ensure everyone is seated comfortably and ask them to focus on their breath.

activities.name: Stage 1: Self
activities.description: For 2 minutes, participants generate feelings of loving-kindness towards themselves, focusing on peace and tranquility.

activities.name: Stage 2: Friend
activities.description: For 2 minutes, participants bring a friend to mind, thinking of their good qualities and wishing them happiness.

activities.name: Stage 3: Neutral Person
activities.description: For 2 minutes, participants extend well-wishing towards a person they feel neutral about.

activities.name: Stage 4: Difficult Person
activities.description: For 2 minutes, participants focus on a person they dislike, trying to think of them positively and sending positive emotions without needing to approve of specific behaviors.

activities.name: Stage 5: Everyone
activities.description: For the final 2 minutes, equalize well-wishing across all previous targets and extend it to include every member of the team.

activities.name: Conclusion
activities.description: Gradually relax out of the meditation and bring the practice to an end before proceeding to work or ceremonies.

completion_criteria: The 10-minute timebox is respected and all five stages of the Metta Bhavana practice are completed.

metrics.name: Team Sentiment
metrics.formula: Post-session qualitative feedback rating

pitfalls: Getting caught up in feelings of hatred during the difficult person stage
Background noise in remote environments
Microphones not being muted during remote sessions

name: Mentorship
objective: Resource mentors and mentees with structured mentoring using visual boards for clarity and alignment.
description: Mentorship is a practice focused on spreading knowledge between people. This version leverages visual boards to provide lightweight structure and alignment for both formal and informal mentoring relationships. It segments the mentoring process into three domains: Alignment (building rapport and setting boundaries), Sessions (facilitating growth and learning), and Reflection (closing the mentorship and planning next steps).

activities.name: Story.Me Cards Rapport Building
activities.description: Engage in light exercises to encourage self-disclosure and build rapport between the mentor and mentee.

activities.name: Explore Mentor/Mentee Context
activities.description: Reflect on motivations through six specific questions, brainstorm potential goals, and define individual boundaries.

activities.name: Establish Mentorship Agreement
activities.description: Meet to agree on shared boundaries, specific outcomes, meeting cadence, and expected behaviors/confidentiality rules.

activities.name: Maintain Mentorship Timeline
activities.description: Collect appreciations, learnings, and resources in a shared visual space throughout the duration of the mentorship.

activities.name: Conduct Mentoring Sessions
activities.description: Use session cards to provide visual aid for conversations and capture specific action commitments.

activities.name: Mentorship Reflection
activities.description: Close the mentorship by celebrating outcomes, discussing network connections for further support, and capturing next steps.

completion_criteria: Mentorship reflection completed, final outcomes celebrated, and next steps for both parties captured.

pitfalls: Unreconcilable conflicts in boundaries
Lack of specificity regarding confidentiality
Treating mentorship as a rigid hierarchy rather than a shifting partnership
benefits: Reinforced information channels outside of hierarchy
Nuanced career growth for both mentor and mentee

name: Metrics Based Process Mapping (MBPM)
objective: Map your process in detail and collect key baseline metrics to identify areas for improvement.
description: Metrics-Based Process Mapping (MBPM) is a detailed process mapping practice that captures process steps, responsible actors, and key time and quality metrics. It is used to view the detailed, micro picture and make tactical improvements with frontline workers, providing a performance baseline before and after automation or process improvement.

activities.name: Identify and scope process
activities.description: Select an agreed upon process to map and define its boundaries.

activities.name: Define process steps
activities.description: Through facilitation, work together to define each individual process step.

activities.name: Align responsible actors
activities.description: Assign each process step to the specific roles or actors responsible for its execution.

activities.name: Estimate metrics
activities.description: For each step, estimate Resources, Process time, Lead time, and % Complete & Accurate.

activities.name: Identify bottlenecks
activities.description: Analyze the map to build shared understanding and identify pain points and bottlenecks.

activities.name: Design future state
activities.description: Create a future state map for subsequent implementation based on the current state findings.

completion_criteria: Current and future state maps are documented, and a performance baseline with metrics is established and agreed upon by the team.

metrics.name: Resources
metrics.formula: Number of people needed to complete the step

metrics.name: Process time
metrics.formula: Time spent actually performing the work

metrics.name: Lead time
metrics.formula: Elapsed time from work availability to completion

metrics.name: % Complete & Accurate
metrics.formula: Percentage of time downstream customers can perform tasks without rework

guidelines.name: Metrics Based Process Mapping by Karen Martin
Value Stream Mapping & Process Mapping - When You Opt For Each
Metrics-Based Process Mapping by Karen Martin & Mike Osterling

benefits: Provides a detailed micro picture for tactical improvements
Determines a performance baseline for automation value
Highlights key performance points to prioritize improvements
Builds shared understanding and visibility into bottlenecks
Builds a data-driven business case for process changes

name: Minimum Viable Product (MVP)
objective: To produce the minimum amount of product functionality required to obtain value and gather feedback for future iterations.
description: Minimal Viable Product (MVP) refers to the minimum amount that can be produced and still obtain value. This value does not have to come in the form of something released to the public; it can come in closed user testing, where the value is the feedback received. It is often referred to as Earliest Testable Product (ETP) within the Agile community.

activities.name: Align with business objectives
activities.description: Ensure the planned MVP aligns with the company's strategic goals, such as revenue targets or specific market priorities.

activities.name: Identify specific problems or improvements
activities.description: Think through specific solutions for user personas and document them as user stories, epics, or features based on user research and competitive analysis.

activities.name: Translate functionality into a development action plan
activities.description: Develop a strategic plan for construction, ensuring the product remains viable and allows customers to complete an entire task with high-quality user experience.

completion_criteria: A working product exists that allows customers to complete an entire task or project with a high-quality user experience.

metrics.name: Time to First Feedback
metrics.formula: Date of Feedback Received - Date of Development Start

guidelines.name: ProductPlan: What is a Minimum Viable Product
Top 10 Interactive Product Roadmap Tools In 2021

pitfalls: Producing half-built tools and features that are not viable
Developing functionality that does not align with strategic business goals
Ignoring quality and user experience in the name of speed
benefits: Unlocks value earlier for the customer
Ensures the team is building the right thing through feedback
Reduces costs by identifying simpler solutions early
Allows for discovery of unexpected customer needs

name: Mission Statements
objective: Capture and define an inspirational purpose of an organization as the starting point of a strategy development process.
description: A mission statement defines the fundamental purpose of an organization, entity or engagement. It briefly defines why an organization exists and acts as an inspirational, clear statement that explains and drives the organization to an overall strategic destination.

activities.name: Concept Identification
activities.description: Identify the overarching reason why the organization exists by asking: Why do we exist?, Who do we serve?, and Why do we care?

activities.name: Silent Collaboration
activities.description: Using silent collaboration, attendees write down their individual answers to the key questions and post them for group viewing.

activities.name: Personal Drafting
activities.description: Each individual crafts their own personal, inspirational, and concise mission statement (1-2 sentences) based on the shared information.

activities.name: Sharing and Phrase Identification
activities.description: Individuals share their statements while the group notes phrases that are compelling or invoke emotion.

activities.name: Consensus Iteration
activities.description: Review and iterate on the draft mission statements until the group reaches a consensus.

activities.name: Litmus Testing
activities.description: Evaluate the final statement against emotional impact and the 't-shirt' test (would associates wear it?).

completion_criteria: A concise (1-2 sentence) inspirational mission statement is agreed upon by consensus and passes the litmus test for emotional resonance.

metrics.name: Time to Consensus
metrics.formula: Time of final agreement - Time workshop started

guidelines.name: How to Write a Mission Statement That Doesn't Suck

pitfalls: Varying questions during interviews which prevents finding patterns
Creating a statement that is too long or not concise
Lack of emotional resonance or inspiration in the final statement
benefits: Serves as the ultimate alignment tool for the organization
Informs all employees of the overall goal they are working toward
Provides a starting point for strategic undertakings

name: Monte Carlo Simulation
objective: Assess and manage project uncertainty and risk, enabling better decision-making and improving project outcomes.
description: A Monte Carlo simulation is a technique used to assess the impact of uncertainty and risk on project schedules, costs, or other key metrics. It involves running multiple simulations using random values for uncertain variables within specified ranges to model different possible outcomes for the project.

activities.name: Identify Uncertain Variables
activities.description: Identify key uncertain variables in the project such as task durations, resource availability, external dependencies, or market conditions.

activities.name: Define Probability Distributions
activities.description: Assign probability distributions (e.g., Triangular, Normal, Uniform) to each uncertain variable based on optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic estimates.

activities.name: Set Up the Simulation
activities.description: Use simulation software or tools to set up the model and input the uncertain variables along with their defined distributions.

activities.name: Define Simulation Parameters
activities.description: Determine the number of iterations to run and specify the confidence level (typically 90% or 95%).

activities.name: Run the Simulation
activities.description: Execute the simulation to randomly sample values from the distributions and calculate project outcomes for each iteration.

activities.name: Analyze Results
activities.description: Compute summary statistics (mean, standard deviation, percentiles) and create visualizations like histograms or probability distribution charts.

activities.name: Identify Risks and Opportunities
activities.description: Assess areas sensitive to uncertainty and identify high-risk tasks or opportunities to mitigate or exploit.

activities.name: Optimize Project Plans
activities.description: Adjust project schedules, budgets, or contingency plans based on the insights gained from the simulation.

activities.name: Communicate Results
activities.description: Share findings and the level of risk with project stakeholders using clear visualizations to inform decision-making.

completion_criteria: Simulation executed, results analyzed for risk/opportunity, and findings communicated to project stakeholders to inform plan optimization.

metrics.name: Probability of Meeting Deadline
metrics.formula: (Count of iterations meeting deadline / Total iterations) * 100

metrics.name: Estimated Cost at Confidence Interval
metrics.formula: Value at the 90th percentile of cost distribution

metrics.name: Schedule Contingency
metrics.formula: P90 Date - P50 Date

pitfalls: Inaccurate probability distributions based on biased data
Insufficient number of iterations leading to unreliable results
Ignoring dependencies and correlations between variables
Over-reliance on simulation results without considering human factors
benefits: Realistic project schedules and budgets by accounting for uncertainty
Quantified likelihood of meeting project objectives
Identification of high-risk tasks and sensitive project areas
Informed resource allocation and contingency planning
Improved communication of risk to stakeholders

name: Mood Board
objective: Find and share inspiration for solutions, features, and/or design elements
description: The Mood Board is a collaborative practice to take time to discover and share inspiration for product solutions, features, and/or design elements. Team members take time to go search for ideas of what a solution might look like, then share those ideas and what they like about each one with the rest of the team.

activities.name: Preparation
activities.description: Create a board or space in an online collaboration tool for members to put screenshots of their ideas.

activities.name: Individual Research
activities.description: Have product team members individually take 25-30 min to research ideas, products, or services for inspiration on the internet.

activities.name: Populate Board
activities.description: Copy and paste findings, including images and notes, to the board.

activities.name: Ideate
activities.description: Keep an eye on what is being added to the board as this could spark more great ideas.

activities.name: Playback
activities.description: Play back and capture any big ideas that resonate with the group.

activities.name: Selection
activities.description: If desired, implement a round of dot-voting to have people select their favorite ideas or elements.

completion_criteria: Big ideas that resonate with the group are captured or a dot-voting round is completed.

metrics.name: Discovery Duration
metrics.formula: End time - Start time

guidelines.name: How to Create a Product Mood Board - Milanote
InVision Mood Boards - Design collaboration reimagined

pitfalls: Lack of alignment on the problem before starting the search
Focusing too much on the tool rather than the inspiration
benefits: Provides creativity and direction for the team
Decreases the time it takes to design and test new ideas

name: Mood Marbles
objective: Gauging team sentiment and psychological safety anonymously.
description: Mood Marbles is a team culture practice that allows each person within a group to communicate how they are feeling at any point in time by using various colored stickies on a chart. The chart is populated with 1 of 3 colored stickies (Green, Yellow, or Red) corresponding to emotional states: Green (feeling good), Yellow (nervous or uncertain), and Red (uncomfortable or unsafe). The process is anonymous and provides a real-time visualization of the team's psychological safety.

activities.name: Set up the Jar
activities.description: Create a visual jar on a physical wall or digital whiteboard and provide team members with Green, Yellow, and Red sticky notes.

activities.name: Define Color Meanings
activities.description: Explain that Green means good to go, Yellow means nervous but okay, and Red means uncomfortable or unsafe.

activities.name: Continuous Tracking
activities.description: Invite team members to place a sticky reflecting their emotional state in the jar at any point in time.

activities.name: Monitor and Address
activities.description: The facilitator monitors the jar. If Yellows or Reds appear, address them through open discussion, anonymous confidence votes, or a dedicated retrospective.

completion_criteria: Sentiment is captured and visualized continuously, and issues identified by red or yellow marbles are addressed.

metrics.name: Sentiment Distribution
metrics.formula: Number of Green vs Yellow vs Red marbles

guidelines.name: Salient Agile Practices for Accelerated Business Outcomes Part 2
How to get value out of your team happiness measurement...and why it matters
Team Mood - Online Mood Marbles Tool

pitfalls: Ignoring yellow or red marbles when they appear
Attempting to identify who placed a specific anonymous sticky
Lack of follow-up actions when sentiment declines
benefits: Provides a safe, anonymous way to communicate feelings

name: Motivation Mapping
objective: Understanding the motivation for ourselves or the customer through a structured canvas.
description: Motivation Mapping is an exercise to understand the motivation for ourselves or the customer using a canvas divided into 5 sections: persona, context, outcomes, motivations, and obstacles. It serves as a visual status board to organize the environment factors, goals, and impediments of a specific persona. This practice is attributed to Gabrielle Benefield's Mobius Navigator Toolkit.

activities.name: Canvas Preparation
activities.description: Divide a canvas into 5 sections: WHO (top-left), CONTEXT (top-right), MOTIVATIONS/JOBS (bottom-left), OUTCOMES (bottom-middle), and OBSTACLES (bottom-right).

activities.name: Team Assembly
activities.description: Gather the team, department, business unit, or organization into one place to conduct the exercise.

activities.name: Identify Persona
activities.description: Define the persona the map is for, narrowing down to a specific target and giving them a name.

activities.name: Identify Context
activities.description: Define the environment, platform, and factors impacting the persona to help the team empathize with their situation.

activities.name: Define Outcomes
activities.description: Think from the persona's perspective to define what they want to achieve, placing these items vertically in the bottom-middle section.

activities.name: Define Motivation
activities.description: For each outcome, identify at least one motivation explaining why the persona is pursuing that specific goal.

activities.name: Define Obstacles
activities.description: Identify what is preventing the persona from making progress or reaching each outcome.

activities.name: Visualization and Maintenance
activities.description: Hang the completed map in a visible place for the organization. Regularly update it as it is a living status board, not a static document.

activities.name: Outcome Linking
activities.description: Ensure that the customer's outcomes are linked to the organization's outcomes to identify which services meet both sets of needs.

completion_criteria: The Motivation Map is complete with all five sections filled, outcomes are linked to motivations and obstacles, and the artifact is displayed in a shared team space for regular updates.

pitfalls: Treating the map as a static document rather than a living status board
Failing to narrow down the persona or give them a specific name
Not linking customer outcomes to organization outcomes
benefits: Organizes complex environments into a simple visual canvas
Helps teams step into the persona's shoes
Aligns product services with both customer and internal organizational goals

name: Moving Motivators
objective: Uncover what motivates your colleagues and yourself
description: Moving Motivators is a practice to uncover what motivates your colleagues and yourself. The practice helps the team members to understand different perspectives by visualizing individual motivation factors and how they are affected by change.

activities.name: Prepare Cards
activities.description: Download and print the cards from the Management 3.0 website or purchase a set.

activities.name: Individual Training
activities.description: Play the game alone first as training to understand the mechanics.

activities.name: Prioritize Motivators
activities.description: Sort the cards from left to right, placing the most important motivation factor on the left and the least important on the right.

activities.name: Assess Individual Change Impact
activities.description: Consider a change in your life and how this change affects your motivation factors. Move the card up if it is a positive effect and down if it is a negative effect.

activities.name: Assess Team Transformation Impact
activities.description: Consider the transformation you would like your team to commit to and look at how it affects your motivators.

completion_criteria: Participants have prioritized their motivators and visualized the impact of a specific change or transformation on those motivators.

guidelines.name: Changing Your Team From The Inside

benefits: Visualize how organizational changes affect personal motivation

name: MSPOTs (Mission, Strategy, Plays, Omissions & Targets)
objective: Unite teams around a strategic vision through a cascading goal-setting framework while preserving autonomy.
description: MSPOTs is a goal-setting framework that ladders down from leadership to individuals in teams. It creates alignment by cascading five key items: Mission (the big-picture vision), Strategy (how to achieve the mission), Plays (annual initiatives), Omissions (projects not to work on), and Targets (measurable goals for accountability).

activities.name: Mission
activities.description: Define the company’s big-picture vision, which rarely changes.

activities.name: Strategy
activities.description: Explain how the company plans to achieve the mission.

activities.name: Plays
activities.description: Define 4-5 big annual initiatives that are part of the strategy.

activities.name: Omissions
activities.description: Identify specific projects that will not be worked on according to the strategy.

activities.name: Targets
activities.description: Establish measurable and realistic means to hold everyone accountable.

completion_criteria: The five cascading items are documented, aligned with the strategy, and shared with the relevant teams.

metrics.name: Target Progress
metrics.formula: (Completed Targets / Total Planned Targets) * 100

guidelines.name: 5 Frameworks for setting better product goals

pitfalls: Time consuming to create for leadership teams
Potential confusion if 'Plays' are not aligned with 'Strategy'
Inconsistency if leadership fails to communicate changes top-down
benefits: Unites team around a strategic vision
Provides a clear framework for focus and alignment
Acts as an effective communication method

name: Mutation Testing
objective: To design new software tests and evaluate the quality of existing software tests.
description: Mutation testing involves modifying a program in small ways. Each mutated version is called a mutant and tests detect and reject mutants by causing the behavior of the original version to differ from the mutant. This is called killing the mutant.

activities.name: Mutant Creation
activities.description: Modify the program in small ways to create mutated versions called mutants.

activities.name: Mutant Execution
activities.description: Run tests against the mutants to see if they detect and reject the changes (killing the mutant).

activities.name: Analysis
activities.description: Identify weakly tested pieces of code where mutants were not killed and identify weak tests that never kill mutants.

completion_criteria: Mutation score is computed and weak tests/code segments are identified.

metrics.name: Mutation Score
metrics.formula: number of mutants killed / total number of mutants

guidelines.name: Practical Mutation Testing at Scale: A view from Google
Mutation testing by example: Execute the test - by Alex Bunardzic

benefits: Identify weakly tested pieces of code
Learn about error propagation and state infection in the program

name: Network Mapping
objective: Build a network of relationships in your organization or team to visually map out commonalities.
description: This practice visually maps out commonalities between members of a team or organization. It provides a map of how each person relates to one another to create a spark for follow-on conversation between folks who may not have met before or to deepen existing relationships. It provides a safe space to understand personal boundaries and build connections across different personality types.

activities.name: Create Personal Profile
activities.description: Everyone writes their name and a few personal characteristics (e.g., favorite fictional character, hometown) on a post-it note.

activities.name: Place Profiles on Canvas
activities.description: Place the post-it notes on a whiteboard or writeable canvas, utilizing the full available space.

activities.name: Form Pairs
activities.description: Participants find a partner and line up to form two lines facing each other.

activities.name: Identify and Map Commonality
activities.description: In 1-2 minutes, each pair finds something in common, draws a line on the whiteboard between their two post-its, and writes the common trait along the line.

activities.name: Rotate and Repeat
activities.description: One line rotates to the left and the mapping process is repeated with new partners for 3-4 rounds.

completion_criteria: Completion of at least 3-4 rounds of mapping to create a functional network of connections.

benefits: Inclusive of both extroverts and introverts

name: News Headlines aka Cover Story
objective: To suspend disbelief and focus on a successful future by imagining a distant state where a product or service has achieved significant public admiration.
description: This Design Thinking practice forces participants to start with the end in mind. Participants list headlines and draft a popular magazine article outlining the success of the product, service, and organization. The article details user benefits, beloved features, and use cases, often including images and customer quotes to create a rich story. It encourages wild ideas and creative thinking without the constraints of the present.

activities.name: Explain Object and Categories
activities.description: Define the template categories: Cover (the big story), Headlines (substance), Sidebars (interesting facets), Quotes (related commentary), Brainstorm (initial ideas), and Images (illustrations).

activities.name: Organize Groups
activities.description: Break players into groups of 4-6 with markers and a template. Groups may select a scribe or work collaboratively on the template.

activities.name: Imagine and Generate Story
activities.description: Participants spend five quiet minutes imagining their own stories before spending 30–45 minutes as a group to generate and document a best-case 'story of the year' on the template.

activities.name: Present Visions
activities.description: Breakout groups reconvene and spend 5–10 minutes each sharing their imagined stories and supporting elements with the larger group.

activities.name: Synthesis and Discussion
activities.description: Note common vision themes and areas of agreement. Ask for observations, insights, and concerns regarding the future state.

completion_criteria: A completed cover story template for each group and a summarized list of common vision themes and insights.

metrics.name: Vision Alignment
metrics.formula: Number of common themes / Total themes generated

metrics.name: Session Duration
metrics.formula: Total time taken - Allocated time

pitfalls: Focusing too much on current technical constraints
Dominance of a single voice in a breakout group
Lack of diverse perspectives among participants
benefits: Shifts focus from features to benefits/outcomes

name: Non-Functional Requirements Map
objective: To gain a shared understanding of non-functional aspects of a system in a visually structured representation.
description: The Non-Functional Requirements Map is a tool to capture and articulate the non-functional requirements of a solution that a team is striving to deliver. It aims to elaborate non-functional areas that are unlikely to be captured using practices primarily focused on functional aspects of a solution. Non-functional requirements are broken down into areas represented by each segment on the canvas which are examined and populated in turn as a group.

activities.name: Review Headings
activities.description: Ensure that everyone participating is comfortable with the headings and what they mean.

activities.name: Capture Considerations
activities.description: For each heading capture important considerations or requirements for the system or solution and add stickies for each item. Depending on the number of participants, you could use facilitation techniques such as 1-2-4-all or splitting into smaller groups to capture items.

activities.name: Playback Stickies
activities.description: For each heading playback the stickies as a group.

activities.name: Determine Outcomes
activities.description: During the playback think about whether each sticky should be: an item added to the backlog and prioritised, part of the Definition of Done, or part of the acceptance criteria for other backlog items.

completion_criteria: The key non-functional considerations for the solution are identified and translated into actionable backlog items, Definition of Done updates, or acceptance criteria.

pitfalls: Participants not understanding the headings
Focusing only on functional aspects
Neglecting to map stickies to actionable backlog items
benefits: Identification of hidden work required to realize non-functional requirements

name: North Star Framework
objective: Helps achieve alignment on the 'why' and overall purpose by identifying a single, crucial metric that captures core product value.
description: The North Star Framework is a model for managing products by identifying a single, crucial metric known as the North Star Metric. This metric best captures the core value that a product delivers to customers. It is useful for product decisions and operational aspects like Support, Customer Success, and Product Marketing. The framework translates strategy into concrete decisions and acts as a leading indicator of sustainable growth.

activities.name: Understand Strategy
activities.description: Understand the organizational strategy and key metrics of success.

activities.name: Analyze Customer Journey
activities.description: Understand the customer journey and key moments and events of value creation and value realization.

activities.name: Identify North Star Metric
activities.description: Analyze metrics, including metrics of conversation, to identify the leading indicators for the key events and moments.

activities.name: Build the Framework
activities.description: Evaluate and link the metric to all initiatives, major product features in the backlog, and OKRs.

completion_criteria: A single North Star metric is identified and documented that is value-based, strategy-aligned, a leading indicator, actionable, understandable, measurable, and not a vanity metric.

metrics.name: North Star Metric Performance
metrics.formula: Value defined by specific product context (e.g., Daily Active Users / Total Users)

pitfalls: Using a vanity metric that does not predict long-term success
Choosing a metric that is not actionable or cannot be influenced
Selecting a lagging indicator instead of a leading indicator
Metric is too complex for non-technical stakeholders to understand
benefits: Achieves alignment between team members and stakeholders
Translates strategy into concrete product and organizational decisions
Connects product activities to broader business sustainable growth
Helps with course correction and maintenance of focus

name: Objectives & Key Results (OKRs)
objective: Align people around a shared ambitious goal and then maintain focus through measuring progress regularly
description: OKRs are a collaborative goal-setting framework for organisations, teams and individuals to set challenging, ambitious goals with measurable results. It consists of an Objective (the outcome) and Key Results (the success criteria used to measure progress).

activities.name: Restate Mission and Vision
activities.description: Start by clarifying the team's mission and vision to ensure everyone is pointing in the same direction.

activities.name: Brainstorm Outcomes
activities.description: Gather ideas for medium to long-term goals using post-its and techniques like 1-2-4-all and dot voting to avoid groupthink.

activities.name: Prioritize Outcomes
activities.description: Identify the top 3-5 ideas using prioritization techniques like priority sliders or the $100 game.

activities.name: Define Objectives
activities.description: Rephrase outcomes as qualitative, inspiring, and time-bound Objectives that describe the destination.

activities.name: Define Key Results
activities.description: Quantify success for each Objective by defining 3-5 specific, measurable metrics that track value and impact.

activities.name: Assign Owners
activities.description: Identify individuals responsible for iterating, finalizing, and tracking each OKR visibly.

completion_criteria: Objectives and Key Results are documented, assigned owners, and made visible to the organization.

metrics.name: Key Result Progress
metrics.formula: (Current Value / Target Value) * 100

metrics.name: Objective Achievement
metrics.formula: Average of associated Key Result percentages

pitfalls: Defining tasks instead of outcomes
Setting too many objectives (over 5)
Using jargon that makes goals unclear
Lack of alignment with company mission

name: Observability
objective: Determine internal states of a system from external outputs to discover and understand system behavior.
description: Observability is the ability to know and discover what is going on in our systems. It enables a holistic view and deep understanding of systems to identify issues faster, understand root causes, and offer better customer experiences. It focuses on handling 'unknown unknowns' by allowing teams to ask any question of their systems at any point in time.

activities.name: Identify Questions
activities.description: Determine what information is needed to understand system behavior and user experience.

activities.name: Instrument Telemetry Data
activities.description: Implement the three pillars of observability: metrics (quantifiable measures), logs (immutable event records), and traces (end-to-end request paths).

activities.name: Correlate Data Sources
activities.description: Link different telemetry sources, such as using unique request IDs, to provide full context across system layers.

activities.name: Analyze and Respond
activities.description: Use the gathered data to identify bottlenecks, validate ideas, and prioritize features or fixes based on actual system and user behavior.

completion_criteria: The system provides sufficient external outputs (telemetry) to answer any question about its internal state and performance without requiring new code deployments.

metrics.name: Requests per second
metrics.formula: Total requests / seconds in period

metrics.name: Memory Usage
metrics.formula: Used Memory / Total Memory

metrics.name: Latency
metrics.formula: Response Time - Request Time

pitfalls: Gathering data without correlating it
Treating metrics as the only source of truth
Failing to define what data is actually valuable for the specific system

name: Offboarding
objective: To build resiliency and reliability for the automation of code deployment, thus resulting in less downtime and more throughput.
description: A practice focused on destroying and re-creating applications using Everything-as-Code. By putting all infrastructure and configuration data into code, applications can be deployed in a repeatable and reliable fashion. This involves deploying code in one environment and then re-deploying into another, or destroying the environment and re-deploying from scratch.

activities.name: Implement Everything-as-Code
activities.description: Put all infrastructure and configuration data into code formats.

activities.name: Initial Deployment
activities.description: Deploy the application into a target environment using the automated code.

activities.name: Destroy and Re-deploy
activities.description: Intentionally destroy the environment and re-deploy into the same or a different environment to verify repeatability.

activities.name: Iterate and Debug
activities.description: Perform this on a 1-3 week cadence to identify and catch all automation bugs until the process is seamless.

completion_criteria: The application can be destroyed and re-created in a reliable, repeatable fashion across different environments with all automation bugs addressed.

metrics.name: Practice Cadence
metrics.formula: Time between offboarding practice sessions

metrics.name: Automation Defect Count
metrics.formula: Number of issues identified during the re-creation process

guidelines.name: Container promotion on Red Hat OpenShift Container Platform

pitfalls: Infrequent practice leading to stale automation scripts
Manual configurations not captured in code
Lack of shared understanding across the team

name: One Minute Mindfulness
objective: Calm the mind and body to help team members manage stress levels.
description: A micro-habit designed to incorporate stress reduction into the daily routine to nurture well-being, boost energy, and develop an empathetic mindset. It is easily integrated into scrum practices, specifically at the start of daily stand-ups.

activities.name: Invitation
activities.description: Invite the team for a minute of relaxation.

activities.name: Physical Comfort
activities.description: Make sure everyone is seated comfortably.

activities.name: Timer Setup
activities.description: Set a timer for one minute.

activities.name: Mindful Breathing
activities.description: Ask participants to close their eyes and focus on their breath; have them inhale through their nose and exhale through their mouth slowly.

activities.name: Exercise
activities.description: Start the timer and enjoy the exercise.

activities.name: Continuation
activities.description: When the minute is over, carry on with your sprint ceremonies.

completion_criteria: One minute timer has elapsed and team returns to the ceremony.

guidelines.name: Don’t Forget to Breathe: A Controlled Trial of Mindfulness Practices in Agile Project Teams
How to Use Mindfulness to Increase Your Team’s Creativity

pitfalls: Team members feeling rushed
Distractions in the environment
Inconsistency in daily application

name: One-word story
objective: Sharpens the ability of a team to listen and progress collectively.
description: This is a common practice used for improvisation theatre. It is an effective and funny way to make a team listen to each other and collaboratively invent a story. Participants are forced to integrate their own ideas with the ideas of others, requiring spontaneity and fast adaptation. It serves as an ice-breaker and provides a fast-track experience to agility and team collaboration.

activities.name: Positioning
activities.description: Participants position themselves in a physical chain or sequence to establish the order of speakers.

activities.name: Topic Stimulation
activities.description: A moderator provides a stimulation topic or prompt to start the story.

activities.name: Story Generation
activities.description: The first person starts with one word, and each subsequent person in the chain adds exactly one fitting word to evolve the narrative.

activities.name: Advanced Variations
activities.description: Moderators may challenge the group by throwing in absurd words or limiting word choices by prohibiting specific letters.

completion_criteria: A complete story is collaboratively generated by the group following the one-word sequence.

guidelines.name: Description of the practice on thegamegal.com

pitfalls: Participants feeling embarrassed
Participants feeling overwhelmed
Lack of listening to previous words

name: Open Decision Framework
objective: Arriving at actionable agreements through transparent, inclusive, and customer-centric participatory practices.
description: Open decision making is an approach to arriving at actionable agreements through participatory practices. It involves clearly sharing problems, requirements, and constraints with affected parties; collaborating purposefully with multiple stakeholders to secure diverse opinions and comprehensive feedback; and managing relationships and expectations across competing needs and priorities. It applies five open source principles: open exchange, participation, meritocracy, community, and 'release early, release often'.

activities.name: Ideation
activities.description: Lead with transparency for the problem you wish to solve or make a decision on. Openly list possible approaches or constraints on the decision that are immovable.

activities.name: Planning and research
activities.description: Actively engage customers and collaborators. Solicit feedback and set the type of feedback desired. Be open about the timeframe, the scope, the feedback/research done, and how you will respond to negative commentary.

activities.name: Design, development, and testing
activities.description: Investigate the options generated from the feedback, highlight changes done based on the feedback and why other feedback was not feasible or used. Identify and leverage ambassadors to promote the options investigated.

activities.name: Launch
activities.description: Share the steps in making the decision (specifically the incorporation of the feedback). Acknowledge and be prepared to make the decision one that is continuously updated based on feedback.

completion_criteria: The decision-making process is documented, feedback is incorporated or addressed, and the decision is published with an acknowledgment of continuous updates.

metrics.name: ODF Maturity Level
metrics.formula: Check against Open Decision Framework Maturity Model PDF criteria

pitfalls: Unanticipated impacts of business decisions due to lack of transparency
Failure to address negative commentary
Excluding affected parties from the feedback loop
benefits: Diverse perspectives leading to better solutions
Limits unanticipated impacts of business projects

name: Open Leadership Mindset
objective: Unlock a team's potential through a specific fusion of behaviors and mindsets that enable people to thrive in an open organization.
description: Open leadership is a blend of mindsets, behaviors, and practices where leadership is not a position but an opportunity open to everyone. It is characterized by five key mindsets: Default to Open, Inclusive, Growth, Opt-in/Appreciative, and Enterprise. Leaders in this model believe everyone has something to contribute and a responsibility to lead, using personal influence rather than positional power.

activities.name: Default to Open
activities.description: Establish transparency as the standard; share information, code, and ideas openly unless there is a specific reason to restrict access.

activities.name: Foster Inclusivity
activities.description: Actively include diverse perspectives and subject matter experts in collaboration and co-creation efforts.

activities.name: Cultivate Growth Mindset
activities.description: Treat talent as a quality that can be developed through effort and practice rather than a fixed trait.

activities.name: Lead through Influence (Opt-in)
activities.description: Use personal power and influence to lead, encouraging others to lead regardless of their formal job title.

activities.name: Prioritize the Enterprise
activities.description: Put the organization's interests and common goals ahead of individual or team-specific interests.

completion_criteria: Team behaviors consistently reflect the five open mindsets and decisions are made transparently following the Open Decision Framework.

guidelines.name: What is the Open Decision Framework?

pitfalls: Using positional power instead of personal influence
Restricting information access without a valid justification
Treating talent as a fixed quality
Prioritizing team fiefdoms over enterprise interests

name: Open Leader Persona
