objective: To collectively create a persona that reflects the mindset, challenges, behaviors, and strengths of an open leader, fostering a shared understanding of transparency, inclusivity, community, collaboration, and adaptability.
description: This practice invites participants to collaboratively construct an 'Open Leader Persona'. By reflecting on the five characteristics of open leadership—transparency, inclusivity, community, collaboration, and adaptability—participants define the mindset, obstacles, specific behaviors, and inherent strengths required to lead openly. This exercise empowers leaders to apply these principles within their organizations to drive positive change and nurture a collaborative work environment.

activities.name: Set Up the Flip Chart
activities.description: Prepare a flip chart with the heading 'Open Leader Persona' and create four quadrants: Mindset, Challenges, Behaviors, and Strengths.

activities.name: Explore Open Leader Characteristics
activities.description: Outline the five core characteristics: transparency, inclusivity, community, collaboration, and adaptability.

activities.name: Group Persona Creation
activities.description: Invite participants to work together to capture their understanding of the persona's attributes.

activities.name: Define Mindset
activities.description: Discuss and record qualities such as empathy, adaptability, and openness to feedback.

activities.name: Explore Challenges
activities.description: Identify obstacles like managing diverse perspectives or navigating organizational resistance to change.

activities.name: Define Behaviors
activities.description: Brainstorm specific actions such as active listening, promoting transparency, and giving credit to team members.

activities.name: Identify Strengths
activities.description: List attributes such as resilience, visionary thinking, and relationship building.

activities.name: Review and Finalize
activities.description: Review the completed persona as a group and make necessary adjustments to capture the essence of open leadership.

activities.name: Concluding Remarks
activities.description: Discuss how to apply these learnings in leadership roles to drive organizational change.

completion_criteria: The Open Leader Persona is fully documented across all four categories (Mindset, Challenges, Behaviors, Strengths) and the group has reached a consensus on the definition.

metrics.name: Participant Engagement
metrics.formula: Number of unique contributors to the persona sections

metrics.name: Completion Time
metrics.formula: End Time - Start Time

pitfalls: Dominant voices overshadowing quieter participants
Focusing on abstract concepts rather than practical, actionable behaviors
Organizational resistance to the identified open behaviors
benefits: Creates a shared understanding of leadership values
Empowers individuals to take ownership of leadership principles

name: Parking Lot (Car Park)
objective: A visible area to help track important items, ideas, questions, and issues that may not be important to discuss at the time, but the group does wish to discuss later.
description: A visible location in a room or virtual whiteboard that captures any type of topic that is not important to discuss at the time, but deemed worthy to discuss later by the group gathered. It serves as a means to capture thoughts and intentions so that they are heard and understood, placed somewhere they won't be forgotten, and followed up on at a later time.

activities.name: Agree on Location
activities.description: Establish a visible location to capture topics to discuss later, such as a wall space with post-its for physical meetings or a shared document for remote sessions.

activities.name: Identify and Capture Items
activities.description: Identify topics that are off-topic during the main discussion and capture them in the Parking Lot. This can be done by anyone in the group to ensure the main purpose of the meeting is met.

activities.name: Review Parking Lot
activities.description: Allot 5-10 minutes at the end of the timebox to review captured items and determine the appropriate next steps or owners for each.

completion_criteria: The session timebox is respected and all captured items have been reviewed with identified next steps.

metrics.name: Parking Lot Resolution Rate
metrics.formula: (Items with defined next steps / Total items captured) * 100

guidelines.name: Quick Tip: The Open Meeting Parking Lot

pitfalls: Ideas are ceremonially dismissed rather than captured for genuine followup
Designating a single person to capture all items rather than shared ownership
Failing to allot time at the end to review the items
Parking Lot is not visible to all participants during the discussion
benefits: Ensures participants feel heard and understood
Prevents meetings from being derailed by off-topic discussions
Generates important insights for future discussions

name: Personal User Manuals
objective: Connecting with colleagues in a deep and meaningful way by communicating how you best work and who you are.
description: A personal user manual (PUM) is a document that allows you to explicitly communicate how you best work, who you are as a human being and provide others advice, ideas and a framework to best interact with you. It serves as a shortcut to a deeper understanding of one another in your own words which allows teams to communicate and collaborate in a much more effective, efficient, and enjoyable way.

activities.name: Select a Template
activities.description: Open a text editor or select a pre-existing template (such as the Atlassian version) to structure your manual.

activities.name: Draft the Content
activities.description: Spend approximately 5 minutes writing about your work preferences, communication style, likes/dislikes, strengths, and weaknesses.

activities.name: Self-Reflection
activities.description: Use the writing process to develop self-awareness by considering how others perceive you and identifying your own blind spots.

activities.name: Share with the Team
activities.description: Distribute the completed manual to colleagues to facilitate better interactions and skip past months of trial and error.

completion_criteria: The manual is written and shared with at least one or more team members.

guidelines.name: Atlassian's Explainer and brilliant slideshow template
The unconventional route's article on User Manuals

pitfalls: Overthinking the content before starting
Assuming others already know your work preferences
Feeling uncomfortable with initial vulnerability
benefits: Reduces wasted time and energy from incorrect assumptions
Promotes a healthier and happier team environment
Shortcuts the long period of trial and error in relationships

name: PESTEL Analysis
objective: Understand your environment and scan for factors that could impact an organization's market, business position, and operational strategies.
description: PESTEL analysis is a framework for scanning the environment for factors that could impact an organization's market, business position, and/or operational strategies. Context helps to identify and frame why an organization should pursue a particular strategy.

activities.name: Identify Scope
activities.description: Identify scope for analysis (business unit, whole company, etc.) with a sponsor and/or key stakeholders.

activities.name: Assemble Experts
activities.description: Pull together a diverse group of experts to obtain a broad set of viewpoints (ex. mix of business, marketing, product, operations, and technical staff).

activities.name: Brainstorm Factors
activities.description: Participants brainstorm environmental factors, trends, and/or changes from six lenses: Political, Economic, SocioCultural, Technological, Environmental, and Legal.

activities.name: Aggregate and Discuss
activities.description: Aggregate / Affinity map similar factors. Discuss/restate all factors to ensure all participants share understanding.

activities.name: Identify Impact
activities.description: Identify the most impactful factors to the organization (ex. using dot voting).

completion_criteria: The most impactful environmental factors have been identified and a shared understanding is established among stakeholders.

pitfalls: Homogeneous group perspectives
Ignoring environmental factors during strategy execution
Performing the analysis once and never revisiting it
benefits: Builds shared understanding across technical and business staff

name: Pick-Play-Pass
objective: Gamify group engagement to cohesively organize divergent perspectives and build shared alignment.
description: Pick-play-pass is a facilitation game used to organize topics or items on a scale (like priority or effort). Inspired by gift-giving games like 'White Elephant', it allows participants to either introduce new items ('Pick') or move existing ones ('Play') with an explanation. This structure ensures that everyone, not just the most vocal participants, has an equal opportunity to influence the outcome.

activities.name: Prepare Backlog
activities.description: Populate an unprioritized backlog with a list of topics to be reviewed by the group.

activities.name: Build Visual Scale
activities.description: Create a visual scale (single axis or 2-axis graph) large enough to fit all topics.

activities.name: First Move
activities.description: The first participant picks an item from the backlog, plots it on the scale, and provides a short explanation for the placement.

activities.name: Pick or Play Phase
activities.description: Subsequent participants choose to either 'Pick' a new item from the backlog or 'Play' by moving a previously placed item, explaining their reasoning in either case.

activities.name: Expansion Phase
activities.description: Once all items are plotted, participants gain a third option: 'Pass'. They can now Pick (if items remain), Play (move an item), or Pass (if they agree with the current layout).

activities.name: Conclusion
activities.description: Continue turns until every participant chooses to 'Pass' in succession, indicating collective agreement.

completion_criteria: The game is over when all participants choose to pass their turn, indicating agreement with the final placement of all items.

pitfalls: Vocal participants or HIPOs (Highest Paid Person's Opinion) dominating the discussion if not facilitated strictly
Moving items without providing the required explanation
benefits: Ensures equal engagement for all participants

name: Plan Your Trip
objective: Manage risks by considering the 4Cs: Costs, Consequences, Context, and Choices.
description: An exercise that determines the appropriate level of rigor for risk management in a project. By using the analogy of planning a hike, participants learn to apply the 4Cs (Costs, Consequences, Context, and Choices) to risk decisions. The practice also uses a purchase-rigor analogy (Coffee vs. Condo) to align the team on the appropriate effort for risk mitigation relative to project stakes.

activities.name: Individual Brainstorming
activities.description: Team members individually list items they would pack for a simple two-mile hike on a warm day using Post-it notes.

activities.name: Group Review
activities.description: Review the lists as a group, discussing the pros and cons of the items in terms of utility versus the burden of carrying them.

activities.name: Scenario Scaling
activities.description: Repeat the exercise with increased complexity, such as a multi-day winter hike in the mountains, to observe how risk preparation scales.

activities.name: The 4Cs Analysis
activities.description: Review the Costs, Consequences, Context, and Choices for each scenario to determine the informed balance needed for the project.

activities.name: Decision Rigor Discussion
activities.description: Compare project risk management to purchase decisions (Coffee, Couch, Car, Condo) to identify the appropriate level of professional rigor and research required.

activities.name: Risk vs. Value Trade-off
activities.description: Discuss the relationship between business value (deposits) and risks (withdrawals) to maximize gains while reducing threats.

completion_criteria: The team reaches consensus and documentation is updated with a shared risk management strategy.

pitfalls: Participants retreating into observer mode
Groupthink during risk identification
Lack of investment in the process due to missing 'why'
benefits: Breaks down barriers to effective risk management
Tailors process rigor to project importance
Teaches risk basics and terminology to the team

name: Priority Sliders
objective: Facilitate conversations about relative priorities to focus upcoming activities
description: A tool involving a finite set of topics/dimensions that are relatively prioritized against each other. Participants use sliders to indicate relative importance (right for more important, left for less). It enables alignment, provides clarification of motivations across stakeholder groups, and acts as a filtering mechanism for later prioritization or decision-making by providing an objective discriminating factor.

activities.name: Choose Metrics
activities.description: Select the metrics for each slider, such as functional completeness, performance, security, or learning.

activities.name: Introduction
activities.description: Introduce the sliders and explain them to the participants.

activities.name: Individual Placement
activities.description: Participants individually write down their placement for each slider on sticky notes for about 5 minutes without sharing to minimize bias.

activities.name: Team Review and Alignment
activities.description: Review each slider as a team, discussing alignment and driving toward an agreed-upon spot on the scale, annotated with a different color sticky.

completion_criteria: Agreed-upon spot on the scale for each slider reached and annotated with a team-consensus sticky.

pitfalls: Groupthink bias if individual sticky notes are not used first
Lack of alignment if discussion is rushed
benefits: Enables alignment and consensus on areas of focus
Clarifies motivations and desires across stakeholder groups
Acts as a filtering mechanism for product backlog prioritization
Provides objective factors for autonomous team decision-making

name: Proto-Persona
objective: Define target users and audience based on stakeholder assumptions to guide product design and build a shared understanding.
description: A proto-persona is a description of the target users and audience of a product based on the assumptions of stakeholders. Creating this ad-hoc persona allows product teams to begin designing and building immediately without getting overly bogged down with the details of user behavior. Since these personas are developed using the preliminary knowledge and assumptions of stakeholders and team members, the persona should be updated with any new information or analysis gained during the course of the project.

activities.name: Team Formation
activities.description: Collaborate as a team to develop the proto-persona to build a shared understanding and ensure team buy-in.

activities.name: Identify User Groups
activities.description: Discuss groups identified through journey mapping, event storming, or customer support feedback and decide which users to focus on.

activities.name: Humanize the Persona
activities.description: Give each user a name to help humanize them and build a common vocabulary within the team.

activities.name: Map Attributes
activities.description: Use a template or sticky notes to list out each user's attributes, goals, and obstacles.

activities.name: Review and Align
activities.description: Discuss each persona's unique circumstances and collectively agree on the primary persona whose problems the team will solve.

completion_criteria: The team collectively agrees on a specific persona and their associated problems to solve.

guidelines.name: A Guide for Creating Personas for Lean UX

pitfalls: Getting bogged down in details of user behavior too early
Failing to update personas with actual user research data
Lack of team-wide participation leading to gaps in shared understanding
benefits: Builds a shared understanding and common vocabulary
Helps prioritize product features and drives focus
Avoids risky knowledge gaps between researchers and developers
Assists in recruiting the correct types of users for research

name: Purpose to practice (P2P)
objective: Enables stakeholders to collaboratively shape the success of their initiative.
description: By using P2P at the start of an initiative, stakeholders shape all elements that determine success: purpose, principles, participants, structure, and practices. This Liberating Structure ensures shared ownership and creative adaptation by including a large number of stakeholders in designing their collective future.

activities.name: Structuring Invitation
activities.description: Invite all or most stakeholders to participate in the design of their new initiative to specify its five essential elements.

activities.name: Introduction
activities.description: Introduce the idea of P2P, the five elements (purpose, principles, participants, structure, and practices), and related questions, handing out blank worksheets.

activities.name: Clarify Purpose
activities.description: Ask 'Why is the work important to you and the larger community?'. Use 1-2-4-All to generate individual ideas, compare in small groups, and integrate themes as a whole group.

activities.name: Define Principles
activities.description: Ask 'What rules must we absolutely obey to succeed in achieving our purpose?'. Use the 1-2-4-All sequence.

activities.name: Identify Participants
activities.description: Ask 'Who can contribute to achieving our purpose and must be included?'. Use the 1-2-4-All sequence.

activities.name: Design Structure
activities.description: Ask 'How must we organize and distribute control to achieve our purpose?'. Use the 1-2-4-All sequence.

activities.name: Specify Practices
activities.description: Ask 'What are we going to do? What will we offer to our users/clients and how will we do it?'. Use the 1-2-4-All sequence.

activities.name: Reflection and Prioritization
activities.description: Step back and look at all five elements together. Use 'What, So What, Now What?' to make sense of next steps and prioritize them as a whole group.

completion_criteria: The five essential elements of the initiative have been defined, a shared purpose is established, and prioritized next steps have been agreed upon by the stakeholder group.

metrics.name: Stakeholder Inclusion Rate
metrics.formula: Number of stakeholders present / Total stakeholders invited

metrics.name: Implementation Speed
metrics.formula: Date of first practice implementation - Date of P2P workshop

guidelines.name: Birth of the Chaordic Age by Dee Hock

pitfalls: Design by a small group or experts-only behind closed doors
Fragmented design process
Top-down control in the structure element instead of distributed control
Failing to revisit P2P design periodically after launch
benefits: Engage everyone’s imagination in designing the collective future
Increased resilience and ability to absorb disruptions
Capacity to rapidly adapt to changing circumstances

name: Quote Wall
objective: To help encourage a positive attitude, fun, and laughter while the Team works together.
description: A collection of quotes or phrases captured from the team that may be motivational, inspiring, or down-right hilarious! This is a simple place to capture all the hilarious, inspiring, smart, and silly things we might say throughout a Team's history together.

activities.name: Prepare the board
activities.description: Create a board with the title 'The Quote Wall'.

activities.name: Capture quotes
activities.description: Capture people's quotes with sticky notes throughout the team's history together.

completion_criteria: A visual board is established and contains quotes captured from the team.

name: Raison d'être
objective: Defining the team's reason for being and creating a shared manifesto for clarity and alignment.
description: This norming and forming practice is run as teams form or embark upon new directions because it is important that they understand their reason for coming together. This exercise helps to define a manifesto for the team so that everyone agrees upon and has crystal clarity on their purpose, serving as a guide-post reminder during times of pressure.

activities.name: The Question
activities.description: Ask the team: 'What is the purpose of this team? Why were we brought together? What is our reason for being?'

activities.name: Individual Drafting
activities.description: Give the team 3 minutes to independently write a paragraph answering the prompt without seeing others' responses.

activities.name: Sharing
activities.description: Have each member of the team read their answer aloud to the team. No debate or discussion is permitted at this point.

activities.name: Re-prompting
activities.description: Ask the same questions from step 1 again to focus thoughts after hearing colleagues' perspectives.

activities.name: Independent Refinement
activities.description: Provide 3 more minutes for each team member to rewrite their paragraph on the back of their sheet.

activities.name: Group Synthesis
activities.description: The team comes together to craft a single unified statement for their reason for being on a single sheet of paper.

activities.name: Manifesto Visualization
activities.description: Transfer the finalized manifesto to a larger format, such as a poster, and hang it in a central location in the team's work area.

completion_criteria: A finalized manifesto is created and displayed in a central location in the team's work area.

guidelines.name: Bruce Mau, MC24: The Manifesto Offers Leaders a Powerful Technique for Creating Inspiration

pitfalls: Team members skewing the reason for being toward their specific discipline rather than the team's functionality
Individuals seeing themselves as representing a silo (e.g. backend, frontend, QA) rather than a product
benefits: Ensures all team members have crystal clarity on purpose
Aligns the team to row in the same direction

name: Realtime Retrospective
objective: Capture feedback from a large number of participants in realtime over an extended period of time to improve the event experience.
description: The Realtime Retrospective technique was created by Emily Webber to capture anonymous, unfiltered feedback in a timeline-like fashion throughout an event. It allows facilitators to monitor the mood and reactions of participants as they happen, enabling adjustments on the fly rather than waiting until the end of a long-running session or multi-day event.

activities.name: Preparations
activities.description: Find a long wall or surface. Roll out drawing paper and draw a horizontal line representing time. Draw a Happy Face above the line, a Sad Face below the line, and a Surprised Face along the line to categorize types of feedback.

activities.name: Explain the Practice
activities.description: Explain the goal to participants. Show them where to find materials and instruct them to place one feedback item per post-it along the chronological timeline from left to right.

activities.name: Review and Improve
activities.description: Cycle back constantly during the event to review the feedback on the wall and make appropriate improvements or adjustments based on the insights provided.

completion_criteria: The event timeframe has concluded and the feedback gathered has been reviewed for actionable improvements.

metrics.name: Feedback Volume
metrics.formula: Total number of sticky notes posted / Number of sessions

pitfalls: Sticky notes falling off walls (mitigated by using drawing paper rolls)
Lack of participation if the goal is not clearly explained
benefits: Low bar of entry for participation
Voluntary and easy to participate anonymously
Visibility of people's moods in realtime
Ability to adjust sessions or activities on the fly

name: Backlog Filter
objective: Identify and action high complexity backlog items to de-risk delivery and improve predictability.
description: The backlog filter is a tool used to surface high-risk work items by viewing them through the lens of complexity and team knowledge. By mapping work based on the involvement required from outside the team and how well the team understands the tasks, organizations can prioritize unknown work and allow for timely management intervention.

activities.name: Preparation
activities.description: Download the remote:af backlog filter template and upload it to a virtual whiteboard tool or print it for an in-person workshop.

activities.name: Explore Backlog
activities.description: Review the current work backlog with the entire team to ensure a shared understanding of the items.

activities.name: Silent Mapping
activities.description: Team members individually and silently map work items to the matrix based on required outside involvement and internal knowledge of the work.

activities.name: Consensus Building
activities.description: Discuss and explore any disagreements in the mapping. If consensus cannot be reached, err on the side of caution regarding risk.

activities.name: Action Mapping
activities.description: Assign actions to the work items based on their position in the matrix, categorizing them into Proceed, Plan, or Probe.

completion_criteria: All targeted backlog items are mapped to the matrix and assigned a specific action (Proceed, Plan, or Probe).

metrics.name: Predictability Improvement
metrics.formula: Actual Delivery / Forecasted Delivery

guidelines.name: A framework to manage uncertainty in early planning of projects

pitfalls: Inability to reach consensus on item placement
Underestimating required outside involvement
Ignoring the 'Probe' actions for highly uncertain items
benefits: Surfaces high risk work items early
Identifies items requiring regular expertise vs outside help

name: ROAM Boards
objective: Categorize project or program risks into four categories (Resolved, Owned, Accepted, Mitigated) to foster collaboration and shared responsibility.
description: ROAM Boards are a simple visualization for categorizing project or program risks. It is a collaborative effort involving all stakeholders to think creatively about how to solve or mitigate problems, creating a sense of shared responsibility for uncovering hard problems that may act as roadblocks to success.

activities.name: Brainwriting
activities.description: Participants silently write down any risks to the plan, regardless of size, for 5-7 minutes.

activities.name: Readouts and Clarification
activities.description: The facilitator reads back the cards for clarity. The team uses affinity mapping to group related cards or eliminate duplicates, avoiding deep-dive problem solving at this stage.

activities.name: Categorization
activities.description: The team assigns each risk set to Resolved, Owned, Accepted, or Mitigated. Owned risks must be assigned to a single person to track progress.

activities.name: Plan Revision
activities.description: The team alters the project plan based on the risks, such as moving work forward for investigation or delaying work that is in the Accepted category.

activities.name: Establish Cadence
activities.description: Determine a weekly or bi-weekly cadence (e.g., during sprint planning or backlog refinement) to review and update the risks.

completion_criteria: All identified risks are categorized, 'Owned' risks have a single designated owner, and a recurring cadence for risk tracking is established.

metrics.name: Risk Identification Count
metrics.formula: Total number of sticky notes generated

metrics.name: Resolution Rate
metrics.formula: Resolved risks / Total risks identified

pitfalls: Deep diving into problem solving during the readout phase
Not assigning a single owner to 'Owned' risks
Failing to record explanations for 'Accepted' or 'Mitigated' risks
Treating the board as a one-time kickoff activity rather than a living document
benefits: Creates a sense of shared responsibility
Scalable from individual teams to large programs

name: Rose Chart
objective: Identify knowledge gaps and create learning focus areas for team members
description: Rose Charts are visual artifacts to display personal competencies in key learning areas in order to highlight strengths and opportunities for up-skilling. They help teams understand where specific skills reside and identify areas for growth and mentoring.

activities.name: Define Competency Categories
activities.description: Identify key competency areas for self-evaluation and label the Category sections of the circular spider chart template.

activities.name: Distribute Templates
activities.description: Provide physical or digital copies of the Rose Chart template to every team member.

activities.name: Conduct Self-Assessment
activities.description: Team members rate their own competency levels within each predefined category using the provided key.

activities.name: Identify Personal Growth Areas
activities.description: Team members fill in the blank category sections with specific skills or areas they personally wish to up-skill in.

activities.name: Review and Plan
activities.description: Present charts to the team to facilitate mentoring pairs or provide them to team leaders for creating tailored learning plans.

activities.name: Periodic Updates
activities.description: Update the charts on a regular cadence (weekly, monthly, or quarterly) to track and visualize growth over time.

completion_criteria: All team members have completed their self-assessment, identified personal growth goals, and shared their results for team or leadership review.

metrics.name: Competency Growth
metrics.formula: Current Competency Rating - Previous Competency Rating

metrics.name: Skill Gap Reduction
metrics.formula: (Number of growth areas addressed / Total growth areas identified) * 100

pitfalls: Inconsistent updates leading to outdated skill profiles
Subjective bias in self-assessment without peer validation
Failing to act on the identified growth opportunities
benefits: Identifies potential mentors within the group
Provides data for tailored individual learning plans

name: Rose, Thorn, Bud
objective: Understand what's working, what's not, and areas of opportunity
description: Rose, Thorn, Bud (RTB) is a team practice born in the Design Thinking community. It is extremely versatile and can be used as a team retrospective, customer journey analysis, or in conjunction with an ideation session to help prioritize ideas for development. Participants share their thoughts about a particular topic and list ideas or thoughts on different-colored stickies that define that thought/idea as something that isn't working well, something that IS working well, and ideas that have a lot of potential if further developed.

activities.name: Prepare the Board
activities.description: On a whiteboard or digital whiteboarding tool, create 3 columns and label them 'Rose,' 'Thorn,' and 'Bud.'

activities.name: Define the Topic
activities.description: Write the topic of analysis above the columns to give the team direction (e.g. 'How did this last week go as a team?').

activities.name: Distribute Materials
activities.description: Give participants 3 pads of stickies (one red, one blue, and one green) and a sharpie.

activities.name: Silent Generation
activities.description: Give the team 10-15 minutes of silent generation to write down ideas/thoughts on appropriately-colored stickies (Red = rose, Blue = thorn, Green = bud). One idea per stickie.

activities.name: Share and Post
activities.description: Have participants put their stickies up on the board in the appropriate column.

activities.name: Cluster and Label
activities.description: Cluster stickies based on themes and label these themed categories using affinity mapping.

activities.name: Identify Actions
activities.description: Review stickies as a team and identify action-items that can help improve work going forward based on thorns and buds.

activities.name: Assign and Follow-up
activities.description: Assign action items to team members and set up a follow-up meeting to discuss progress/results.

completion_criteria: Action items are assigned to team members and a follow-up meeting is scheduled.

guidelines.name: Atomic Object Blog: Rose, Bud, Thorn
Rose, Thorn, Bud (RTB): A 5-minute Game to Kickstart Your Mindfulness Habit
A Mindful Way to Reflect: Rose, Thorn, and Bud

benefits: Collaboratively prioritize what to do next
Allows everyone on the team to participate in sharing and analyzing
Versatile application for retrospectives or customer journeys

name: Scenario Casting
objective: Get a handle on a complex and elusive domain by structuring projects through domain-driven scenarios.
description: Scenario Casting is a collaborative tool for structuring software development projects driven by domain expertise expressed in scenarios. It is especially useful for complex or elusive domains involving many participants. A scenario is an outline of an interesting situation that implies a certain outcome, described strictly from a domain perspective in pure domain language. It is concrete, detailed, and distinctive without prescribing a solution.

activities.name: Brainstorm Scenario Backlog
activities.description: Brainstorm example scenarios of how individual ideas, questions, and concerns affect the domain using strictly domain language.

activities.name: Prioritize and Scope
activities.description: Prioritize the Scenario Backlog and agree on the project or iteration scope.

activities.name: Create Orientation Scenarios
activities.description: Combine the top-priority scenarios into coherent overarching Orientation Scenarios.

activities.name: Collaborative Modeling
activities.description: Flesh out and tell the stories of the Orientation Scenarios using methods like Domain Storytelling or Event Storming.

completion_criteria: A clear overview of the domain is achieved and a focus is set for everyone involved in the iteration.

pitfalls: Prescribing a solution instead of describing the domain situation
Using technical jargon instead of pure domain language
Creating scenarios that are too abstract or generic
benefits: Helps learn about people, concerns, conventions, and policies
Identifies connections and borders between different domain areas
Generates ideas for future project iterations
Creates a shared understanding between stakeholders and technical teams

name: Scrum
objective: Deliver value to the customer early and often through iterative and incremental delivery.
description: Scrum is a framework that embodies Agile and makes the principles a reality through a group of practices. It is designed to help teams work together more effectively and efficiently by providing a set of roles, events, and artifacts that facilitate collaboration and continuous improvement.

activities.name: Sprint Planning
activities.description: Commit to a set of defined work as a team for the upcoming sprint cycle and create the Sprint Backlog.

activities.name: Daily Standup
activities.description: Daily sync to coordinate team effort, report on progress of work, and communicate problems and improvements.

activities.name: Sprint
activities.description: A time-boxed event that moves the team towards the goal set for that iteration.

activities.name: Sprint Review
activities.description: Review what was completed during the sprint with stakeholders to collect feedback on the increment.

activities.name: Sprint Retrospective
activities.description: Team reflection on internal processes with the goal to continuously improve.

completion_criteria: Successful delivery of a Product Increment at the end of a time-boxed Sprint and completion of the Retrospective.

metrics.name: Productivity
metrics.formula: Items Completed / Items Planned

pitfalls: Sticking to a rigid plan instead of adapting
Lack of self-organization within the development team
Failure to prioritize based on customer value
benefits: Ensure building the right thing through feedback

name: Security checklist
objective: Shift left security by running through a checklist during code review to improve security posture and code quality.
description: The Security Checklist is a tool created to improve security culture in dev teams and help them consistently check their code for common security risks. The tool is intended for use as part of a software team’s code review process, ensuring that vulnerabilities are discovered early when they are cheaper and easier to fix.

activities.name: Pre-push cleanup
activities.description: The author removes all secrets, including passwords, keys, and tokens, from the committed code before pushing to the repository.

activities.name: Reviewer Engagement
activities.description: Ensure the right people are engaged for the review and that the purpose of the change is clearly stated and understood.

activities.name: Technical Security Inspection
activities.description: Reviewers check for debug functionality, user-supplied data validation, log entry quality, dependency vetting, and secure response messaging.

activities.name: Testing Verification
activities.description: Testers verify that test coverage is sufficient and that misuse cases are represented in the test suite.

activities.name: Risk Documentation
activities.description: Raise and document any unresolved risks discovered during the review before marking the process as complete.

completion_criteria: The checklist is fully completed, all items are addressed, and any unresolved risks are documented in the team's risk log.

metrics.name: Security Vulnerabilities Found
metrics.formula: Total number of security-related issues identified during the checklist process

metrics.name: Checklist Completion Rate
metrics.formula: (Number of code reviews with completed checklists / Total number of code reviews) * 100

guidelines.name: Code Review Security Checklist Implementation Manual
Agile Application Security: Enabling Security in a Continuous Delivery Pipeline

pitfalls: Using the checklist as a standalone teaching tool without context
Treating the checklist as an accountability or punishment mechanism
Removing safety steps because they are difficult to perform
Failing to involve the entire team in checklist modifications
benefits: Improves security culture within development teams
Identifies vulnerabilities early in the lifecycle
Reduces the cost and complexity of fixing security bugs
Provides a consistent framework for checking common risks

name: Service Blueprint
objective: A business process diagram analyzed from the people perspective to holistically dissect operational workflows and business structures.
description: A Service Blueprint is an applied process chart that maps a particular operational workflow or product offering from a people-centric perspective. It provides empathy and shared understanding of user, business, technical, and support actions across several layers: Physical Evidence, People Actions, Front-stage Actions, Back-stage Actions, and Support Channels. It is read chronologically from left to right and uses specific lines (Interaction, Visibility, Internal Interaction) to separate different domains of the service delivery.

activities.name: Preparation and Scoping
activities.description: Identify primary users and focus on one main process prioritized by the top user or business problems to be solved. Book a work area with a large whiteboard or wall.

activities.name: Template Construction
activities.description: Create a large physical template grid with sections for Physical Evidence, People, Front-stage, Back-stage, and Support Channels, separated by defined lines of interaction and visibility.

activities.name: Team Alignment
activities.description: Gather the project team, walk through the template, and ensure everyone understands the 'Live Theater' metaphor (actors, props, scenes, backstage) to contextualize the activity.

activities.name: Define Process and Actors
activities.description: Align on the specific business process to be dissected and reach a consensus on the people (primary and secondary users) involved in the actions.

activities.name: Step-by-Step Mapping
activities.description: Facilitate the mapping by vertical slices, focusing on one step at a time. Document physical evidence, user actions, front-stage tools (interfaces), back-stage technical requirements (databases/servers), and support channels.

activities.name: Visualization and Refinement
activities.description: Use drawings and color-coding to highlight pain points or opportunities. Digitize the blueprint to preserve it as a point of reference and update it based on subsequent research findings.

completion_criteria: A completed blueprint chart (physical or digital) representing the end-to-end service process, with all stages, technical actions, and support channels documented and aligned upon by the team.

metrics.name: Phase Duration
metrics.formula: Estimated time for a user to complete all steps within a specific phase

metrics.name: Discovery Yield
metrics.formula: Total number of unknown risks or process gaps identified during the session

guidelines.name: Rethinking Service Blueprints for Agile Delivery

pitfalls: Including too many users at once, making the blueprint overly complex
Failing to digitize the physical board, leading to loss of information
Not updating the blueprint after conducting user research
Jumping to solutions before mapping the 'As-is' state
benefits: Aligns end-to-end perspectives on a service experience
Identifies process problem areas and growth opportunities
Envisions customer flow and architecture of service experience
Makes abstract concepts tangible through visualization

name: Service Level Indicators
objective: Quantify the quality of your service from the user's perspective to drive data-driven decision making.
description: Service Level Indicators (SLIs) are meaningful measurements of a service's performance related to what users value, such as availability, latency, or response time. In Site Reliability Engineering, SLIs provide the quantitative data necessary to set Service Level Objectives (SLOs) and manage error budgets, enabling teams to balance reliability with new feature development.

activities.name: Define user-centric availability
activities.description: Identify critical user journeys and ask what the user hopes to accomplish to define what 'good' performance or 'availability' means to them.

activities.name: Map system components
activities.description: Define system boundaries and infrastructure components that interact with each user journey to identify where capabilities are exposed.

activities.name: Define SLIs per component
activities.description: Identify specific measurements (e.g., errors per day, latency per hour) for each component that reflect the user's definition of success over time.

activities.name: Determine data points
activities.description: Select telemetry data and metrics closest to the user to ensure the measurements are representative of their actual experience.

activities.name: Create a baseline
activities.description: Begin measuring immediately to establish a performance baseline, even before making improvements, to enable informed decision-making.

activities.name: Observe and iterate
activities.description: Track SLIs over a period (e.g., 1-2 weeks) and correlate them with user happiness, refining the indicators as understanding of the service grows.

completion_criteria: Critical user journeys are mapped, SLIs are defined and baselined, and a dashboard is created to visualize service quality over time.

metrics.name: Availability (Success Rate)
metrics.formula: (Successful Requests / Total Requests) * 100

metrics.name: Latency Compliance
metrics.formula: (Requests meeting response time threshold / Total Requests) * 100

guidelines.name: Best Practices for Modern Complex Systems

pitfalls: Defining too many SLIs, which can cause distraction
Measuring specific moments instead of performance over a time window
Delaying baseline measurement to wait for performance fixes
benefits: Informed prioritization of reliability vs. features
Enables continuous improvement through historical comparison
Aligns technical metrics with user experience

name: SMART Goals
objective: Setting SMART objectives ensure that your goals hit 5 key criteria, to ensure progress.
description: Smart Goals are a goal-setting framework introduced by George T. Doran in 1981. It serves as a guideline to improve the chances of succeeding in accomplishing goals by focusing on the 'what' rather than the 'how' through five specific criteria: Specific, Measurable, Attainable, Realistic, and Time-related.

activities.name: Draft Initial Goal
activities.description: Start with a basic version of the goal (e.g., 'Better typing').

activities.name: Make it Specific
activities.description: Refine the goal so everyone understands exactly what it is (e.g., 'Increase typing speed').

activities.name: Make it Measurable
activities.description: Define how progress will be measured (e.g., 'Increase typing speed to 100 wpm').

activities.name: Ensure it is Attainable
activities.description: Relate the goal to specific circumstances and individuals.

activities.name: Ensure it is Realistic
activities.description: Verify that the goal is not out of reach and can be achieved (e.g., adjusting '100 wpm' to '75 wpm' if more realistic).

activities.name: Set Time-related parameters
activities.description: Establish a dedicated time frame so the goal is not open-ended (e.g., 'in 3 months').

completion_criteria: The goal satisfies all five criteria: it is Specific, Measurable, Attainable, Realistic, and Time-related.

metrics.name: Goal Attainment
metrics.formula: Actual Result / Targeted SMART Goal Result

guidelines.name: The Ultimate Guide to SMART Goals

pitfalls: Open-ended goals with no timeframe
Goals that are out of reach or unrealistic
Vague goals that not everyone understands
benefits: Ensures everyone understands what the goal is

name: Spaghetti Wall
objective: An always-open space for collaborating on new problems and ideas.
description: The Spaghetti Wall is a physical or digital workspace that is set up as a place to capture budding ideas and challenges in your organization. It serves as a designated space where problems and aha moments can be captured at any time and organically collaborated on by others in the organization to see what sticks.

activities.name: Setup the Workspace
activities.description: On a large, physical wall space, rolling whiteboard, or digital tool (Miro, MURAL), label the Spaghetti Wall in a location it can occupy indefinitely.

activities.name: Onboard the Team
activities.description: Communicate the purpose of the wall to the organization and encourage colleagues to add ideas or challenges using one sticky note per item.

activities.name: Capture Ideas and Problems
activities.description: When encountering pain points or innovative ideas during daily work, add them to the wall. Reach out to specific individuals to add their thoughts.

activities.name: Organic Collaboration
activities.description: In spare time, team members visit the wall to read through items and add clarifying questions or follow-up ideas to help them mature.

activities.name: Refine and Converge
activities.description: If an idea gains traction, use dot-voting or specific solution statements to refine the focus and hone in on the most promising solutions.

activities.name: Transition to Prototyping
activities.description: Once an idea is mature enough, create a Lean Canvas or pitch deck to present to leadership for feedback and rapid prototyping.

activities.name: Board Management
activities.description: Assign a small group to manage the wall, which includes adding leadership challenges, removing stale ideas, and moving mature ideas into development.

completion_criteria: The practice is ongoing; success is defined by ideas progressing to prototypes or being removed if they are no longer relevant, maintaining an active and updated space.

metrics.name: Idea Conversion
metrics.formula: (Mature Ideas / Total Ideas Posted) * 100

guidelines.name: Wall of Ideas: How to Create a Thousand Ideas in No Time

pitfalls: Hard time organically progressing an idea without structure
Ideas becoming stale or unmanaged
Lack of engagement during spare time
Vague problem statements that lack a clear user segment
benefits: Visualizes problems and ideas for organizational transparency
Ensures continuous seeking of ways to improve working methods
Strengthens a culture of innovation and collaboration
Provides an outlet for problem-solvers to drive organizational impact

name: Split Testing - A/B Testing
objective: Quantitative testing of two alternative designs to learn which better leads to a specific goal
description: This is a randomised experiment in which we compare and evaluate the performance of different versions of a product in pairs. Both product versions are available in production (live) and randomly provided to different users. Data is being collected about the traffic, interaction, time spent and other relevant metrics, which will be used to judge the effectiveness of the two different versions based on the change in user’s behaviour.

activities.name: Establish Target Outcomes
activities.description: Identify the specific performance goals or target outcomes you intend to improve.

activities.name: Create Pairs
activities.description: Prepare two versions of the product that are identical except for a single characteristic which will vary.

activities.name: Simultaneous Deployment
activities.description: Deploy both versions to production simultaneously to ensure external factors vary consistently across both groups.

activities.name: Data Collection
activities.description: Collect data regarding traffic, interaction, and time spent for both versions.

activities.name: Compare and Evaluate
activities.description: Evaluate the conversion or activity data between the two versions to determine which performs better.

completion_criteria: A significant difference between the versions is identified or a decision is made based on target outcome performance.

metrics.name: Conversion Rate
metrics.formula: Successful Actions / Total Users

metrics.name: User Activity
metrics.formula: Total Interactions per version

guidelines.name: The Ultimate Guide to A/B Testing
Blue-green Deployments, A/B Testing, and Canary Releases
The Lean Startup by Eric Ries

pitfalls: Failing to differentiate cohorts (new vs. returning users)
Versions differing too much (more than a single characteristic)
Tests not run simultaneously
Insignificant results requiring longer run times
benefits: Simple to apply and fast to execute
Provides objective criteria for design decisions
Allows for 'tuning the engine' as per Lean Startup
Enhances user experience and organizational results

name: Split Testing - Multivari Testing
objective: Quantitatively test how multiple factors influence the success of a design
description: Multivari (Multivariate or Multi-variable) testing is a product testing approach that allows teams to understand the influence of multiple different factors on product performance simultaneously. Unlike A/B testing, it analyzes how different combinations of factors (such as features or design elements) achieve the best performance and identifies potential reinforcement between those factors using statistical analysis.

activities.name: Identify Factors
activities.description: Determine the multiple different factors and values that potentially influence the performance of the product.

activities.name: Design Product Versions
activities.description: Design different versions of the product that differ from each other on multiple different factors based on Full Factorial, Fractional Factorial, or Taguchi testing methods.

activities.name: Traffic Redirection
activities.description: Randomly redirect customers to the different product versions in a production environment.

activities.name: Data Collection
activities.description: Collect raw data from customer interactions with the various versions.

activities.name: Statistical Analysis
activities.description: Perform a statistical analysis of variance (ANOVA or MANOVA) over the collected data to understand the impact of each factor and their interplay.

completion_criteria: A statistically significant amount of data is collected and analyzed to provide representative results for the whole population, identifying the optimal combination of factors.

metrics.name: Statistical Significance
metrics.formula: Standard ANOVA/MANOVA variance calculation

metrics.name: Interaction Volume
metrics.formula: Total interactions per product variant

guidelines.name: Multivariate Testing 101: A Scientific Method Of Optimizing Design
Multivariate Testing In Action: Five Simple Steps To Increase Conversion Rates

pitfalls: Insufficient transactions or interactions to achieve statistical significance
Using results that are not representative of the whole population due to small sample size
Complexity of managing multiple product versions simultaneously
benefits: Assess impacts of multiple factors simultaneously
Identify interplay and reinforcement between factors
Saves time and effort compared to multiple sequential A/B tests
Informs product pivot or development phases with quantitative evidence

name: Stacey Matrix
objective: Decide on a management approach by evaluating the level of agreement and certainty.
description: The Stacey Matrix is a visual representation of different approaches to management based on the level of agreement and certainty within a group or organization. It helps to identify the most suitable approach, such as a command and control approach, a consensus-based approach, or a more organic and adaptive approach.

activities.name: Identify the Decision or Change
activities.description: Clearly define the specific decision or change that needs to be made.

activities.name: Assess Certainty
activities.description: Determine the level of certainty about the situation. Evaluate if there is a high degree of certainty about the outcomes or if the future is uncertain.

activities.name: Assess Agreement
activities.description: Determine the level of agreement among stakeholders regarding the decision or change.

activities.name: Plot the Situation
activities.description: Plot the decision or change on the Stacey Matrix based on the levels of certainty and agreement.

activities.name: Select the Appropriate Approach
activities.description: Choose the most suitable approach: Command and control (High Certainty/Agreement), Consensus-based (High Certainty/Low Agreement), Organic/Adaptive (Low Certainty/High Agreement), or Experimentation (Low Certainty/Low Agreement).

activities.name: Implement and Monitor
activities.description: Implement the chosen approach and monitor its effectiveness, being prepared to adapt as needed.

completion_criteria: The decision or change has been plotted on the matrix and a management approach has been selected and agreed upon by stakeholders.

metrics.name: Consensus Level
metrics.formula: (Number of stakeholders in agreement / Total stakeholders) * 100

guidelines.name: Understanding The Stacey Matrix & How It Helps Manage Complexities
Exploring the Stacey Matrix: Navigating complexity with clarity

pitfalls: Misinterpreting the levels of uncertainty as purely technical
Ignoring dissenting voices when assessing agreement
Using the matrix as a rigid rule rather than a guide

name: Start At The End
objective: Identify a set of assumptions which must be tested in order achieve a long term goal by defining what success looks like.
description: Start At The End is an exercise to identify assumptions that must be tested to achieve long-term goals. Participants describe success for their work (typically on a 12-month horizon) and use this to define the goal of the effort. The team then imagines potential failure scenarios to enumerate and prioritize key questions or assumptions that need to be examined early in the work. This technique is derived from The Sprint Book.

activities.name: Define Success
activities.description: Identify what success looks like from the perspective of both the customer and the business, typically looking 12 months into the future.

activities.name: Identify Potential Failures
activities.description: Imagine all the ways the project can fail to uncover fears and hidden risks within the domain.

activities.name: Enumerate Key Questions
activities.description: Using the results from the failure exercise, list key questions or assumptions that must be examined at the start of the work.

activities.name: Prioritize Questions
activities.description: Rank the key questions to identify which assumptions are most critical to examine early to ensure the team achieves the goal.

completion_criteria: A shared understanding of the goal is achieved and a prioritized list of critical assumptions has been created for early examination.

pitfalls: Juggling a shopping list of features instead of business objectives
Failing to keep the results visible in the project room
Avoiding the discussion of failure/fears
Lack of involvement from technical or business sponsors
benefits: Puts business value at the center of the work
Yields high domain knowledge insights through fear sharing
Creates alignment on the long-term mission

name: Start With Why
objective: Connect to your purpose and discover the core reason an organization or individual exists.
description: Start with why is a practice to discover your organisation's or your personal why. It is a process of discovery, not invention, aimed at finding the deeper meaning behind activities to drive belief, engagement, and trust. By aligning 'Why', 'How', and 'What', organizations can inspire people and create a powerful differentiator in the market.

activities.name: Discovery
activities.description: Look inward to identify the core purpose or 'Why' that already exists within the individual or organization rather than trying to invent it.

activities.name: Aligning the Golden Circle
activities.description: Align the Why (purpose), How (process), and What (product/service) to build trust and consistency across all communications and actions.

activities.name: Defining Vision and Mission
activities.description: Formulate a Vision statement based on the founder's intent (Why) and a Mission statement describing how the company will create that future (How).

completion_criteria: The core 'Why' is identified and documented through Vision and Mission statements that are clearly understood and used to guide decision-making.

metrics.name: Employee Engagement
metrics.formula: Engagement Survey Result

metrics.name: Retention Rate
metrics.formula: ((Employees at End - New Hires) / Employees at Start) * 100

guidelines.name: Simon Sinek: How great leaders inspire action
Drive: The Surprising Truth About What Motivates Us

pitfalls: Defining the 'Why' in dry, self-serving terms like 'maximizing shareholder value'
Treating the process as an invention of a new slogan rather than a discovery of existing purpose
Failing to remain true to the 'Why' after it has been identified
Focusing on features and competition ('What' culture) rather than purpose
benefits: Increases employee performance by providing purpose, autonomy, and mastery
Differentiates the organization in crowded markets
Builds trust with customers and employees
Provides clarity for long-term vision and leadership roles

name: Stop the World Event
objective: Empower team members to stop everything when they feel engagement is off course to find a compromise and maintain progress towards customer success.
description: A 'Stop the World' event occurs when a team member identifies that the current engagement is off-course. The entire team halts work to collaboratively find an acceptable compromise, identify necessary changes or actions with specific owners, and ensure psychological safety is maintained throughout the process.

activities.name: Identify Off-Course Engagement
activities.description: A team member recognizes that the engagement is no longer aligned with customer success or agreed-upon outcomes.

activities.name: Halt Progress
activities.description: The team stops all current activities to focus entirely on addressing the identified issue.

activities.name: Collaborative Problem Solving
activities.description: The full team works together to find a compromise or solution that is acceptable to all participants.

activities.name: Define Actions and Owners
activities.description: Identify specific actions or changes required to move forward and assign owners to each task.

completion_criteria: The team reaches consensus on a proposed compromise, identifies specific actions and owners, and maintains psychological safety.

guidelines.name: High Performing Teams Need Psychological Safety

benefits: Allows for rapid adaptation to new information
Responds to fundamental shifts in customer priority
Maintains team psychological safety and engagement

name: Story Kick-offs
objective: To refresh the need and perform an in-depth analysis or validation of a user story immediately before development begins.
description: A joint walkthrough and explanation of a user story by the Product Owner with the specific pair of developers who will be working on it. This occurs right before development starts to ensure the team has the necessary context, answers to technical questions, and a final check on scope changes.

activities.name: Preparation
activities.description: The Product Owner ensures the story is ready with clear goals, users, value, acceptance criteria, assumptions, and necessary mock-ups or scripts.

activities.name: Story Narrative Walkthrough
activities.description: The Product Owner walks the developers and Quality Analysts through the actual story narrative, explaining context and acceptance criteria.

activities.name: Review Supporting Artifacts
activities.description: Use whiteboards, sequence diagrams, or the application itself to visualize the explanation and ensure technical understanding.

activities.name: Question and Answer Session
activities.description: Developers and QAs ask questions to clarify analysis. Any modifications to the story or conversation outcomes are captured for later reference.

activities.name: Address Roadblocks
activities.description: Identify questions requiring further stakeholder input. Decide whether to proceed with a reduced scope or park the story until answers are found.

completion_criteria: The pair of developers has a clear understanding of the story scope, acceptance criteria are validated, and the story is task out for development.

pitfalls: Conducting kick-offs at the end of the day or right before lunch when focus is diluted
Starting a kick-off before developers have finished their current story
Incomplete analysis by the Product Owner prior to the meeting
benefits: Reduces loss of context caused by time lag between planning and execution
Provides an opportunity for deep-dive analysis that may have been skipped in IPM
Ensures the team is aware of any context changes since the story was first written

name: Story RePointing
objective: Improve Story Pointing accuracy by reconciling estimated versus actual story sizes after completion.
description: Story RePointing is used to reconcile the size of a story completed by your team after the sprint is over. Teams will make new story point estimates based on the actual work done to complete a story, then compare that to the original estimation and calculate an Accuracy Differential for each completed story. Individuals can talk about why they thought a story was larger/smaller than originally estimated.

activities.name: Prepare Grid
activities.description: Draw a grid on a whiteboard including columns for Story Identifiers, Reviewed Value, Original Value, and Differential.

activities.name: Distribute Sizing Materials
activities.description: Distribute Value Cards (such as Point Cards or Sizing Cards) to all team members.

activities.name: Conceal Original Data
activities.description: Conceal the Original Values from the team using Post-Its or by marking them in after the review.

activities.name: Share Story Details
activities.description: Share screens with the team via laptops or TV so all members can read the details of each story without seeing prior pointing details.

activities.name: Re-Estimate Story
activities.description: Give the team one minute to read a story, then ask everyone to simultaneously hold up their chosen Value Card.

activities.name: Record Reviewed Value
activities.description: Average the Value Cards shown by the team and record it in the Reviewed Value column.

activities.name: Calculate Differential
activities.description: Display the Original Value and calculate the Differential between the new and original estimates.

activities.name: Facilitate Discussion
activities.description: Ask the individuals who completed the story and those with differing valuations to explain why they felt the story was larger or smaller than originally estimated.

activities.name: Iterate through Stories
activities.description: Continue the process until all stories from the sprint have been RePointed.

activities.name: Log Results
activities.description: Record all data in an Excel Estimation Tracker to monitor accuracy over time.

completion_criteria: All stories from the completed sprint have been reviewed, re-pointed, and the differentials have been recorded in the tracking tool.

metrics.name: Accuracy Differential
metrics.formula: Reviewed Value - Original Value

pitfalls: Failing to conceal original values which biases the re-pointing
Not allowing enough time for team members to read story details
Skipping the discussion phase regarding why valuations differed
benefits: Keeps teams conscious about their pointing decisions
Stimulates team discussion and shared understanding of work size

name: Storytelling Framework
objective: Morph ideas into impactful, emotive stories to resonate with your audience and ensure they connect, care, and remember the message.
description: The Storytelling Framework is a structured approach to transforming messages into compelling narratives. It guides individuals or teams through defining a core message, structuring the narrative flow, mapping emotional journeys, and selecting visual aids to maximize communication effectiveness and team alignment.

activities.name: Build your story
activities.description: Define the core message, objective, target audience, supporting data, obstacles, and a supporting experience or story.

activities.name: Design your telling
activities.description: Structure content into sections such as Opening, Key Message, Change/Impact, Story, Data, and Closing, mapping the content gathered in step one to these sections.

activities.name: Reflect the emotions
activities.description: Assign an intended emotion to each section to create an emotional journey for the audience, ensuring they engage and care about the message.

activities.name: Make it tangible
activities.description: Map specific visual styles or images to each section to create a consistent look and feel for the final output.

activities.name: Select medium and convert
activities.description: Choose the best format (e.g., blog, video, presentation) based on how the audience consumes information and convert the structured content into that format.

work_products: Final Communication Artifact (Video, Blog, or Presentation)
completion_criteria: The story is structured across defined sections, includes a mapped emotional journey, and is finalized in a chosen distribution format.

metrics.name: Audience Engagement
metrics.formula: Number of active responses / Total audience reached

metrics.name: Message Recall
metrics.formula: Number of participants who can articulate the core message / Total participants surveyed

guidelines.name: Shapes of stories, by Kurt Vonnegut
6 ways to make stories stick & spread
Made to stick, by Chip & Dan Heath

pitfalls: Solely providing information without emotional connection
Losing the audience in excessive data
Creating a 'flat' narrative without varying emotions
Ignoring the opening and closing sections which are the most memorable
benefits: Makes audiences care about the message
Provides a clear structure for brainstorming

name: System Context Mapping
objective: Understand and model the scope and environment a solution will operate in, i.e. systems and actors the solution will be interfacing with.
description: System Context is a way to capture, describe and communicate our understanding of a systems boundary and environment it is operating in. It consists of a simple to understand diagram where the system is treated as a black box, and optionally a table with additional information. The focus is reasoning about the system’s context rather than its inner workings.

activities.name: Discovery Conversation
activities.description: Listen to or participate in discovery conversations with project stakeholders to gather context about the solution.

activities.name: Visualize the System Context
activities.description: Participants visualize their understanding by drawing the system under consideration in the center, surrounding it with external systems and human actors, and connecting them with lines representing data flows.

activities.name: Discussion and Alignment
activities.description: Exchange drawings with other participants to discuss commonalities and differences in understanding until a common view is reached.

activities.name: Capture Additional Details
activities.description: Once the diagram stabilizes, capture detailed metadata in a tabular format, such as System Owner contact info, API links, or LDAP queries for actors.

completion_criteria: A common understanding is achieved among participants and the context diagram is stabilized.

guidelines.name: Wikipedia article on System Context Diagrams
C4 Model Description of the System Context
Arc42 section on capturing the system context

pitfalls: Adding details about the system’s internals (avoiding the black box approach)
Depicting end-to-end process flows between external systems which clutters the diagram
Confusing System Context Mapping with high-level Architectural Overviews (System Diagrams)
benefits: Helps understand system boundaries (what is in and out of scope)
Facilitates reasoning about interfaces to surrounding systems and actors
Assists in compiling a database of related information like system ownership and lifecycles
Ensures interaction diagrams remain in sync with the external environment

name: System Diagram
objective: A method for describing a system at the highest level possible to ensure a clear, complete, and common understanding.
description: A system diagram is a simple and very high-level description of a system that exists or needs to be built. It is designed to be drawn collaboratively in a short amount of time to help teams navigate complexity by identifying the system's purpose, boundaries, inputs, outputs, subsystems, and feedback loops.

activities.name: Gather the team
activities.description: Gather the team around a blank whiteboard or collaborative digital diagramming tool.

activities.name: Draw the System Boundary
activities.description: Begin by drawing a large box in the middle of the canvas to represent the system's boundary.

activities.name: Define the Purpose
activities.description: As a team, define the main purpose of the system in one brief sentence and write it above the box. If it cannot be defined in one sentence, the system may be too complex.

activities.name: Identify Inputs
activities.description: Define one or more inputs that the system will receive from external actors.

activities.name: Identify Outputs
activities.description: Define one or more outputs that the system will provide to external actors.

activities.name: Map Subsystems
activities.description: Define one or more subsystems inside the boundary that typically process inputs or perform other tasks.

activities.name: Establish Feedback Loops
activities.description: Define the feedback loops the system has in place to continuously improve itself.

completion_criteria: All components (Purpose, System Boundary, Inputs, Outputs, Subsystems, and Feedback Loops) are defined and the team reaches a common understanding.

pitfalls: Struggling to define the Purpose in just one sentence indicates the system is too complex and should be split into multiple diagrams.
benefits: Provides common understanding across technical and non-technical stakeholders
Can be created quickly and collaboratively

name: Systemic Consensing
objective: Find optimal decisions in groups of any size without conflict by focusing on the proposal with the least resistance.
description: Systemic Consensing (SC) aims at the consideration of all voices of a group, including those of the reserved or silent group members. Instead of focusing on enthusiasm, it focuses on the objections ('resistance'). Consensus is reached by choosing the proposal that produces the least dissatisfaction and conflict potential, making it most likely to provide a viable solution for the task at hand.

activities.name: Determine the Question
activities.description: Formulate a question for the problem that cannot be answered with a simple yes or no.

activities.name: Creative Phase: Collecting Solutions
activities.description: Brainstorm and list solution proposals. All ideas are recorded without comment or discussion. Include a 'default option' like 'leave everything as it is' to act as a limit of reasonability.

activities.name: Assessment Phase
activities.description: Each group member assigns Resistance points (R-points) to each proposal, where 0 means 'no resistance' and 10 means 'strong resistance'.

activities.name: Evaluation Phase
activities.description: Sum the points for each proposal. The proposal with the lowest score represents the least resistance and is closest to consensus.

activities.name: Iteration and Refinement
activities.description: For complex scenarios, analyze high resistance scores to identify concerns, refine proposals, and repeat steps 2-4 until a suitable solution is found.

completion_criteria: A solution proposal with the lowest cumulative resistance score is identified and accepted by the group.

metrics.name: Resistance Score
metrics.formula: Sum of R-points per proposal

guidelines.name: Das SK Prinzip - Wie man Konflikte ohne Machtkämpfe löst

pitfalls: Consensus by fatigue or resignation
Dominance by a single person ('the boss decides')
Tactical voting strategies
Majority voting ignoring valuable concerns
benefits: Produces the least dissatisfaction in the group
Highly scalable for any number of participants and proposals

name: Target Outcomes
objective: Capture and articulate the goals and outcomes a team are striving to deliver
description: A tool to capture and articulate the goals and outcomes a team are striving to deliver. It summarizes findings from discovery practices into finite statements that can be publicly displayed. It focuses on measurable customer and business impacts rather than just technical outputs.

activities.name: Preparation
activities.description: Prepare a whiteboard template in advance with title, steps, and clear examples of expected outcomes.

activities.name: Identify Goals
activities.description: Apply the 'Start At The End' practice to identify the desired end state.

activities.name: Group Topics
activities.description: Use 'Affinity Mapping' to group topics and identify core themes from the discovery phase.

activities.name: Define SMART Goals
activities.description: Summarize the findings into Target Outcomes and write a SMART goal to serve as a starting point for Impact Mapping.

completion_criteria: Target outcomes are articulated as SMART goals and publicly displayed for the team.

metrics.name: Outcome Focus Ratio
metrics.formula: Number of outcomes identified / Total work items

pitfalls: Focusing purely on outputs (tasks) rather than measurable impacts
Lack of clarity in preparation before the working session
Insufficient use of examples during facilitation
benefits: Provides a visual reminder for prioritization
Shifts team focus toward measurable customer and business value

name: Team Empowerment Agreement
objective: Accelerate confident decision-making and reduce bottlenecks by clarifying boundaries to foster autonomy, trust, and accountability.
description: Empowerment Agreements are co-created understandings between managers/leaders and their teams that clarify decision-making boundaries, check-in points, and definitions of success. These agreements foster autonomy, mutual trust, and aligned accountability, enabling faster, more confident decision-making and reducing dependency bottlenecks.

activities.name: Set the Context
activities.description: Explain the intent to enable clear, shared understanding of empowerment to ensure clarity and freedom to act without hesitation.

activities.name: Co-create the Agreement
activities.description: Facilitate a structured conversation addressing independent decisions, check-in points, and success measures.

activities.name: Document and Share
activities.description: Capture the collective agreements into a lightweight (max 2 pages) document or visual format accessible to everyone.

activities.name: Review and Adapt
activities.description: Schedule regular touch-points, such as quarterly reviews, to adjust the agreement as trust deepens and the team matures.

completion_criteria: A documented agreement exists, is accessible to the team, and includes defined success measures and a review schedule.

metrics.name: Speed of Delivery
metrics.formula: Cycle Time

metrics.name: Team Morale
metrics.formula: Average of team survey results

metrics.name: OKR Achievement
metrics.formula: Items Completed / Target Items

pitfalls: Framing the agreement as a way to police or restrict decisions
Leaders failing to respect and support the agreed-upon autonomy
Keeping the agreement static and not evolving it over time
Creating overly complex or lengthy documentation
benefits: Builds psychological safety by removing ambiguity
Improves speed and quality of decision-making

name: Team Sentiment
objective: Track the mood of a team with visible feedback to identify and address problems early.
description: Team Sentiment practices track the mood of a team and provide a mechanism for team members to rapidly feed back, either publically or anonymously, when their mood has changed. It acts as an information radiator to assess the overall team health and feeling, enabling issues to be identified and addressed early.

activities.name: Introduction
activities.description: Introduce and facilitate the team sentiment practice to the team.

activities.name: Material Setup
activities.description: Place containers or digital tools in a strategic place where all team members see them regularly (e.g., near doors or digital dashboards).

activities.name: Initial Sentiment Log
activities.description: Encourage team members to think about their current mood and add a representative marble or digital marker (e.g., green for positive, red for negative).

activities.name: Ongoing Updates
