activities.description: Team members update their mood markers on an ad-hoc basis as their sentiment changes throughout the project.

completion_criteria: The practice is adopted on an ongoing basis with visible mood trends being used to drive team discussions.

metrics.name: Sentiment Ratio
metrics.formula: (Number of Positive Marbles / Total Marbles) * 100

pitfalls: Inconsistent participation by team members
Lack of psychological safety preventing honest feedback
Failure to act on negative mood trends
benefits: Provides an information radiator for team health
Facilitates quick discussions on team direction

name: Team Workspaces
objective: How to create the right workspace(s) for high-performing teams to foster creativity and collaboration.
description: This practice involves the intentional design of physical or temporary 'pop-up' workspaces to influence team performance. It covers a collection of best practices including open plan layouts, flexible furniture, maximized wall space for visualization, specialized zones for quiet or breakout work, and necessary technical infrastructure like high-bandwidth networking and video conferencing tools.

activities.name: Design Open Plan
activities.description: Establish a workspace that allows for immediate and constant communication, ensuring it is large enough for the whole team and visitors to sit together without barriers.

activities.name: Maximize Wall Space
activities.description: Identify and prepare large amounts of wall or glass space to serve as information radiators for visualizing work, using foils, boards, or sticky notes.

activities.name: Provision Flexible Furniture
activities.description: Equip the space with movable items such as desks on rollers, folding desks, and stackable chairs to allow the team to re-configure the area as needed.

activities.name: Establish Functional Zones
activities.description: Designate specific areas for different needs, including breakout areas for brainstorming, meeting rooms for reviews, quiet zones for focused work, and call booths.

activities.name: Configure Technical Infrastructure
activities.description: Ensure high-bandwidth networking without restrictions, provide dual external screens for developers, and set up 360-degree video cameras for remote inclusivity.

completion_criteria: The physical workspace is configured to support team mobility, work is actively visualized on walls, and all functional zones (quiet, breakout, meeting) are accessible and equipped.

guidelines.name: Make Space - Recommended Stanford d.school book
Blog post about logo and design of Boston space

pitfalls: Noise levels in open plans hindering progress
Confidentiality issues in shared spaces where 3rd parties can overhear/see work
Quiet zones being 'hijacked' by non-team members
Cleaning services displacing or removing post-it notes and artifacts
Network restrictions (VPN blocks or IP limits) disrupting workflow
benefits: Allows for status transparency by 'walking the walls'
Improves developer productivity through external dual screens
Supports physical wellbeing with standing desks
Increases remote worker inclusivity through video cameras

name: Team Story
objective: Trace the evolution of teams within an organization to gain insights into team dynamics, development stages, and interdependencies.
description: Team Story: Tracing the Evolution of Teams is an exercise where participants recount the journey of a specific team from inception to the present. By mapping significant events across development stages like Forming, Storming, Norming, and Performing, teams reflect on how they operate within the organizational ecosystem.

activities.name: Set up the Wall
activities.description: Prepare a wall or board with posters representing team development stages (Creation Process, Forming, Storming, Norming, Performing, Adjourning) and quadrants for 'Things working well' and 'Things not working well'.

activities.name: Introduce the Exercise
activities.description: Explain the purpose of exploring team evolution and emphasize the value of learning from shared experiences.

activities.name: Individual Reflection
activities.description: Participants individually reflect on a specific team and capture observations on sticky notes.

activities.name: Recall Team Journey
activities.description: Instruct participants to recall significant events, challenges, and achievements across the team's history.

activities.name: Write Keywords
activities.description: Participants write keywords or short sentences describing each stage and place them on the corresponding posters.

activities.name: Display Team Stories
activities.description: Participants present their written summaries and share unique experiences or lessons with the group.

activities.name: Group Discussion
activities.description: Facilitate a discussion on similarities and differences between team journeys and their roles in the ecosystem.

activities.name: Reflection and Closing
activities.description: Conclude by emphasizing the application of these lessons to future team-building efforts.

completion_criteria: The exercise is complete when all six stages of the team journey have been populated and the group discussion has finished.

metrics.name: Stage Completion
metrics.formula: Number of populated stages / 6

guidelines.name: Tuckman’s Model and Team Longevity - Tom Geraghty

pitfalls: Participants not having observations for every development section
Focusing only on positive events while ignoring 'Storming' challenges
Dominant voices overshadowing individual team stories
benefits: Gain insights into successful team formation
Identify areas for improvement in team dynamics

name: The Art Gallery
objective: To help encourage and highlight the creativity of the Team!
description: The Art Gallery is a place to capture the various doodles that a Team might draw throughout their time together. This simple practice shows off the creativity of team members by creating a dedicated space for their sketches.

activities.name: Create the Gallery Board
activities.description: Create a board titled "The Art Gallery" to serve as the display area.

activities.name: Collect Doodles
activities.description: Capture team members' doodles using sticky notes or tape and place them on the board.

completion_criteria: A board titled "The Art Gallery" is created and populated with doodles from team members.

benefits: Fosters a creative and collaborative team culture
Provides a fun way to capture team history

name: The Big Picture
objective: To create a shared understanding of a software pipeline by visualizing all steps from source code to the end user.
description: The Big Picture Workshop is a collaborative activity used to visualize every step a software pipeline goes through. It maps the journey of code from its source, through compilation and testing, until it reaches the end user. This practice bridges the gap between technical and business stakeholders, articulating the complexity of continuous delivery using simple tools like stickies and tape.

activities.name: Set up the environment
activities.description: Prepare a large physical space or digital board. Use painters tape or digital borders to draw servers or platforms, such as an OpenShift Cluster, to frame the big picture.

activities.name: Segment namespaces and environments
activities.description: Divide the platform space into segments representing projects or namespaces like CI/CD, development, and test environments. Label them using stickies.

activities.name: Define local environments
activities.description: Add local development environments (e.g., developer laptops) outside or inside the cluster. List and doodle the required local tools like Ansible or NodeJS.

activities.name: Map CI/CD tooling
activities.description: Add specific CI/CD tools (e.g., Jenkins, Tekton) to the relevant namespace. Write a one-line description for each to assist with onboarding.

activities.name: Visualize the pipeline flow
activities.description: Draw the pipeline stages and add low-level detail for each step. Tell the story of code moving from source through builds into the production environment.

completion_criteria: A visual representation of the end-to-end software delivery pipeline is completed and displayed as an information radiator.

metrics.name: Shared Understanding Alignment
metrics.formula: Number of team members who can explain the pipeline / Total team members

guidelines.name: The Big Picture Open Practice Podcast

pitfalls: Hiding the artifact instead of using it as an information radiator
Overcomplicating the initial drawing before the team understands the flow

name: The Matrix of Principles
objective: A reflection tool to capture how stakeholders understand Deming's 14 Management Principles and identify areas for improvement.
description: The Matrix of Principles is a reflective exercise where team members position Deming's 14 Management Principles on a 2x2 matrix. The horizontal axis measures personal agreement with the principle, while the vertical axis measures how effectively the principle is applied within the organization. This helps teams share management beliefs and identify cultural gaps.

activities.name: Preparation
activities.description: Take a blank sheet of paper or use a whiteboard.

activities.name: Matrix Setup
activities.description: Draw a 2x2 matrix. The horizontal axis represents agreement with the principle (agree on the right, disagree on the left). The vertical axis represents application in the organization (applied at the top, not applied at the bottom).

activities.name: Positioning Principles
activities.description: Position Deming's 14 Management Principles by placing their corresponding numbers on the matrix. Each team member uses a different color to distinguish their views.

activities.name: Outlier Discussion
activities.description: The facilitator identifies outliers on the matrix and asks them to explain their position to the group.

activities.name: Group Reflection
activities.description: The facilitator asks the group if the visual mapping inspires new ideas or actions for the team's development.

completion_criteria: The 2x2 matrix is populated with all team members' perspectives and a group discussion on outliers and potential improvements has been completed.

metrics.name: Principle Alignment
metrics.formula: Number of principles with high consensus vs. high variance

guidelines.name: Changing Your Team From The Inside

pitfalls: Participants not understanding the 14 principles before starting
Facilitator influencing members' placement of principles
Failing to discuss the outliers
benefits: Enables team to share beliefs on management
Identifies specific areas for organizational improvement
Visualizes diversity of thought within the team

name: The RICE Scoring Model
objective: Prioritize projects or decisions based on Reach, Impact, Confidence, and Effort.
description: The RICE Scoring Model is a simple technique used to prioritize projects or decisions. It evaluates four factors: Reach (number of people impacted), Impact (severity or importance of effect), Confidence (level of certainty regarding the outcome), and Effort (urgency or importance of the decision compared to others).

activities.name: Define Your Projects or Decisions
activities.description: List all the projects or decisions you need to prioritize. Be clear and concise in describing each option.

activities.name: Establish Scoring Criteria
activities.description: Decide on a scoring system (e.g., 1-5 or decimals) for Reach, Impact, Confidence, and Effort. Clearly define what each score represents for each criterion.

activities.name: Assign Scores
activities.description: Work through each project and assign a score for each RICE factor. Ideally, discuss and agree on these scores as a team.

activities.name: Calculate RICE Scores
activities.description: Multiply the assigned scores for Reach, Impact, Confidence, and Effort together for each project to determine a total RICE score.

activities.name: Compare and Prioritize
activities.description: Compare total RICE scores across all options. Projects with higher scores are prioritized higher.

completion_criteria: Every project or decision has been assigned scores for all four RICE factors, and a final ranked list has been generated based on the total scores.

metrics.name: Total RICE Score
metrics.formula: Reach * Impact * Confidence * Effort

benefits: Simplicity: Easy to understand and apply without extensive experience.
Transparency: Promotes clear communication and understanding of prioritization choices.
Flexibility: Adaptable to different situations by adjusting relative importance of criteria.

name: The Theory of Constraints
objective: Identify and manage the single bottleneck in a process to maximize total throughput.
description: The Theory of Constraints (ToC) is a methodology for identifying the most important limiting factor (i.e., constraint) that stands in the way of achieving a goal and then systematically improving that constraint until it is no longer the limiting factor. In manufacturing and software development, this often manifests as a single slowest component or bottleneck that limits the speed of the entire system.

activities.name: Identify the constraint
activities.description: Look at where Work In Progress (WIP) is piling up in your process or board.

activities.name: Exploit the constraint
activities.description: Make quick improvements to the throughput of this specific stage using existing resources (making the most of what you have).

activities.name: Subordinate the constraint
activities.description: Align the rest of the process stages to support the bottleneck; ensure other stages don't overproduce and cause more pressure.

activities.name: Elevate the constraint
activities.description: Invest in more capacity, people, or better tools to increase the size or scale of the constraint until it is solved.

activities.name: Repeat the process
activities.description: Once the bottleneck moves elsewhere, restart from step one to identify the new constraint.

completion_criteria: The constraint is elevated to the point that it no longer limits the throughput of the system compared to other stages.

metrics.name: Throughput
metrics.formula: Total completed items / time period

metrics.name: Work In Progress (WIP)
metrics.formula: Number of active items in the system

guidelines.name: THE GOAL: A PROCESS OF ONGOING IMPROVEMENT Dr. Eliyahu M. Goldratt
Theory of Constraints (TOC) of Dr. Eliyahu Goldratt

pitfalls: Improving components that are not the bottleneck (local optimization)
Adding pressure to a downstream bottleneck by speeding up upstream processes
Treating ToC as a one-time activity rather than a continuous cycle

name: Threat Modeling
objective: Identify potential threats, rate their severity, and discuss mitigations to build secure systems by design.
description: Formally, threat modeling is a process by which potential threats are identified and rated for severity and possible mitigations are discussed. It involves thinking about how a system could be broken and considering preventive actions. It is a human-centric reasoning process rather than just a tool, most effective when multiple stakeholders like developers, architects, and security specialists collaborate to look at the system from different angles.

activities.name: Scope Definition
activities.description: Determine whether to model the entire service or break it down into smaller features, components, or engineering teams for granular tracking.

activities.name: Architecture Understanding
activities.description: Review the system architecture down to the single-component level and analyze how data flows between atomic elements.

activities.name: Threat Identification
activities.description: Brainstorm potential threats and attack vectors unique to the service, identifying areas where data passes over virtual trust boundaries.

activities.name: Vulnerability Assessment
activities.description: Compare architecture design and configuration against industry standards to discover weaknesses.

activities.name: Mitigation Planning
activities.description: Propose and document realistic mitigations for identified weaknesses to provide a basis for secure implementation.

activities.name: Feedback Integration
activities.description: Provide the findings as a framework for subsequent penetration testing and security lifecycle analysis.

completion_criteria: All potential threats for the defined scope are documented, severity levels are assigned, and rational mitigation decisions have been agreed upon by stakeholders.

metrics.name: Early Detected Vulnerabilities
metrics.formula: Number of design flaws identified before coding starts

metrics.name: Security Cost Avoidance
metrics.formula: (Projected cost of post-release fix) - (Cost of early mitigation)

guidelines.name: The Security Cards, A Security Threat Brainstorming Toolkit
Elevation of Privilege (EoP) Threat Modeling Card Game

pitfalls: Treating threat modeling as a tool-only activity rather than a human process
Waiting until after release to perform the assessment
Ignoring natural inter-team communication difficulties
Attempting one massive model for a large system instead of component-level modeling
benefits: Finds design flaws before release when they are cheapest to fix
Improves user confidence in system security posture
Exposes exploitable weak points missed by code reviews or standard testing
Targets security testing and code review efforts effectively

name: Tribute Wall
objective: Share and spread feedback to the whole team.
description: A simple and powerful way to share and spread feedback to the whole team. Product Owners, UX-designers, BA's and other persons who have contact with stakeholders and users get a lot of first-hand feedback. This feedback should not stop there as it's really the team that have delivered the product, hence they should see all the great feedback coming in. It's a way to empower the team as they become aware of the impact their work has.

activities.name: Setup the Wall
activities.description: Add 'Tribute Wall' with big letters on an empty wall space or board.

activities.name: Collect Feedback
activities.description: Write down feedback quotes on sticky notes, collect them from emails or memory.

activities.name: Post to Wall
activities.description: All team members add feedback to the wall.

activities.name: Continuous Maintenance
activities.description: Continue to add quotes as they come in; ask users and stakeholders that visit the team to add their own.

completion_criteria: The wall is visible to the entire team and contains feedback directed to the product or team.

pitfalls: Including feedback directed to a specific person instead of the product/team
Forgetting to update the wall with new feedback regularly

name: Ubiquitous Language
objective: Unambiguously define the terms and concepts of a business domain to build a common language between all project participants.
description: Ubiquitous Language is a practice of building up a canonical, rigorous, and unambiguous language between everyone involved in the software development process. Originally introduced in Domain-Driven Design (DDD), it serves as a shared language between the development team, users, product owners, and stakeholders to ensure that business concepts are accurately reflected in the final software implementation.

activities.name: Collect Domain Concepts
activities.description: Identify domain concepts from other discovery practices such as Event Storming, Impact Mapping, or Empathy Mapping.

activities.name: Develop Definitions
activities.description: Draft clear and canonical definitions for identified concepts, utilizing diagrams or drawings when visual aids are more effective than text.

activities.name: Validate with Stakeholders
activities.description: Review the concepts and definitions with the development team, users, and the Product Owner to solicit feedback and ensure alignment.

activities.name: Iterate
activities.description: Continuously refine the language and definitions as understanding of the domain evolves through the development lifecycle.

completion_criteria: A central glossary exists that is accessible for reading and reviewing by everyone involved in the development process, with tracked updates.

metrics.name: Concept Clarity Rate
metrics.formula: Number of agreed-upon definitions / Total identified domain concepts

guidelines.name: Martin Fowler article on Ubiquitous Language
Domain-Driven Design: Tackling Complexity in the Heart of Software

pitfalls: Ambiguity in definitions leading to incorrect technical implementations
Developing the language in isolation without user or stakeholder input
Failure to maintain the glossary as the domain knowledge evolves
benefits: Better understanding of the business domain and users
Removal of ambiguity from the development process
Established common language between developers, stakeholders, and users

name: UI Design Workshop
objective: Quickly sketch rough ideas for the user interface to build shared understanding and jump-start development.
description: A UI workshop is an activity in which participants quickly sketch rough ideas for the user interface using whiteboards, sticky notes, or paper. It helps take high-level business processes and turn them into concrete designs that developers can begin working on, even without a dedicated UX designer.

activities.name: Describe what we're sketching
activities.description: Explain the user goal, sticky note size, mobile-first approach, and time limits.

activities.name: Individual Sketching
activities.description: Every participant sketches their ideas for 10 minutes.

activities.name: Playback
activities.description: Each person shares their sketches for 2 minutes and places them on the board.

activities.name: Gather Feedback
activities.description: Spend 2-3 minutes per person's flow using colored stickies to mark likes and dislikes.

activities.name: Second Sketching Round
activities.description: Participants refine their designs in another 5-7 minute session.

activities.name: Mark Screens
activities.description: Label each screen with a letter and number for identification during voting.

activities.name: Secondary Playback
activities.description: Each person shares for 2 minutes while others note their favorite letter/number combinations.

activities.name: Merge Designs
activities.description: Merge the designs based on the voting results.

completion_criteria: Participants have voted on design elements and a shared understanding of the UI flow is established.

metrics.name: Workshop Duration
metrics.formula: End Time - Start Time

metrics.name: Idea Volume
metrics.formula: Count of unique UI screens created

guidelines.name: Wireflows: A UX Deliverable for Workflows and Apps
3 rapid prototyping exercises to improve your UX skills
The Tuesday portion of the book

pitfalls: Spending too much time on fidelity
Lack of diverse team participation
Focusing on aesthetics rather than user flow
benefits: Builds UX confidence in team members

name: Usability Testing
objective: Observe real users interacting with a product to identify confusion, validate needs, and refine features.
description: In a usability testing session, the team observes real users interacting with the product. Typically, a facilitator sits with a user and asks her to complete tasks and explain her thinking as she goes. The team sits in a separate room and observes the testing by video. It focuses on what the user thinks and does in the real world rather than a focus group. It can be run on an existing product, a prototype, or even a competitor's product to test early and often.

activities.name: Prepare the Test Object
activities.description: Select an existing product, a prototype, or a competitor's product for the user to interact with.

activities.name: Facilitate Interaction
activities.description: A facilitator asks a real user to complete tasks and explain their thinking aloud as they navigate.

activities.name: Observe and Record
activities.description: The team observes the session via video from a separate room to build empathy and understanding.

activities.name: Analyze and Adapt
activities.description: The team reviews findings to determine if they are on the right track and identify which features to build next.

completion_criteria: The testing session is completed, observed by the team, and user feedback is documented to inform product direction.

metrics.name: Task Success Rate
metrics.formula: Successful tasks / Total tasks attempted

metrics.name: Session Duration
metrics.formula: 30 minutes

guidelines.name: The art of guerrilla usability testing

pitfalls: Mistaking usability testing for a focus group
Testing too late in the development cycle
Ignoring that user-stated needs may differ from actual needs
benefits: Builds empathy and understanding of real user behavior
Prevents wasting time on confusing or unnecessary features
Validates whether the product is solving real user needs

name: Unicorn, Skunk, Rat (USR)
objective: Ensure your meeting/workshop stays on track and attendees contribute positively.
description: USR (Unicorn, Skunk, Rat) is a gamified technique used during meetings or workshops to encourage positive contributions and discourage disruptive behavior. Participants' names are tracked on a board, and they receive points (positive or negative) based on their interaction style and the quality of their ideas.

activities.name: Prepare Scoreboard
activities.description: Write the names of all meeting participants on a board with three headings: Unicorn, Skunk, and Rat.

activities.name: Award Unicorns
activities.description: Award a Unicorn (+1 point) to any participant who suggests a good idea. Any participant can award a Unicorn to another.

activities.name: Award Skunks
activities.description: Award a Skunk (-3 points) to any participant being disruptive, difficult, or derailing discussions.

activities.name: Award Rats
activities.description: Award a Rat (-1 point) to any participant who leads the conversation down an unproductive 'rat hole'.

activities.name: Tally and Reward
activities.description: At the end of the meeting, calculate total points for each person and award a prize to the individual with the highest score.

completion_criteria: The meeting or workshop has concluded and final points have been calculated.

metrics.name: Individual USR Score
metrics.formula: (Unicorns * 1) + (Skunks * -3) + (Rats * -1)

pitfalls: Over-gamification distracting from the actual meeting agenda
Potential for the scoring to be used punitively rather than constructively
benefits: Reduces time wasted on unproductive tangents
Provides immediate feedback on meeting behavior

name: V2MOMs (Vision, Values, Methods, Obstacles, & Measures)
objective: Enable communication of strategy, especially when you need strong top-down alignment.
description: V2MOMs stand for Vision, Values, Methods, Obstacles, and Measures. Similar to the MSPOT framework, it enables strategy communication and alignment across an organization by defining a big-picture goal and how it will be achieved and measured.

activities.name: Establish Company V2MOM
activities.description: The leadership team creates a high-level V2MOM for the entire company.

activities.name: Develop Functional V2MOMs
activities.description: Functional leaders create specific V2MOMs for their teams that align with the company-wide version.

activities.name: Define Vision
activities.description: Establish the big-picture goal for the particular period or goal.

activities.name: Identify Values
activities.description: Determine the things that are important to the team and will guide behaviors.

activities.name: Prioritize Methods
activities.description: List the actions needed to achieve the vision in priority order.

activities.name: List Obstacles
activities.description: Identify the challenges and hurdles that need to be overcome to achieve the goal.

activities.name: Establish Measures
activities.description: Define the measurable results that indicate progress toward the goal.

completion_criteria: V2MOM documents are completed for both leadership and functional levels and shared across the organization for alignment.

guidelines.name: What does the V2MOMs process look like?

name: Virtual Obeya Design Studio
objective: Create a centralized digital hub to aggregate and radiate critical information across teams for real-time decision-making.
description: A digital adaptation of the physical 'Large Room' (Obeya) concept from the Toyota Production System. It serves as a central radiator of organizational information, supporting strategy, data, work, and culture perspectives. Designed for remote and hybrid environments, it visualizes real-time performance metrics and strategic alignment to promote autonomy and transparency.

activities.name: Pre-Session Communication
activities.description: Share background materials asynchronously to explain the Obeya concept and set expectations for participants.

activities.name: Digital Board Setup
activities.description: Prepare digital boards for the four MADO sections: Strategy, Data, Work, and Culture, including frameworks and access instructions.

activities.name: Synchronous Information Brainstorming
activities.description: Hold a timeboxed session where participants identify required metrics such as OKRs, risk dashboards, and social connection points.

activities.name: Prioritization and Voting
activities.description: Use democratic voting to select the top 5 information needs for each MADO section.

activities.name: Ownership and Tooling Validation
activities.description: Confirm data update frequency, assign individual ownership for sections, and identify any existing tooling gaps.

activities.name: Risk Mitigation
activities.description: Address concerns regarding data confidentiality, integrity, and availability within the digital space.

activities.name: Group Memory Translation
activities.description: Translate the workshop outputs into the formal Virtual Obeya tool and document constraints.

activities.name: Review Cadence Establishment
activities.description: Schedule regular review sessions to revisit the Obeya and adapt it to organizational changes.

completion_criteria: The Virtual Obeya digital board is populated with prioritized metrics, data ownership is assigned, and a recurring review schedule is established.

metrics.name: Decision Cycle Time
metrics.formula: Date of Decision - Date of Data Update

metrics.name: Information Radiator Engagement
metrics.formula: Count of unique team members accessing the Obeya board weekly

pitfalls: Psychological intimidation/fear of metrics
Tooling gaps and missing automated data sources
Poor balance between synchronous and asynchronous contributions
Decentralized or absent visualization of strategy
benefits: Supports rapid onboarding of new members

name: Virtual Office
objective: Identify pre-existing dynamics and build team spirit when forming a new team.
description: Building a Virtual Office when working remotely is a blend of introduction round, ice breaker and team builder while providing valuable insights into pre-existing dynamics, individual sentiments and functional expertise. The Virtual Office acts as a strong navigator for future collaboration between groups of people and individuals in your newly formed team.

activities.name: Prepare the Virtual Office Image
activities.description: Find a picture which represents your Virtual Office and ties back to a story relevant to the project name, business domain, or team location.

activities.name: Add Office Areas
activities.description: Add creative areas where people can place themselves based on roles or opposites (e.g., calm-busy, technical-non technical) rather than functional departments.

activities.name: Onboard the Team
activities.description: Bring the team to the virtual office space at the start of a gathering to combine introduction, ice breaking, and expectation gathering.

activities.name: Individual Introductions
activities.description: Team members create a sticky with their name, introduce their functional area and purpose, and place themselves in the area that best represents them, explaining why.

activities.name: Maintain and Revisit
activities.description: Return to the space when new members join, for project milestones, or when the team feels the office needs redecoration or a check-in on changed dynamics.

completion_criteria: All team members have placed themselves on the board, introduced themselves, and shared their reasoning for their chosen location.

pitfalls: Using functional areas like Security, Marketing, or Storage which hinders value-based thinking
Participants grumpily denying the exercise
Failing to revisit the board when team dynamics change
benefits: Kicks off with an emotionally positive experience
Provides insights into existing dynamics and sentiments

name: Wardley Mapping
objective: Strategic decisioning through visualization of a value chain's evolution.
description: Wardley Mapping is a framework for strategic orientation in business to gain topographical intelligence by visualizing the geography of the business landscape and value chain evolution. Inspired by military strategy, it uses mapping as a paradigm for understanding the strategic landscape.

activities.name: Identify Users
activities.description: Identify the users being served by the business.

activities.name: Identify Needs
activities.description: Identify the user's needs to be satisfied as top-level product offerings or capabilities.

activities.name: Identify Capabilities
activities.description: Identify the specific capabilities needed to meet the identified user needs.

activities.name: Determine Evolution Stage
activities.description: For each capability, evaluate its characteristics to determine its stage of evolution (Stage I, II, III, or IV).

activities.name: Draw Map
activities.description: Draw the complete value chain with dependency relationships, placing the User at the top and Capabilities across the evolution stages.

completion_criteria: A complete value chain map is produced where Users, Needs, and Capabilities are accurately placed within the four stages of Evolution with dependency relationships included.

metrics.name: Strategic Insight Identification
metrics.formula: Number of new insights or unknown risks identified during mapping

pitfalls: Difficulty in deciding the evolution stage for specific capabilities
Failing to break down large capabilities into smaller ones
benefits: Supporting common Domain Driven Design (DDD) practices
Clear insights into delivering desired value

name: Westrums Cultural Typology Assessment
objective: Predict and improve organizational safety and performance by measuring information flow and cooperation.
description: Based on Ron Westrum's 2004 research, this assessment categorizes cultures into Pathological (power-oriented), Bureaucratic (rule-oriented), or Generative (performance-oriented). It focuses on how information is handled, how failures are treated, and the level of cooperation between groups.

activities.name: Conduct Survey
activities.description: Distribute a 6-question survey to team members covering information seeking, failure treatment, shared responsibilities, collaboration, enquiry, and new ideas.

activities.name: Score Agreement
activities.description: Use a 5-point Likert scale (1: Strongly Disagree to 5: Strongly Agree) to measure responses to the survey questions.

activities.name: Calculate Average
activities.description: Sum and average the scores for a summative score: 0-1 (Pathological), 2-3 (Bureaucratic), or 4-5 (Generative).

activities.name: Analyze and Improve
activities.description: Identify low-scoring individual lines and investigate specific practices to improve those areas, such as cross-functional collaboration or communication channels.

completion_criteria: Averaged score calculated and specific areas for improvement identified for the team.

metrics.name: Summative Westrum Score
metrics.formula: Average(Sum of individual question scores)

guidelines.name: Lean Enterprise: How High Performance Organizations Innovate at Scale
ACCELERATE The Science of Lean Software and DevOps

pitfalls: Measuring too frequently (more than once per quarter)
Non-anonymous surveys leading to dishonest answers
Setting score improvement as a performance target (Goodhart's Law)
benefits: Predictive relationship with software delivery performance

name: What If...
objective: Lightly challenge rules and norms to spur innovation
description: The 'What If...' practice is a team activity that seeks to identify the current 'rules' or 'norms' surrounding a particular challenge, then eliminate one of those rules or norms to broaden the spectrum of innovative ideas. This practice was created by Duncan Wardle, former Head of Innovation and Creativity at Disney, to overcome subconscious mental barriers known as the 'river of thinking'.

activities.name: List the rules
activities.description: Choose a challenge topic, set up a board with three columns (Rules, What If, Imagine If), and have participants list all rules, norms, and barriers for 5 minutes.

activities.name: Pick a Rule to Break
activities.description: Vote on the rule that would most broaden ideation if eliminated. Move it to the 'What If...' column and reword it as a provocative statement.

activities.name: Imagine If...
activities.description: Spend 10 minutes writing ideas for future solutions assuming the selected rule doesn't apply. Present ideas and vote on which to experiment with.

activities.name: Create Next-steps
activities.description: List action items for the top-voted idea, assign ownership, and plan a follow-up in 1-2 weeks to check progress.

completion_criteria: The top-voted idea has assigned action items and ownership for experimentation.

guidelines.name: Breaking the Rules, an Innovation Tool
What If? The Secret Tool That Created Disneyland, Netflix and Uber

pitfalls: Subconscious mental barriers (river of thinking) limiting scope
Large groups running short on time during ideation
benefits: Broadens the spectrum of innovative ideas
Challenges established mental barriers and industry norms
Identifies creative solutions to complex business challenges

name: Wheel-of-Misfortune
objective: Build confidence in on-call engineers via simulated outage scenarios.
description: An informal exercise or game that aims to build confidence for on-call engineers through simulated outage scenarios. It helps participants practice problem debugging and solving under stress, ensures understanding of incident response protocols, promotes effective communication, emphasizes a blameless culture, and facilitates the sharing of experience from past incidents.

activities.name: Assign Roles
activities.description: Choose a Game Master to coordinate the session and a Volunteer to act as the primary on-call engineer.

activities.name: Select Scenario
activities.description: The Game Master selects a past or fictional incident scenario, balancing difficulty with the volunteer's experience level.

activities.name: Simulate Troubleshooting
activities.description: The volunteer spins the wheel to choose a scenario and explains the theoretical actions, lookup queries, and dashboard checks they would take to solve the incident.

activities.name: Manage Simulated Time
activities.description: The volunteer tracks time during the exercise to simulate real-world incident pressures like SLA or SLO breaches.

activities.name: Team Engagement
activities.description: The Game Master and volunteer involve the rest of the group to ask questions, share expertise, and explore alternative debugging methods.

activities.name: Conduct Debrief
activities.description: At the end of the session, the team holds a debrief to discuss learnings and potential improvements to response protocols.

completion_criteria: The simulated incident is resolved or concluded and a debrief of the learnings has been performed.

metrics.name: Simulated Resolution Time
metrics.formula: End of session - Start of session

pitfalls: Scenario difficulty not aligned with volunteer experience level
Lack of engagement from the rest of the team
Ignoring time constraints during the simulation
Failure to maintain a blameless culture during the exercise

name: Where to Start
objective: Solve the problem of choosing properly which product should be treated in a digital transformation journey
description: This practice focus on helping people to unveil the most complex systems and processes that are urging to be affected by transformational work in an organization, with the purpose of strengthen and diffuse accross the organization the knowledge of Design Thinking, Agile and DevOps culture. It is a tool that facilitates conversations about relative priorities, clarifies system importance across teams, increases team autonomy, and breaks integration silos.

activities.name: Define Initial Focus
activities.description: Start with relative priorities and concentrate the team's focus on defining which system should be approached first in a transformational journey.

activities.name: Visualize Operations
activities.description: Using sticky notes, participants work together to visualize how the operation is performed amongst users, systems, and the processes involved.

activities.name: Analyze Value, Pain, and Desires
activities.description: For each system, list the value obtained, the pain points, and the desires in improving each system or process as identified in the process design.

activities.name: Timeline and Prioritization
activities.description: Distribute these improvements in an estimated timeline and prioritize the most important ones for the digital transformation.

completion_criteria: A prioritized timeline of improvements is created and the team reaches consensus on which system or process to address first in the transformation journey.

guidelines.name: Where to Start - Open Practice Library

pitfalls: Skipping the visualization of user interactions with systems
Failing to involve all key stakeholders (Tech, PO, Business) in the prioritization process
benefits: Brings a sense of belonging to the digital transformation journey

name: Whole Product Plotting
objective: A visual approach for scoping the most important features of a product/service.
description: Whole Product Plotting is a drawing of multiple concentric circles, each of which represents an additional layer of details for the product/service. At the core are the generic product/service characteristics, followed by a minimal conditions circle (must-haves), an augmented product circle, and a potential circle (differentiations and customer retention features).

activities.name: Identify Generic Characteristics
activities.description: Define the core product/service characteristics at the center of the plot.

activities.name: Define Minimal Conditions
activities.description: Identify the 'must-haves' or minimal conditions required for the product.

activities.name: Determine Augmented Product Layers
activities.description: Map out additional features that go beyond basic expectations.

activities.name: Identify Potential Features
activities.description: Explore differentiators and customer retention features for the outermost circle.

activities.name: Translate to Actionable Backlog
activities.description: Use the visual map to align the team and prioritize the Product Backlog.

completion_criteria: The product features are visually mapped across all concentric circles and translated into a prioritized set of features.

metrics.name: Prioritization Alignment
metrics.formula: Aligned Team Members / Total Team Members

pitfalls: Over-complicating the core circle
Ignoring potential differentiation in favor of just must-haves
Lack of stakeholder involvement in the plotting phase
benefits: Opens up the product development for the whole team
Provides transparency in prioritisation and strategy alignment
Helps move the focus on the important / differentiating features
Allows for uncovering options for pivoting existing products

name: Why do we exist
objective: Achieve a shared understanding of the organizational purpose
description: This exercise encourages participants to explain their organization to an extraterrestrial being, promoting creative thinking beyond the ordinary. It facilitates reflection on the organization's purpose, values, customers, potential, and challenges to foster a unified narrative and deeper understanding within the team.

activities.name: The Question
activities.description: Display the question 'How would you communicate with someone from another planet to tell them about your organization?' on a flip chart or slide.

activities.name: Individual Reflection
activities.description: Participants take 3 minutes to individually reflect on the question and capture their thoughts on separate sticky notes.

activities.name: Creative Teamwork
activities.description: In sub-groups or pairs, participants create a story or message using visual props like pictures, drawings, Dixit cards, or emojis to answer the question, aligning individual thoughts into a cohesive story.

activities.name: Presentation
activities.description: Sub-groups or pairs present their creative creations to the entire group to foster shared understanding.

completion_criteria: All sub-groups have presented their stories and the group has reached a shared understanding of organizational purpose within the timebox.

metrics.name: Participation Engagement
metrics.formula: (Number of active participants / Total number of invitees) * 100

metrics.name: Story Completion
metrics.formula: 1 if all groups presented a cohesive story, 0 otherwise

guidelines.name: How do you explain your organization's purpose? 3 lessons from Red Hat

pitfalls: Getting lost in technical details instead of high-level purpose
Over-reflecting individually without enough time for team collaboration
Ignoring the alien persona and reverting to standard corporate speak

name: World Coffee
objective: To leverage our collective intelligence to generate fresh perspectives and actionable ideas on the central theme.
description: The World Café is a structured conversational process that simulates the relaxed, creative, and engaging atmosphere of a café. Participants are seated in small groups at tables to discuss a specific, powerful question. After set rounds of conversation, participants move to different tables, mixing with new people. This movement acts as a cross-pollination mechanism, allowing ideas and insights to travel and connect throughout the entire group, leading to a deeper, collective discovery.

activities.name: Create a Hospitable Space
activities.description: Arrange the room with several small tables for 4-5 people each. Cover tables with paper tablecloths and provide colorful markers for doodling and writing ideas.

activities.name: Define Powerful Questions
activities.description: Develop open-ended, relevant, and thought-provoking questions that the group will explore during the rounds.

activities.name: Welcome and Introduction
activities.description: The facilitator welcomes participants, explains the purpose, process, rotations, and Café Etiquette such as listening to understand and connecting diverse perspectives.

activities.name: Round 1 Discussion
activities.description: Participants discuss the first powerful question for 20-30 minutes, writing and drawing ideas on the paper tablecloths.

activities.name: The Rotation
activities.description: At the end of the round, one person stays as the Table Host while others travel to different tables to cross-pollinate ideas.

activities.name: Round 2 & 3
activities.description: Table Hosts welcome new arrivals and share previous insights while travelers share ideas from their former tables. The group continues the conversation for 2-3 more rounds.

activities.name: Collective Harvest
activities.description: The entire group comes together to share patterns, themes, and key insights, which are captured on a flip chart or whiteboard to create a visual map.

work_products: Paper tablecloths with doodles and ideas
Flip chart of patterns and themes
completion_criteria: Collective harvest is complete, with patterns and key insights documented and shared with all participants.

metrics.name: Participant Count
metrics.formula: Count of unique participants attending (min 12)

metrics.name: Actionable Ideas Generated
metrics.formula: Total number of unique actionable items harvested

metrics.name: Session Duration
metrics.formula: Total time from intro to harvest

pitfalls: Poorly phrased or closed-ended questions
Lack of hospitable atmosphere
Dominating voices at individual tables
Insufficient time for meaningful rotation and harvesting
benefits: Surfaces collective wisdom already present in the group
Encourages diverse perspectives and innovative solutions

name: Yes! And...
objective: Improvise to create more good ideas, and build strong team work skills.
description: A fun team practice for generating lots of great ideas that emerged from the Theatresports community. It creates an environment where everyone feels safe and confident contributing ideas without fear of rejection by encouraging people to accept ideas as they are and build upon them as stepping stones.

activities.name: Team Formation
activities.description: Break the group into small teams of 4–6 people and assign the challenge: plan a party to celebrate last quarter’s achievements.

activities.name: Round 1: Rejection
activities.description: Everyone shares an idea one-at-a-time. Every time one person shares an idea, someone else replies: 'No, what we need to do is…' and explains an alternative idea.

activities.name: Round 2: Qualification
activities.description: Follow the same approach as Round 1, but the response must be 'Yes, but…' where the person presents an objection before sharing their idea.

activities.name: Round 3: Acceptance and Expansion
activities.description: Apply the 'Yes, and…' approach. People build on others' ideas by replying 'Yes, and we can also…'.

completion_criteria: Completion of all three rounds where the final round results in collaborative expansion of ideas.

guidelines.name: Yes, And… Improv Techniques To Make You A Better Boss
4 Rules of Improv and How They Relate to Customer Support
Improvisation is ‘yoga for your social skills’ and can make you better leaders

pitfalls: Critiquing ideas immediately
Rejecting or refining ideas too early
Fear of rejection hindering contribution
benefits: Creates a safe environment for contribution
